Result: Children with ASD showed attenuated neural responses in early prediction (P200) and reanalysis (P600) but heightened semantic integration demands (N400) under mismatch conditions. They also had greater variability in hemispheric lateralization unrelated to vocabulary performance.

Conclusion: The study highlights cascading processing differences during recursive comprehension in ASD, linking diminished early engagement to increased semantic and reduced syntactic processing efficiency. Temporal dynamics and neural variability are critical for understanding language differences in ASD.

Abstract: Recursion enables the generation of hierarchical linguistic structures but imposes substantial processing demands during real-time comprehension. While difficulties with complex syntax have been reported in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), the temporal dynamics of recursive processing remain poorly understood. This study used event-related potentials (ERPs) to examine how Mandarin-speaking children with ASD process two-level recursive locative constructions. Twenty-four children (12 ASD, 12 typically developing, TD) participated in a cross-modal sentence-picture matching task. Neural responses were analyzed across three processing stages associated with structural prediction (P200), semantic integration (N400), and syntactic reanalysis (P600), with mental age controlled. Results revealed a systematic divergence between groups. TD children showed clear P200 and P600 modulation in response to structural mismatch, whereas ASD children exhibited attenuated early differentiation and reduced late reanalysis effects. In contrast, ASD children showed enhanced N400 responses under mismatch conditions, indicating increased semantic integration demands. In addition, the ASD group displayed significantly greater inter-individual variability in hemispheric lateralization, although lateralization strength was not associated with receptive vocabulary performance. These findings support a cascading account in which reduced early predictive engagement in ASD leads to increased integration costs and diminished reanalysis efficiency during recursive processing. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of both temporal processing dynamics and neural variability in understanding language differences in ASD.

</details>


### [147] [AdaPlanBench: Evaluating Adaptive Planning in Large Language Model Agents under World and User Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05622)
*Jiayu Liu,Cheng Qian,Zhenhailong Wang,Bingxuan Li,Jiateng Liu,Heng Wang,Jeonghwan Kim,Yumeng Wang,Xiusi Chen,Yi R. Fung,Heng Ji*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AdaPlanBench introduces a benchmark to assess LLMs' ability to adaptively plan under dynamic world and user constraints revealed progressively during interaction. The study highlights challenges in achieving high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks inadequately address adaptive planning with progressively revealed dual constraints in real-world scenarios.

Method: Developed AdaPlanBench, a benchmark with 307 household tasks, dual constraints, and a multi-turn interactive protocol where hidden constraints are disclosed as agents propose invalid plans.

Result: Experiments on ten leading LLMs showed adaptive planning challenges, with the highest model achieving 67.75% accuracy. Performance worsens with additional constraints, especially user constraints.

Conclusion: AdaPlanBench serves as a tool for investigating adaptive planning, revealing shortcomings in current LLMs and emphasizing difficulties in handling dynamically revealed constraints.

Abstract: Planning for real-world problems by language models often involves both world and user constraints, which may not be fully specified upfront and are progressively disclosed through interaction. However, existing benchmarks still underexplore adaptive planning under such progressively revealed dual constraints. To address this gap, we introduce AdaPlanBench, a dynamic interactive benchmark for evaluating whether Large Language Model (LLM) agents can adaptively plan and re-plan under progressively revealed world and user constraints. AdaPlanBench is built on 307 household tasks, with a scalable constraint construction pipeline that augments each task with dual constraints. At runtime, agents interact with the environment in a multi-turn protocol where hidden constraints are revealed only when the agent proposes a plan that violates them, requiring iterative plan revision under accumulating feedback. This makes planning challenging, as agents must infer and track constraints from feedback while re-planning effectively. Experiments on ten leading LLMs show that adaptive planning under dual constraints remains challenging, with the best model reaching only 67.75% accuracy. We further observe that performance degrades as more constraints accumulate, with user constraints posing a particularly large challenge and failures often stemming from weaker physical grounding and reduced effectiveness. These results establish AdaPlanBench as a testbed for dual-constrained interactive planning and highlight the challenge of reliable adaptation to dynamically revealed constraints in LLM agents.

</details>


### [148] [When New Generators Arrive: Lifelong Machine-Generated Text Attribution via Ridge Feature Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05626)
*Zhen Sun,Yifan Liao,Zhicong Huang,Jiaheng Wei,Cheng Hong,Yutao Yue,Xinlei He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes RidgeFT, a framework for lifelong machine-generated text (MGT) attribution that ensures adaptability to new generators while retaining recognition for old ones, without using exemplar replay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in lifelong MGT attribution, where models must continuously adapt to new text generators while retaining recognition of previously encountered ones, a task where existing methods often struggle to balance these requirements.

Method: RidgeFT operates by training a task-aware encoder, storing class-wise sufficient statistics, and freezing the encoder for closed-form updates. It uses covariance calibration, fixed random features for improved representation, and ridge regression for new class updates.

Result: RidgeFT consistently outperforms existing baselines in macro-F1 scores, effectively balancing old-class retention and new-class adaptation across different domains and protocols.

Conclusion: Feature-stable, lightweight analytic updates like RidgeFT provide an effective and efficient solution to the challenges of lifelong MGT attribution.

Abstract: Machine-generated text (MGT) attribution aims to identify the specific generator responsible for a given text, thereby providing fine-grained evidence for model accountability and misuse investigation. As new large language models continue to emerge, attribution models must continuously incorporate new generators while preserving their ability to recognize previously seen ones. Prior works have shown that this lifelong MGT attribution setting is challenging, and existing methods often struggle to achieve a stable balance between adapting to new classes and retaining old ones. To address this issue, we propose RidgeFT, a lightweight analytic update framework that does not rely on exemplar replay. RidgeFT trains a task-aware encoder on the initial generator set, stores compact class-wise sufficient statistics when each generator class is first observed, and then freezes the encoder for replay-free closed-form updates. It then suppresses generator-irrelevant variation through covariance calibration, improves representation capacity with fixed random features, and updates new classes through closed-form ridge regression based on class-level sufficient statistics. Across multi-topic evaluations with varying initial generator setups, RidgeFT consistently outperforms baselines. It achieves the best macro-F1 across domains, backbones, and incremental protocols, while also improving both old-class retention and new-class adaptation. These results suggest that feature-stable analytic updates provide a simple yet effective approach to lifelong MGT attribution.

</details>


### [149] [Bootstrapping Semantic Layer from Execution for Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05634)
*Youngwon Lee,Jaejin Kim,Seung-won Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GATE is introduced to improve text-to-SQL by bootstrapping missing groundings using execution feedback and reusable memory, achieving better results than previous methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-SQL systems often lack sufficient grounding when dealing with real-world databases. Conventional methods requiring predefined semantic layers are incomplete, especially in under-documented expert domains. Addressing these shortcomings motivates this research.

Method: The method, GATE, keeps grounding hypotheses open and uses execution feedback to decide the correct grounding. Supported groundings are stored as reusable execution-grounded memory, improving text-to-SQL conversion iteratively.

Result: GATE achieves consistent improvements over existing baseline methods in both real-world and controlled text-to-SQL benchmarks.

Conclusion: Execution feedback not only validates queries but can bootstrap reusable memory groundings, enhancing text-to-SQL performance in under-specified and expert-domain scenarios.

Abstract: Real-world text-to-SQL is often under-specified until user phrases are grounded in how the database stores values. Prior work attempts to address this by requiring a semantic layer to specify groundings in advance, but such specifications are often incomplete, especially in expert domains where domain-specific conventions are under-documented. As this leaves multiple grounding hypotheses open for the same SQL part, we introduce GATE (Grouding After Test from Execution), which bootstraps missing groundings from execution feedback. GATE keeps grounding hypotheses open while executing the already grounded parts to obtain observations. Then, only the hypothesis supported by that observation is grounded and stored as a memory entry, recording what was tested and how the open part should be written in SQL. These entries accumulate into execution-grounded memory, allowing later steps to reuse supported groundings. Across real-world and controlled benchmarks, GATE consistently improves over strong baselines, demonstrating that execution can serve not only as validation but also as a bootstrapping mechanism for reusable memory in text-to-SQL.

</details>


### [150] [QueryAgent-R1: Bridging Query Generation and Product Retrieval for E-Commerce Query Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05671)
*Dike Sun,Zheng Zou,Jingtong Zang,Qi Sun,Huaipeng Zhaoand Tao Luo,Xiaoyi Zeng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces QueryAgent-R1, a memory-augmented framework focusing on improving e-commerce search by bridging the gap between click-through rates (CTR) and product conversion rates (CVR) through chain-of-retrieval optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods in e-commerce search optimization focus on query-level relevance but fail to ensure that retrieved products match user preferences, leading to high CTR but low CVR.

Method: QueryAgent-R1 utilizes a memory-augmented agentic framework that integrates real inventory retrieval, a consistency reward in RL for query optimization, and a memory abstraction module for user profiling.

Result: QueryAgent-R1 demonstrates superior performance on proprietary and public datasets and improves Query CTR by 2.9% and CVR by 3.1% in large-scale online A/B testing.

Conclusion: QueryAgent-R1 effectively aligns query recommendations with user preferences, boosting both query click and conversion rates.

Abstract: Query recommendation in e-commerce search aims to proactively suggest queries that match users' potential interests. However, existing methods mainly optimize query-level relevance, while neglecting whether the retrieved products align with users' downstream preferences. This mismatch often leads to high query click through rates (CTR) but low product conversion rates (CVR). To bridge this gap, we propose QueryAgent-R1, a memory-augmented agentic framework that improves end-to-end alignment via chain-of-retrieval optimization. Our QueryAgent-R1 grounds query generation in real inventory retrieval, allowing the agent to validate and refine queries based on retrieved products. We also design a consistency reward in the agentic reinforcement learning (RL) process to jointly optimize query relevance and downstream engagement. In addition, we construct a memory abstraction module for efficient user profiling. To support offline evaluation, we construct two datasets based on both proprietary industrial data and public datasets, on which QueryAgent-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines. Moreover, on a large scale production platform, QueryAgent-R1 improves Query CTR by 2.9% and guided CVR by 3.1% in online A/B tests.

</details>


### [151] [Value-and-Structure Alignment for Routing-Consistent Quantization of Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05688)
*Hancheol Park,Geonho Lee,Tairen Piao,Tae-Ho Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method, VSRAQ, to address the challenge of quantizing Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models while preserving their routing consistency to maintain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models, despite their efficiency, are sensitive to routing instability caused by quantization, which can degrade performance by altering the expert-selection process. This necessitates a quantization method compatible with the unique properties of MoE models.

Method: VSRAQ is a post-training quantization objective that ensures expert-selection behavior is preserved by combining value alignment (to match routing-relevant scores) and structure alignment (to retain expert order and decision boundaries).

Result: The experiments demonstrate that VSRAQ improves expert-selection consistency, effectively reducing quantization-induced performance degradation and outperforming baseline methods.

Conclusion: VSRAQ offers an effective way to quantize MoE models without inference-time overhead, ensuring routing consistency and better performance compared to existing quantization techniques.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale foundation models efficiently by activating only a subset of experts for each token, but their large number of expert parameters still makes quantization essential for practical deployment. Unlike dense models, however, MoE models are sensitive to routing instability: small quantization-induced perturbations can change the top-$k$ expert selection, altering the computation path and degrading model quality. We propose Value-and-Structure Routing Alignment for Quantization (VSRAQ), a MoE-specific post-training quantization objective that preserves pre-quantization expert-selection behavior under quantization. VSRAQ combines two complementary objectives that jointly preserve expert-selection behavior: value alignment, which matches routing-relevant logits or scores, and structure alignment, which preserves expert ordering and top-$k$ decision boundaries. By maintaining routing consistency, VSRAQ reduces quantization-induced degradation without introducing any inference-time overhead and can be integrated into existing quantization frameworks. Experiments on recent MoE foundation models show that VSRAQ improves expert-selection consistency and consistently outperforms reconstruction-only and router-aware baselines.

</details>


### [152] [Rethinking LoRA Memory Through the Lens of KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05698)
*Chunsheng Zuo,Liaoyaqi Wang,William Jurayj,William Fleshman,Benjamin Van Durme*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how lightweight, document-specific modules (LoRA adapters) interact with context memory in document-level QA. LoRA becomes critical when context is limited or removed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and optimize the interaction between parameter-side memory (e.g., LoRA adapters) and context-side memory (KV cache) in retrieving document information for question answering.

Method: The study gradually removes document key-value states (KV cache) and measures the impact of LoRA adapters in recovering lost document information for QA tasks. It also examines the effects of different supervision styles and encoding strategies.

Result: Document LoRA has limited impact when the KV cache is largely intact but significantly improves (by 13-21 ROUGE-L points) performance when no document context is present. Its effectiveness is greatest when used during answer generation rather than document encoding, and QA-specific supervision strengthens adapters most effectively.

Conclusion: Document LoRA serves as a decoding-time parametric memory, especially useful when context-side evidence is scarce, making it a complementary and situationally critical memory channel.

Abstract: Parametric retrieval augmentation encodes document information into lightweight, document-specific modules such as LoRA adapters, reducing the need to include all evidence as input context. However, it remains unclear how this parameter-side memory interacts with context-side memory stored in the KV cache. We study this interaction in document-level question answering by progressively evicting document key-value states and measuring when a document LoRA contributes beyond the retained context. We find that document LoRA adds little when the KV cache is largely intact, but becomes increasingly useful under aggressive compression, recovering 13-21 ROUGE-L points when no document context remains. The gain is largest when the base model encodes the document, and the adapter is applied only during answer generation, suggesting that document LoRA is better understood as decoding-time parametric memory than as a document encoder. Finally, QA-style supervision produces substantially stronger adapters than raw-context next-token-prediction. These results position document LoRA as a complementary memory channel whose value emerges precisely when context-side evidence is scarce.

</details>


### [153] [Beyond tokens: a unified framework for latent communication in LLM-based multi-agent systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05711)
*Yingzhuo Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper reviews latent communication in multi-agent systems with large language models, proposing a 3-axis framework analyzing communication elements and summarizing design patterns and challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address inefficiencies in natural language communication protocols in multi-agent systems and explore latent communication for improved performance.

Method: The authors introduce a 3-axis framework to classify and analyze latent communication techniques and study 18 representative methods.

Result: The paper identifies five design patterns and outlines key challenges including cross-architecture alignment and secure latent channels.

Conclusion: The proposed framework helps structure the research landscape, promotes accessibility for new researchers, and offers terminology for future studies.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems built on large language models (LLMs) have become a prevailing paradigm for tackling complex reasoning, planning, and tool-use tasks. The dominant communication protocol in such systems is natural language: agents exchange messages token-by-token, verbalising their internal reasoning so that peers can read, verify, and respond. While convenient and interpretable, this protocol suffers from three structural drawbacks -- high inference cost, irreversible information loss during discretization, and ambiguity/redundancy of natural language. A growing body of work therefore explores an alternative protocol -- latent communication -- in which agents exchange continuous representations (embeddings, hidden states, or KV-caches) directly, bypassing the bottleneck of text generation. This paper presents a unified framework for organising the rapidly expanding literature on latent communication. We analyse existing methods along three orthogonal axes: (1) WHAT information is communicated (Embeddings, Hidden States, KV-Caches, or other continuous state); (2) WHICH sender-receiver alignment is used (latent-space alignment and layer alignment); and (3) HOW the communicated information is fused into the receiver (concatenation, prepending, mathematical operations, cross-attention, or cache restoration). Under this 3-axis framework, we systematically categorise eighteen representative methods proposed between 2024 and 2026, identify five major design patterns, and surface a set of open challenges -- including cross-architecture alignment, security of latent channels, compression for edge deployment, and the relationship between latent communication and latent chain-of-thought. We hope that this framework both lowers the barrier to entry for new researchers and provides a vocabulary for comparing future work.

</details>


### [154] [Interpreting Style Representations via Style-Eliciting Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05716)
*Junghwan Kim,David Jurgens*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel framework to improve the interpretability of style representations in authorship analysis by employing style-eliciting prompts with large language models, resulting in superior performance in style description and imitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learned style representations are often latent and hard to interpret, posing challenges for their practical application in authorship analysis and writing style modeling.

Method: The authors propose using style-eliciting prompts, which are instructions to guide LLMs to generate texts reflecting specific stylistic features. They create a dataset of 1,010 style features across 26 stylistic categories and train a decoder to recover the style prompt from the style representation of generated text.

Result: The proposed approach demonstrates significant improvements over baselines, outperforming them in tasks such as recovering style prompts, generating text that mimics a specific style, and steering LLM outputs to replicate the style of human-written text.

Conclusion: Style-eliciting prompts provide an interpretable and practical way to access stylistic information within style representations, overcoming limitations of prior methods reliant on direct LLM output.

Abstract: Style representation learning is a powerful tool for authorship analysis and modeling writing style, yet the latent nature of learned representations makes them difficult to interpret. Recent work has attempted to explain these representations by generating natural language descriptions with large language models (LLMs) conditioned on input text. However, such descriptions are often prone to the LLM's biases and hallucinations, and they lack an explicit objective and practical utility. In this work, we propose a novel framework for interpreting style representations through style-eliciting prompts: natural language instructions designed to steer LLMs to generate text that reflects specific stylistic attributes. We curate 1,010 distinct style features spanning 26 stylistic categories and construct a dataset by prompting an LLM to generate text conditioned on these features. Using this data, we train a decoder to generate a style prompt from the style representation of the generated text. We evaluate our approach on three tasks: (1) recovering original style prompts from generated text, (2) generating text in the same style using the recovered prompts, and (3) steering LLM outputs to match the style of human-written texts. Experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines that directly prompt LLMs with target text, achieving superior performance in both style description and style imitation. These results highlight that style-eliciting prompts can provide a practical and interpretable interface to stylistic information encoded in style representations.

</details>


### [155] [Narrative Knowledge Weaver: Narrative-Centric Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning for Long-Form Text Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05724)
*Qiuyu Tian,Fengyi Chen,Yiding Li,Youyong Kong,Fan Guo,Yuyao Li,Jinjing Shen,Zhijing Xie,Yiyun Luo,Xin Zhang,Yingce Xia,Zequn Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Narrative Knowledge Weaver (NKW) framework for long-form narrative question answering, focusing on reasoning over story-world elements rather than isolated facts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind the study is to address the challenge of answering long-form narrative questions, which require reasoning over evolving story elements, such as character states and temporal dynamics—something existing models struggle to handle effectively.

Method: The paper proposes the NKW framework that integrates textual evidence with atomic facts, graph structures, entity profiles, and storylines. NKW uses various tools at query time to assemble evidence and evaluate multiple constraints crucial for story comprehension.

Result: The NKW framework demonstrated superior performance on screenplay-level story-world QA benchmarks like STAGE, while remaining competitive on passage-centered tasks like FairytaleQA and QuALITY. Ablation studies and analyses confirmed its robustness across diverse reasoning types.

Conclusion: The study concludes that NKW enhances reasoning capabilities for narrative QA tasks by combining textual, graphical, and narrative components, offering complementary benefits in understanding character interactions, temporal flow, and causality in stories.

Abstract: Long-form narrative QA requires reasoning over evolving story worlds rather than isolated passages: answers may depend on earlier goals, changing character states, social relations, causal triggers, temporal position, and later consequences. Existing retrieval and graph-augmented generation methods improve evidence access, but their units--chunks, entities, relations, summaries, or tool actions--do not directly encode how evidence functions in a story. We introduce Narrative Knowledge Weaver(NKW), a source-grounded framework that aligns textual evidence, atomic facts, canonical graph structure, entity profiles, interactions, episodes, and storylines. At query time, NKW uses text, graph, and narrative tools with post-retrieval reading skills to assemble evidence and audit actor, scope, polarity, state, and temporal constraints. Across STAGE, FairytaleQA, and QuALITY, NKW is strongest on screenplay-level story-world QA while remaining competitive on more passage-centered benchmarks. Ablations, question-type analyses, graph-asset statistics, and case studies show complementary benefits for character, scene, temporal, causal, and narrative-progression reasoning.

</details>


### [156] [AdaPLD: Adaptive Retrieval and Reuse for Efficient Model-Free Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05742)
*Runheng Liu,Jincheng Xie,Wen Hu,Xingchen Xiao,Heyan Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces AdaPLD, a speculative decoding method that boosts generation speed by improving context reuse and handling uncertainty in continuations, achieving up to 3.10x decoding speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address limitations in model-free speculative decoding, specifically the low recall of lexically anchored retrieval and brittleness of deterministic span copying, which hinder decoding speedup.

Method: AdaPLD improves context reuse with a semantic similarity approach to expand retrieval opportunities and introduces branched reuse hypotheses to better handle uncertainties in continuation.

Result: The proposed method significantly reduces the number of target-model forward passes and achieves a substantial decoding speedup of up to 3.10x across various benchmarks.

Conclusion: AdaPLD effectively enhances speculative decoding by overcoming recall and robustness challenges, offering a training-free and versatile solution to accelerate generation tasks.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates generation by verifying multiple drafted tokens in a single target-model forward pass, reducing sequential decoding iterations. Model-free variants avoid auxiliary draft models by reusing text and model states already available during generation, but their speedup depends on the reliability of the constructed drafts. We identify two limitations of existing reuse-based methods: lexically anchored retrieval has limited recall under surface-form variation, and deterministic span copying can be brittle when the retrieved context does not uniquely determine the continuation. We propose \emph{AdaPLD}, a training-free method that adaptively improves both retrieval and draft construction. AdaPLD preserves high-precision lexical reuse while using semantic similarity to recover additional reuse opportunities when lexical matching fails. It further constructs branched reuse hypotheses to account for continuation uncertainty, rather than relying on a single copied span. Across diverse benchmarks, AdaPLD reduces target-model forward passes and achieves up to $3.10\times$ decoding speedup.

</details>


### [157] [PlanBench-V: A Spatial Planning Map Benchmark for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05744)
*Minxin Chen,He Zhu,Junyou Su,Wen Wang,Yijie Deng,Wenjia Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PlanBench-V, the first benchmark for evaluating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in interpreting spatial planning maps, highlighting its domain-specific challenges and limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where current multimodal benchmarks overlook the complex cognitive processes required in spatial planning map interpretation, crucial for urban planning and governance.

Method: The authors develop PlanBench-V, which includes the creation of the Spatial Planning Map Database (SPMD) with 223 annotated maps and 1629 question-answer pairs, along with a theory-informed evaluation framework assessing perception, reasoning, association, and implementation capabilities of VLMs.

Result: Experiments with two generations of VLMs show improvements, with Qwen3.6-Plus outperforming GPT-4o by 27% in 2026, but all models still underperform on implementation-focused tasks demanding judgment and policy sensitivity.

Conclusion: Current VLMs show progress but remain inadequate for professional planning contexts, emphasizing the need for domain-specific multimodal reasoning frameworks.

Abstract: Spatial planning maps are central to territorial governance, translating planning objectives, regulations, and spatial strategies into visual forms for decision-making, public communication, and institutional coordination. Their interpretation, however, requires fine-grained visual perception, spatial reasoning, and policy-informed professional judgment, creating major challenges for both human learners and AI systems. With the rapid progress of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), their use in urban planning analysis is gaining attention, yet existing multimodal benchmarks mainly target general visual understanding and overlook the domain-specific cognitive processes of planning practice. To address this gap, we introduce PlanBench-V, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating VLMs in spatial planning map interpretation. We first build the Spatial Planning Map Database (SPMD), an expert-annotated dataset of 223 planning maps and 1629 question-answer pairs curated by professional planners, covering diverse geographic regions and cartographic styles. We then propose a theory-informed evaluation framework assessing four progressive capabilities: Perception, Reasoning, Association, and Implementation, corresponding to the cognitive pipeline of planning map interpretation. Extensive experiments across two generations of VLMs show clear progress but persistent limitations. The best 2026 agentic reasoning model, Qwen3.6-Plus, substantially outperforms the best 2025 model, GPT-4o, by 27%. Nevertheless, all models still struggle with implementation-oriented tasks requiring evaluative judgment, policy sensitivity, and constraint-aware decision-making. These findings reveal fundamental limitations of current VLMs in professional planning contexts and highlight the need for domain-adaptive multimodal reasoning frameworks. Code and data are available at https://plangpt.github.io.

</details>


### [158] [MARDoc: A Memory-Aware Refinement Agent Framework for Multimodal Long Document QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05749)
*Kaifeng Chen,Hongtao Liu,Qiyao Peng,Jian Yang,Yongqiang Liu,Xiaochen Zhang,Qing Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MARDoc, a framework for long-document question answering, which separates tasks into specialized agents and uses structured memory to improve multi-hop reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current systems for multimodal long-document QA struggle with noisy multi-hop reasoning due to a single, growing context that scatters key evidence.

Method: MARDoc uses three agents: the Explorer for retrieval, the Refiner for structuring evidence, and the Reflector for evaluating sufficiency, with a structured memory for dynamic updates.

Result: MARDoc demonstrates strong performance on MMLongBench-Doc and DocBench, outperforming same-backbone baselines.

Conclusion: Structured memory and task-specific agent specialization improve the efficiency and accuracy of multi-hop reasoning in long-document QA.

Abstract: Iterative retrieval-reasoning agents have recently shown promise for multimodal long-document question answering. However, most existing systems maintain a single growing context that mixes retrieval traces, observations, and intermediate reasoning. As interactions accumulate, key evidence becomes scattered and diluted, making multi-hop reasoning noisy. We propose MARDoc, a Memory-Aware Refinement Agent framework that decouples long-document QA into three specialized agents: an Explorer for multi-granularity multimodal retrieval, a Refiner for distilling interaction traces into structured evidence and reasoning memories, and a Reflector for checking evidence sufficiency and providing targeted feedback. Across iterations, the agents rely on a dynamically updated structured memory rather than a full accumulated interaction history. This design reduces context noise while preserving answer-critical facts and their logical dependencies. Experiments on MMLongBench-Doc and DocBench show that MARDoc achieves strong results, outperforming same-backbone baselines and demonstrating the effectiveness of structured memory for agentic document QA.

</details>


### [159] [CollabBench: Benchmarking and Unleashing Collaborative Ability of LLMs with Diverse Players via Proactive Engagement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05793)
*Hong Qian,Yuanhao Liu,Zihan Zhou,Zongbao Zhang,Hanjie Ge,Haotian Shi,Liang Dou,Xiangfeng Wang,Jingwen Yang,Aimin Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CollabBench, a benchmark designed to train and evaluate LLM-based agents for effective collaboration in games, addressing both efficiency and affective aspects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in collaborative settings where LLMs struggle to effectively work with human-like diverse partners, and create immersive environments for grounded interaction.

Method: Proposes CollabBench with a Diverse Player Profile Simulation pipeline for modeling player behaviors, Collaborative Agentic Training to unify actions, communication, and reasoning, optimization with a hybrid reward system, and extends classic environments for systematic evaluation.

Result: Trained models show 19.5% higher task efficiency and 24.4% improved affective performance compared to base models. Analysis identifies areas for improvement in collaborative scenarios.

Conclusion: CollabBench provides a meaningful step forward in building and evaluating collaborative agents, highlighting both advancements and areas needing further exploration in effective cooperation.

Abstract: While LLM-based agents excel at individual tasks, effective collaboration with realistic human partners remains challenging. Most of the existing conversation-level collaborative studies lack grounded interaction and behavioral execution, motivating the need for cooperative game environments that enable contextualized and immersive collaboration. To this end, this paper proposes CollabBench, a benchmark for evaluating and training collaborative agents in cooperative games. CollabBench features a Diverse Player Profile Simulation pipeline to model varied players behaviors, and a Collaborative Agentic Training paradigm that unifies reasoning, communication, and action via agentic rollouts, optimized with a hybrid reward balancing task efficiency and affective adaptation. We further extend classic environments to CWAH-MultiPlayer and Cook-MultiPlayer for systematic evaluation under diverse personalities. Experiments with efficiency and affective metrics show that our trained models outperform base models, achieving 19.5% higher efficiency and 24.4% improved affective performance. Further analysis reveals key collaborative limitations of existing models and offers insights for future collaborative training.

</details>


### [160] [Can LLMs Be Constrained to the Past? Improving Knowledge Cutoff through Recall-Based Prompting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05804)
*Michiro Asai,Ailiang Lin,Yu Kishimoto,Takao Obi,Satoshi Kosugi,Kotaro Funakoshi,Manabu Okumura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces two recall-based prompting strategies to improve large language model cutoff-based generation, achieving better performance on tasks, including counterfactuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of large language models in accurately responding to questions where post-cutoff knowledge is causally relevant but not explicitly queried, requiring strategies to enhance cutoff-constrained knowledge generation.

Method: The paper introduces Self-Recall (SR) prompting, where the model reiterates its cutoff constraint, and Question-Recall (QR) prompting, where the model recalls question-relevant information valid under the cutoff, testing these strategies on benchmarks.

Result: The proposed methods outperform direct-answer and step-by-step reasoning baselines across three benchmarks and show strong improvements on counterfactual questions. Performance varies with cutoff distance, but combining SR and QR consistently gives the best results.

Conclusion: Recall-based prompting strategies (SR and QR) are effective for improving large language model performance in cutoff-dependent tasks, especially under varying cutoff years, making the approach robust and impactful.

Abstract: Prompted knowledge cutoff instructs a large language model (LLM) to act as if information beyond a specified cutoff date were unavailable. However, prior work mainly relies on direct-answer generation, which struggles when post-cutoff knowledge is not explicitly queried but is only causally related to the question. To address this limitation, we propose two recall-based prompting strategies: Self-Recall (SR), which asks the model to restate its cutoff constraint, and Question-Recall (QR), which requires the model to recall question-relevant information valid under the cutoff. Across three existing benchmarks, our methods outperform both direct-answer prompting and conventional step-by-step reasoning baselines, with particularly strong improvements on counterfactual questions. To investigate robustness across different cutoff settings, we further construct the Multi-cutoff Historical Event Benchmark (MHEB), which evaluates the same question under multiple cutoff years. Results show that knowledge cutoff performance varies with cutoff distance, while combining SR and QR consistently yields the best performance.

</details>


### [161] [ProSPy: A Profiling-Driven SQL-Python Agentic Framework for Enterprise Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05836)
*Zhaorui Yang,Huawei Zheng,Sen Yang,Yuhui Zhang,Haoxuan Li,Zhizhen Yu,Xuan Yi,Chen Hou,Defeng Xie,Chao Hu,Minfeng Zhu,Dazhen Deng,Haozhe Feng,Danqing Huang,Yingcai Wu,Peng Chen,Wei Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProSPy is a profiling-driven SQL-Python framework that improves Text-to-SQL systems in handling enterprise-scale databases by structuring reasoning into four stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges in applying Text-to-SQL systems on real-world databases, such as large schemas, incomplete metadata, dialect-specific SQL syntax, and complex analytical questions.

Method: ProSPy extracts data evidence through profiling, prunes schemas for context relevance, uses a dialect-agnostic SQL interface for intermediate views, and conducts detailed analysis with Python.

Result: ProSPy outperformed baselines with execution accuracies of over 60% on Spider 2.0 datasets and demonstrated robustness to SQL dialect variations.

Conclusion: ProSPy efficiently combines SQL and Python, addressing key challenges of schema complexity, metadata completeness, and SQL dialect diversity, providing enhanced performance and robustness for Text-to-SQL tasks.

Abstract: Large language models have substantially advanced Text-to-SQL systems, yet applying them to enterprise-scale databases remains challenging. Real-world databases often contain large and heterogeneous schemas, incomplete metadata, dialect-specific SQL syntax, and complex analytical questions that are difficult to solve with a single SQL query. To address these challenges, we propose ProSPy, a Profiling-driven SQL--Python agentic framework for enterprise-scale Text-to-SQL. ProSPy structures the reasoning process into four stages: it first extracts fine-grained data evidence through automatic profiling, progressively prunes large schemas into task-relevant contexts, fetches intermediate views through a dialect-agnostic SQL interface, and finally performs flexible downstream analysis with Python. This design combines the efficiency of SQL over large databases with the flexibility of Python-based analysis, while reducing reliance on unreliable metadata and improving robustness across SQL dialects. Experiments on Spider 2.0-Lite and Spider 2.0-Snow show that ProSPy consistently outperforms strong baselines with both open-source and proprietary models, achieving execution accuracies of 60.15% and 60.51% with Claude-4.5-Opus, without majority voting. Further analysis shows that ProSPy is robust to SQL dialect variations and achieves a favorable trade-off between schema recall and precision.

</details>


### [162] [Mechanistic Insights into Functional Sparsity in Multimodal LLMs via CoRe Heads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05843)
*Ruoxi Sun,Quantong Qiu,Juntao Li,Zecheng Tang,Yihang Lou,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper uncovers functional sparsity in Multimodal Large Language Models, identifying specialized attention heads (CoRe heads) responsible for extracting task-relevant features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand how MLLMs retrieve relevant visual information from noisy, complex contexts while performing vision-language tasks.

Method: Researchers develop a token-level metric called Retrieval Attention Mass (RAM) to identify CoRe heads, perform causal interventions by ablating attention heads, and validate findings through acceleration experiments.

Result: A small, specialized subset of attention heads (top 5% of CoRe heads) proves critical for multimodal reasoning performance. Ablating them severely degrades performance, while eliminating lower-ranked heads has minimal impact.

Conclusion: The study introduces the concept of functional sparsity in MLLMs, highlighting CoRe heads' critical role, and offering insights for future model design and optimization.

Abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate remarkable proficiency on complex vision-language tasks, the mechanisms by which they extract query-relevant visual features from complex, noisy contexts remain opaque. In this paper, we present an in-depth interpretability study that uncovers a profound structural property within MLLMs: functional sparsity in cross-modal retrieval. Leveraging a token-level metric termed Retrieval Attention Mass (RAM), we identify and characterize a highly specialized subset of attention heads, referred to as Context-aware Retrieval (CoRe) heads. Across diverse visual domains and model scales, we observe a clear functional division: CoRe heads act as dedicated information extractors, while most other heads distribute attention over broader contextual regions. Causal interventions further demonstrate the necessity of these specialized heads. Ablating only the top 5% of CoRe heads causes significant degradation in multimodal reasoning performance, whereas ablating lower-ranked heads has minimal effect. Moreover, acceleration experiments validate the utility of CoRe heads, showing that leveraging this localized sparsity significantly accelerates inference while maintaining robust task performance. Our findings reveal a structural principle of functional sparsity within MLLMs, refining the current understanding of mechanistic interpretability and laying a theoretical foundation that can inspire future architecture design and model optimization.

</details>


### [163] [Towards Truly Multilingual ASR: Generalizing Code-Switching ASR to Unseen Language Pairs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05846)
*Gio Paik,Hyunseo Shin,Soungmin Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores generalizing code-switching ASR (CS-ASR) models to unseen language pairs through merging and domain generalization methods, finding limited success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Code-switching ASR remains a challenging task because of the scarcity of multilingual CS speech datasets, especially for diverse language pairs, and the existing methods' scalability limitations.

Method: The authors used model merging and domain generalization techniques to investigate the ability to transfer CS capabilities learned from a set of seen language pairs to new, unseen language pairs.

Result: Merged bilingual CS-ASR models showed a modest ability to generalize to unseen language pairs, suggesting limited transferability across language pairs.

Conclusion: While showing some potential, the generalization of CS-ASR capabilities to unseen language pairs remains a challenge, highlighting the need for further research into more scalable and generalized approaches.

Abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has become a key technology for human--AI interaction. However, code-switching ASR (CS-ASR) remains particularly challenging due to the severe scarcity of multilingual CS speech resources across diverse language pairs. Existing approaches primarily improve CS-ASR performance through synthetic CS speech generation or pair-specific fine-tuning on limited bilingual datasets. Nevertheless, these approaches face an inherent scalability limitation, as support for CS must be developed separately for language pairs whose number grows combinatorially with the number of supported languages. In this work, we investigate whether CS capabilities learned from a limited set of seen language pairs can generalize to unseen language pairs through model merging and domain generalization methods. Our experiments show that merged bilingual CS-ASR models modestly generalize to unseen language pairs, suggesting limited transfer of bilingual CS capabilities across language pairs.

</details>


### [164] [Forgive or forget: Understanding the context of hate in audio retrieval systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05857)
*Arghya Pal,Sailaja Rajanala,Raphael C. -W. Phan,Shekhar Nayak*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to handle toxic retrieval in text-to-audio systems while maintaining semantic relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of preserving intent and details while reducing toxic speech in text-to-audio systems.

Method: Introduce a causal debiasing framework with a sentiment-controlled mediator and two strategies, Forgive and Forget, to mitigate toxicity.

Result: Demonstrated consistent reduction in toxicity without significantly impacting retrieval accuracy.

Conclusion: The method enhances both safety and reliability in retrieval pipelines.

Abstract: Handling toxic retrieval in text-to-audio systems is challenging due to contextual dependencies. Existing strategies (e.g., rephrasing, summarization) risk altering intent or omitting details. We propose a post hoc causal debiasing framework with a sentiment-controlled mediator to preserve semantic relevance while suppressing harmful speech. Our approach is model-agnostic and integrates seamlessly with existing retrieval pipelines. We introduce two variants: Forgive, which re-ranks and filters toxic audio via logit adjustment, and Forget, which generates counterfactual toxic prompts to mitigate harmful retrievals. Experiments show consistent toxicity reduction with minimal loss in retrieval accuracy, improving both safety and reliability.

</details>


### [165] [ReverseEOL: Improving Training-free Text Embeddings via Text Reversal in Decoder-only LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05858)
*Ailiang Lin,Zhuoyun Li,Yusong Wang,Keyu Mao,Kotaro Funakoshi,Manabu Okumura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReverseEOL, a technique to improve text embeddings of frozen LLMs by combining standard forward embeddings with reversed ones, enhancing representation in training-free scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the limitations of decoder-only LLMs where causal attention biases contextualized representations, particularly due to the inability to attend to future context.

Method: Proposes ReverseEOL, which reverses the input text to generate reversed embeddings, combining them with forward embeddings to create richer representations. This grants each token access to previously unavailable context.

Result: ReverseEOL consistently boosts text embedding performance across various LLM architectures and sizes, validated through STS and MTEB benchmarks.

Conclusion: ReverseEOL is a simple yet impactful approach to enhance training-free embeddings in LLMs, with extensive experiments confirming the value of the reversal mechanism.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have opened new avenues for generating training-free text embeddings. However, the causal attention in decoder-only LLMs prevents earlier tokens from attending to future context, leading to biased contextualized representations. In this work, we propose Reverse prompting with Explicit One-word Limitation (ReverseEOL), a simple yet effective method for enhancing the representational capability of frozen LLMs. ReverseEOL augments the standard forward embedding with an additional reversed embedding derived from the reversed input text. Since reversing the input exposes each token to context inaccessible in the original order, the resulting reversed embedding effectively provides complementary information to the original one. As a result, combining the forward and reversed embeddings yields a richer final representation. Comprehensive experiments on STS and MTEB benchmarks demonstrate that ReverseEOL significantly improves the performance of existing training-free baselines across a broad range of LLMs with diverse architectures and scales. Extensive ablations and analyses further confirm the necessity of our reversal mechanism.

</details>


### [166] [TARPO: Token-Wise Latent-Explicit Reasoning via Action-Routing Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05859)
*Liting Zhang,Shiwan Zhao,Xuyang Zhao,Zichen Xu,Jianye Wang,Qicheng Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TARPO is a reinforcement learning framework for large language models (LLMs) that adaptively switches between discrete token generation and continuous latent reasoning, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the deterministic limitation of continuous representations in reinforcement learning, enabling expressive reasoning while maintaining exploration.

Method: TARPO introduces a binary action-routing head that determines token-wise switches between discrete and continuous reasoning in LLMs. The framework is optimized jointly with a shared advantage signal.

Result: Extensive experiments using Qwen2.5 and Llama variants show that TARPO achieves superior performance compared to existing RL baselines in diverse benchmarks, with stable training and adaptive behavior.

Conclusion: TARPO is an effective RL approach for combining discrete and latent reasoning in LLMs, delivering better performance and adaptability while preserving stable training dynamics.

Abstract: Latent reasoning has emerged as a promising alternative to discrete Chain-of-Thought (CoT) in large language models (LLMs), enabling more expressive reasoning by operating over continuous representations. However, the inherently deterministic nature of continuous representations limits policy exploration in reinforcement learning (RL). To address this, we propose TARPO (Token-Wise Latent-Explicit Reasoning via Action-Routing Policy Optimization), a pure RL framework that adaptively switches between discrete token generation and continuous latent reasoning at each step. TARPO introduces a lightweight action head router that observes the current hidden state and samples a routing decision from a binary mode-selection space, preserving the stochasticity of discrete token sampling from the vocabulary. The LLM backbone and router are jointly optimized end-to-end with a shared group-relative advantage signal. Extensive experiments across Qwen2.5 (from 1.5B to 7B) and Llama-3.1-8B backbones demonstrate that TARPO consistently outperforms existing explicit and latent reasoning RL baselines across diverse benchmarks. Further analysis shows that TARPO learns adaptive token-wise switching behaviors while maintaining stable training dynamics. Our code is available at https://github.com/NKU-LITI/TARPO-master.

</details>


### [167] [Analysis of the Neglect-Zero Effect in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05864)
*Jin Tanaka,Daiki Matsuoka,Ryoma Kumon,Hitomi Yanaka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study examines whether large language models (LLMs) exhibit the human cognitive bias known as the neglect-zero effect by using structural priming experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to understand the similarities between human cognitive processes and LLMs, particularly in how they handle inferences influenced by the neglect-zero effect.

Method: The authors use structural priming to analyze LLM behavior. They design primes that force considerations of zero-models and evaluate their influence on subsequent target sentences.

Result: Results indicate that the LLMs studied do not appear to display the neglect-zero effect, suggesting a divergence from human cognitive tendencies in this aspect.

Conclusion: LLMs do not replicate the human neglect-zero effect and handle such cognitive tasks differently, based on the study's findings.

Abstract: We investigate the extent to which the language processing of LLMs resembles human cognitive processes, focusing on a human cognitive bias called the $\textit{neglect-zero effect}$. This effect refers to the human tendency to ignore $\textit{zero-models}$, which are configurations that render a proposition vacuously true by virtue of an empty set. We focus on two types of inferences driven by the neglect-zero effect, and examine how LLMs process these inferences by comparing their behavior with that in an inference that does not involve the neglect-zero effect. For this purpose, we employ a paradigm based on $\textit{structural priming}$, where recent exposure to a preceding sentence (the $\textit{prime}$) facilitates the processing of a subsequent sentence (the $\textit{target}$) due to their structural similarity. We prepare primes to force LLMs to consider the zero-model, and analyze whether they also consider it in the target. The results suggest that the neglect-zero effect may not occur in the LLMs analyzed in this study. Our code is available at https://github.com/ynklab/neglect_zero

</details>


### [168] [YouZhi: Towards High-Concurrency Financial LLMs via Adaptive GQA-to-MLA Transition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05868)
*PSBC LLM Team,Huawei LLM Team,Ruihan Long,Junjie Wu,Tianan Zhang,Duo Zhang,Yaozong Wu,Jinbin Fu,Chang Liu,Zhentao Tang,Wenshuang Yang,Xin Wang,Zhihao Song,Ning Huang,Wenjing Xu,Shuai Zong,Shupei Sun,Sen Wang,Jing Hu,Bin Wang,Xinyu Wang,Junkui Ju,Zequn Ding,Jie Ran,Man Luo,Shixiong Kai,Linkai Hou,Kaichao Liang,Hu Zhao,Yang Zhao,Shucheng Lin,Wei Yu,Chenghan Jiang,Jingjing Ding,Jiahui Zhang,Tian Jin,Yuhang Zhang,Dong Guo,Wei Sun,Jun Xie,Jianwei Li,Lei Cao,Pei Li,Jiabin Li,Jia Yuan,Rui Yuan,Jing Zhu,Mingxuan Yuan,Zhangcheng Lv,Xin Jiang,Xiuhong Fei,Xiaozhe Ren,Yulong Li,Zhipeng Zhang,Hang Wang,Zhaohui Xu,Rui Zhao,Yibo He,Xinzhuang Niu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces YouZhi-LLM, a financial large language model optimized for higher efficiency and cost-effectiveness by addressing KV-cache memory issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the high memory overhead and low scalability caused by KV cache issues in LLM deployment, especially for financial applications.

Method: YouZhi-LLM employs a layer-adaptive GQA-to-MLA transition framework, integrated with generalized knowledge distillation and financial-specific fine-tuning, built on the Huawei Ascend ecosystem.

Result: YouZhi-LLM significantly reduces perplexity degradation, improves financial benchmark scores, and increases model concurrency, offering better performance and deployment efficiency.

Conclusion: This systematic approach provides a cost-effective, scalable solution for financial LLM inference, demonstrating the potential of optimized architectures for high-throughput scenarios.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) drive significant financial innovations, yet their high-concurrency deployment is severely bottlenecked by KV cache memory overhead, which inflates infrastructure costs and throttles scalability. To address this, we propose YouZhi-LLM, a highly efficient financial LLM empowered by a comprehensive structural transition and training pipeline natively built on the Huawei Ascend ecosystem. At its algorithmic core, YouZhi-LLM features a layer-adaptive GQA-to-MLA transition framework that dynamically assigns per-layer FreqFold sizes, maximizing KV-cache compression while minimizing perplexity degradation. To recover representation capacity and inject domain expertise, the Ascend-based training pipeline seamlessly integrates generalized knowledge distillation with financial-specific supervised fine-tuning. Evaluations demonstrate the superiority of this systematic approach, with the adaptive transition reducing perplexity degradation by up to 35% over uniform baselines. Crucially, when evaluated on Ascend NPUs via vLLM-Ascend, the massive KV-cache reduction translates directly into deployment efficiency. Compared to their respective base models, YouZhi-7B yields a 12.3% improvement in average financial benchmark score alongside a 2.69$\times$ increase in maximum concurrency; similarly, YouZhi-14B achieves a 7.0% accuracy gain and a 2.43$\times$ concurrency boost, establishing a new paradigm for cost-effective, high-throughput financial inference.

</details>


### [169] [Evaluating Stochastic Collapse and Implicit Bias in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05874)
*Huiyuan Zheng,Houtao Zhang,Boyang Wang,Qingyi Si,Hongcheng Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces RandomBench to evaluate stochastic behaviors in Multimodal LLMs, identifying issues of non-randomness termed 'Stochastic Collapse.'


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current metrics for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) focus on utility, overlooking their behavior in scenarios requiring equal preference for multiple valid choices.

Method: The authors propose RandomBench along with three metrics—Randomness Index (RI), Bias Coverage Index (BCI), and Bias Intensity Index (BII)—to analyze uniform randomness of MLLMs in decision-making scenarios.

Result: The study identifies 'Stochastic Collapse,' where MLLMs fail to distribute random outputs evenly. Experiments show a drop in randomness metrics and persistence of biases across languages and representation styles.

Conclusion: MLLMs are inadequate in maintaining stochastic behaviors under logic-neutral scenarios, necessitating benchmarks like RandomBench to improve their performance in such cases.

Abstract: Current evaluations for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) overwhelmingly focus on utility-driven objectives, leaving model behavior under logic-neutral scenarios largely underexplored. Stochasticity is essential in scenarios where multiple actions are equally valid, such as recommending travel itineraries or daily schedules where multiple options have similar utility. In such settings, deterministic policies may lead to repetitive behaviors and reduced coverage of valid alternatives. To bridge this gap, we propose RandomBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate whether MLLMs can maintain distributionally neutral behavior when selecting among equivalent options. We further introduce three metrics, including RI, BCI, BII, to quantify entropy and distributional bias. Experiments reveal a pervasive phenomenon termed Stochastic Collapse, where MLLMs fail to maintain uniform randomness under explicit random instructions, with top-1 probabilities reaching 97% from the ideal one quarter baseline and RI dropping to 0.068 in Claude Sonnet 4.6. Extensive ablation studies further demonstrate that these deviations persist across languages and representation formats, highlighting the robustness of distributional collapse in logic-neutral decision settings.

</details>


### [170] [Staying with the Uncertainty: Uncertainty-Scaffolding Strategies for Artificial Moral Advisors in LLM-to-LLM Simulated Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05890)
*Salvatore Greco,Hainiu Xu,Jacopo Domenicucci,Yulan He,Sylvie Delacroix*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores the conversational behaviors of large language models (LLMs) as artificial moral advisors (AMA), focusing on strategies to manage uncertainty in dialogues on ethical dilemmas. Six strategies were tested across different model types and persona formats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AMAs can effectively engage users in ethical conversations by managing uncertainty and promoting deeper engagement rather than providing definitive answers.

Method: The study defined three uncertainty strategies (Perspective-Multiplying, Tension-Preserving, Process-Reflecting) and three controls (Baseline, Persuasive, Sycophantic). These strategies were tested in dialogues where an LLM engaged a simulated user on ethical dilemmas, with pre- and post-conversation surveys to measure impact. Two persona formats (Declarative and Narrative) were also compared.

Result: Key findings include: (1) open models show alignment with human ambiguity through persona divergence, while closed models do so through hedging; (2) declarative personas capture initial diversity, whereas narrative personas support realistic belief changes; (3) conversational patterns vary clearly across strategies; (4) uncertainty strategies foster distinct engagement qualities rather than differing significantly in stance revision.

Conclusion: LLMs as AMAs can employ different uncertainty strategies to yield varied conversational engagement qualities. These strategies should emphasize qualitative engagement rather than definitive stance revisions, making ethical dialogues richer and more nuanced.

Abstract: LLMs are increasingly deployed as Artificial Moral Advisors (AMA) in a variety of contexts: what kind of conversational patterns should they display? In this paper, we study how AMA can help their interlocutors "stay with the uncertainty". We propose three modes of uncertainty (Perspective-Multiplying, Tension-Preserving, Process-Reflecting) and compare them against three control conditions (Baseline, Persuasive, Sycophantic). A user-agent LLM engages in a dialogue on an ethical dilemma with an AMA following a specific uncertainty strategy, and completes pre- and post-conversation questionnaires. We further examine the effect of two persona prompt formats (Declarative and Narrative). We found that (1) no single model dominates as a simulated user agent, with open models aligning with human ambiguity through between-persona divergence and closed models through within-persona hedging; (2) declarative personas better capture initial stance diversity while narrative personas show more realistic belief revision; (3) all six AMA strategies produce distinguishable conversational patterns; and (4) uncertainty strategies differ not in how much stance revision they produce, but in the quality of engagement they sustain.

</details>


### [171] [EMBER: Efficient Memory via Budgeted Evidence Retention for Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05894)
*Yilong Li,Suman Banerjee,Tong Che*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces EMBER, a method for retaining compact evidence in memory for long-horizon systems under token budget constraints, yielding significantly improved retrieval performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of efficiently retaining and retrieving answer-relevant evidence in long-horizon systems when access to raw history is restricted or costly.

Method: The authors propose EMBER, a learned retention policy that generates compact evidence capsules combining source excerpts and metadata, trained via feedback from ingestion-retrieval-answer processes to optimize evidence survival within a token budget.

Result: EMBER outperforms the best non-EMBER baseline on the LongMemEval-RR benchmark, showing a significant gain in F1 scores (0.3017 vs. 0.1765) and consistently better performance across other metrics.

Conclusion: Retaining highly relevant evidence under token budget constraints is critical for long-horizon systems, and EMBER offers a robust solution by compactly storing and preserving retrieval-ready evidence while improving overall system performance.

Abstract: Long-horizon agents can archive large histories, but future answers still incur retrieval, rereading, and context costs. When retained memory misses answer-relevant evidence, the system must return to larger portions of the raw history. We study budgeted evidence survival: before the query is known, which source evidence should be retained so that it remains recoverable and usable under a fixed retained source-evidence token budget? We instantiate this setting as Budgeted Pre-Query Retention, where memory is written during ingestion and later read without access to the full raw stream. We introduce EMBER, a learned retention policy that constructs a compact, source-backed evidence state. EMBER stores evidence capsules: verbatim source excerpts paired with retrieval keys and update metadata, preserving both grounding and read-time access. Post-query outcome feedback trains the writer to preserve evidence across the ingestion-retrieval-answer chain. On LongMemEval-RR, our LongMemEval-derived retained-evidence protocol, EMBER-14B reaches 0.3017 F1 at the 8192-token retained-evidence comparison point, compared with 0.1765 for the strongest non-EMBER budgeted baseline. Across retained source-evidence budgets, EMBER improves F1, Retain-Recall, and Read-Recall, indicating that long-horizon memory depends on retaining evidence within the budget rather than rereading larger histories.

</details>


### [172] [Representing Research Attention as Contextually Structured Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05895)
*Jessica Rodrigues,Angelo Salatino,Gard Jenset,Scott Hale*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'attention flows' as structured representations to better capture and analyze research attention over time and context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the gap where research attention is typically represented by aggregated counts, which do not accurately reflect its contextual and temporal dynamics.

Method: The paper develops 'attention flows,' evaluates their performance through analogy-based benchmarks, and compares them with other representations based on signal and sequence.

Result: Attention flow-based models outperform other methods in capturing structural dynamics, show robustness to partial data and perturbations, and improve structural comparison.

Conclusion: Structured and contextual modeling of research attention improves its interpretability and offers more robust tools for research evaluation.

Abstract: Research attention is widely used as an indicator of visibility, influence, and societal uptake, yet it is typically represented as aggregated counts that do not preserve how attention develops across contexts over time. This creates a mismatch between how attention is interpreted and how it is represented. We propose attention flows as contextually structured representations that encode the organisation of attention and its evolution over time. We evaluate whether these representations capture transferable structure by constructing a benchmark based on analogy-style reasoning across research outputs. Comparing signal, sequence, and flow-based representations, we find that flow representations more effectively support structural comparison, particularly in settings where attention is shaped by temporal progression or context distributions. We further show that learned flow representations improve robustness under partial observation and structural perturbation. Overall, these results support modelling attention as a contextually structured phenomenon and provide a basis for more informative approaches to research evaluation.

</details>


### [173] [Reducing Hallucinations in Complex Question Answering using Simple Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (long version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05901)
*Christopher J. Wedge,Joshua Stutter,Danny Dixon,Jacek Cała*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper suggests enhancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems using lightweight graph structures to improve factual accuracy and reduce hallucination in queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of LLMs, including hallucination and reasoning over proprietary data without model fine-tuning.

Method: Integrating lightweight graph structures with a set of vector search and graph query tools over structured datasets like English Wikipedia.

Result: Graph-based tools improved precision and recall in answers, reduced hallucinated responses by half, and achieved superior truthfulness scores in QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Lightweight graph structures can significantly enhance RAG systems' performance in factual accuracy and reasoning with minimal additional token overhead.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have fundamentally transformed the landscape of Natural Language Processing. Despite these advances, LLMs and LLM-based systems remain prone to a variety of failure modes. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems have emerged as a common deployment scenario seeking to both avoid the well known risk of the LLM "hallucinating" information, and to enable reasoning and question answering over proprietary information that the LLM did not have access to during training without resorting to expensive model fine-tuning.
  In this work, we explore the idea of using a lightweight graph structure with a relatively simple graph schema, to support the RAG subsystem via a dedicated toolset. We design an agentic system with a variety of vector search and graph query tools operating over a structured dataset based on a curated subset of English Wikipedia articles, and evaluate its performance on questions from MoNaCo, a challenging Wikipedia QA benchmark of complex query answering tasks.
  Our results show that the introduction of graph-based tools can significantly increase the precision and recall of factual correctness, can halve the number of hallucinated answers, and achieves the highest fine-grained truthfulness score among the three evaluated scenarios. All this with a modest increase in token usage.

</details>


### [174] [ACE-SQL: Adaptive Co-Optimization via Empirical Credit Assignment for Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05906)
*Xiaobing Chen,Ai Jian,Eryu Guo,Zhiqi Pang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ACE-SQL is a reinforcement learning framework for Text-to-SQL that improves schema linking and SQL generation by jointly optimizing retrieval and generation under execution feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for Text-to-SQL struggle with schema linking in large and complex databases, either relying on implicit full-schema generation or static gold-column supervision, which might not align with generator policies.

Method: ACE-SQL builds an online column-set pool from generator rollouts and determines adaptive on-policy retrieval targets based on execution feedback to induce mutual adaptation between retriever and generator.

Result: ACE-SQL achieves a greedy execution accuracy of 65.3% on BIRD Dev using approximately 0.93k output tokens per query, with RL training performed on around 3k synthetic question-database pairs.

Conclusion: This bidirectional adaptation strategy improves schema linking and execution accuracy, optimizing Text-to-SQL generation under execution feedback with practical results available on GitHub.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL maps natural language questions to executable SQL queries. Modern databases often contain large and complex schemas, making schema linking a critical step for accurate SQL generation. Existing methods either rely on full-schema generation, which leaves schema linking implicit within a large search space, or use a separate retriever trained with static gold-column supervision, whose targets may be suboptimal for the current generator policy. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Co-optimization via Empirical Credit Assignment for Text-to-SQL (ACE-SQL), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that jointly optimizes schema retrieval and SQL generation under execution feedback. ACE-SQL constructs an online column-set pool from generator rollouts and derives adaptive on-policy retrieval targets from the column set most frequently associated with execution-correct rollouts. This induces bidirectional adaptation, where the retriever adapts toward column sets that the generator can execute correctly, while the generator adapts to the retriever's evolving schema selections under execution feedback. With approximately 3k synthetic Text-to-SQL question-database pairs for RL training, ACE-SQL achieves 65.3% greedy execution accuracy on BIRD Dev while using 0.93k output tokens per query. The repository is available at https://github.com/xbchen1/ACE-SQL.

</details>


### [175] [Better Literary Translation: A Multi-Aspect Data Generation and LLM Training Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05924)
*Zhihao Lin,Ziqi Zhu,Hao Huang,Guanghui Wang,Peiyang He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for improving literary translation quality with large language models (LLMs), achieving state-of-the-art performance on metrics and generalizing well to unseen works.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality annotated data for literary translation is scarce, and there is a need to balance fluency and literary effect in translations.

Method: The authors propose a multi-aspect iterative refinement framework using specialized LLM translators. Quality data is generated for supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. Different training strategies (SFT, DPO, GRPO) are compared.

Result: Generated references improve SFT performance by 8.65 CEA100 points. Using GRPO in reinforcement learning adds a further 1.51-point improvement, and resulting models LitMT-8B and LitMT-14B significantly outperform benchmarks.

Conclusion: The framework produces high-quality models for literary translation, performing competitively with Claude Sonnet 4.5 and generalizing effectively to out-of-domain texts.

Abstract: Literary translation poses unique challenges due to the scarcity of high-quality annotated data and the need to balance expression fluency with literary effect. We present a multi-aspect iterative refinement framework that generates high-quality translation references and preference data through specialized LLM translators, each targeting a distinct quality dimension. We leverage the generated data for supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. Experiments show that our generated references outperform the original ground truth for SFT by 8.65 CEA100 points. For reinforcement learning, we find that DPO leads to performance degradation in this setting, while leveraging an explicit reward model for GRPO yields an additional 1.51 point improvement. We attribute this to the stability of two-stage training and GRPO's online exploration capability. Our resulting models, LitMT-8B and LitMT-14B, achieve 67.25 and 69.07 CEA100 respectively on the MetaphorTrans English-to-Chinese literary translation benchmark, competitive with Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 68.43, and demonstrate strong generalization to out-of-domain literary work (i.e., O. Henry).

</details>


### [176] [To Be Multimodal or Not to Be: Query-Adaptive Audio-Visual Person Retrieval via Active Modality Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05931)
*Erfan Loweimi,Mengjie Qian,Kate Knill,Guanfeng Wu,Chi-Ho Chan,Abbas Haider,Muhammad Awan,Josef Kittler,Hui Wang,Mark Gales*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a multimodal retrieval system that adapts to the availability of voice and face data, achieving superior performance over unimodal and fixed fusion alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in multimodal person retrieval from real-world video archives, where one modality (face or voice) may be absent or inconsistent, affecting retrieval precision.

Method: The method involves a query-adaptive framework that detects active modalities using cross-modal score consistency. It uses classifiers based on cross-modal features to determine the active modalities and adjust retrieval accordingly.
