Result: The proposed system achieved 89% accuracy in detecting active modalities and 94.2% P@1 on the BBC Rewind dataset, surpassing speaker-only, face-only, and fixed fusion systems by a significant margin.

Conclusion: The study concludes that adaptive fusion based on active modality detection outperforms traditional fixed multimodal systems, bridging the performance gap to an oracle system with ground-truth labels.

Abstract: When retrieving a person from a video archive by voice and face, should the system be multimodal or not? In real-world broadcast archives, unlike curated benchmarks, a target may be heard but unseen, seen but unheard, or both. Fusing scores from an absent modality injects noise, degrading precision below the best unimodal system. We propose a query-adaptive framework that detects active modalities via cross-modal score consistency: when both modalities are active, files retrieved by one also score highly on the other; this agreement breaks down when a modality is absent. Classifiers driven by these cross-modal features achieve 89% detection accuracy. On the BBC Rewind corpus (with over 12,000 broadcast videos) the adaptive system attains 94.2% P@1, outperforming speaker-only (82.9%), face-only (93.4%), and fixed fusion (90.0%), recovering 64% of the gap to an oracle with ground-truth modality labels (96.6%).

</details>


### [177] [Epistemic Injustice in Language Models: An Audit of Pretraining Filters and Guardrails](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05936)
*Marco Antonio Stranisci,A Pranav,Rossana Damiano,Christian Hardmeier,Anne Lauscher*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies the unintended negative consequences of language model content filtering, particularly its disproportionate suppression of marginalized groups' mentions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how pretraining filters and inference-time guardrails contribute to epistemic erasure and to question the balance between automated and human judgment in moderating content.

Method: The authors audited four pretraining filters and three inference-time guardrails, focusing on Common Crawl sentences with gender and regional-origin mentions, augmented by a manually annotated subset of 500 sentences.

Result: They found that filtering is often triggered by lexical cues and misses genuinely harmful content. Marginalized groups are over-flagged, while human annotators would retain most flagged content.

Conclusion: Filtering and moderation in language models contribute to epistemic erasure by disproportionately suppressing mentions of marginalized groups, highlighting the limitations of current automated systems.

Abstract: Modern language models rely on pretraining filters to remove undesirable content from training corpora and inference-time guardrails to suppress undesirable outputs during deployment. In this paper, we examine how these filtering and moderation decisions produce forms of epistemic erasure and reveal tensions both across automated systems and between these systems and human judgment. We audit four pretraining filters and three inference-time guardrails on Common Crawl sentences containing gender and regional-origin mentions, together with a manually annotated subset of 500 sentences. Our analysis shows that filtering and guardrail decisions are strongly associated with blocklist-based lexical cues, while frequently failing to flag content containing private information or explicit hate speech. At the same time, marginalized groups, particularly transgender people, women, and Central Americans, are significantly over-flagged across systems. Human annotators, by contrast, would retain 88.5\% of filter-flagged and 91.3\% of guardrail-flagged content, often recognizing representational harms arising from tensions of content removal that current systems fail to capture. Taken together, our findings document a form of epistemic erasure in which mentions of marginalized groups are disproportionately removed before pretraining and additionally suppressed again at inference time.

</details>


### [178] [Large Language Models are Perplexed by some Political Parties](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05937)
*Paul Lerner,François Yvon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study evaluates the political fairness of large language models (LLMs) by analyzing their perplexity towards texts from various political groups, revealing biases against far-right and nationalist parties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to investigate the underexplored topic of political fairness in LLMs, especially when applied to sensitive domains like politics, where biased behavior might lead to unintended consequences.

Method: The authors assess political fairness using the measure of perplexity, proposing that fair models should assign equal probabilities to political texts from different groups. They applied their methodology across ten LLMs and three multilingual datasets covering 37 languages.

Result: The study discovered that LLMs exhibit higher levels of perplexity (higher difficulty) when processing texts from far-right and nationalist groups compared to social-democratic ones. Additionally, the findings align with previous research on translation fairness, and perplexity was shown to be correlated with downstream translation performance.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that political fairness issues in LLMs mainly stem from their pretraining phase, with little changes observed after instruction-tuning, signifying that biases are intrinsic to the models' initial data exposure.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used, including in political applications, but their political fairness has been little studied. We assess it using perplexity, posing that a fair model should give equal probability to all political groups. However, we find, across ten LLMs and three datasets covering 37 languages, that LLMs are more perplexed by the texts of far right and nationalist parties than of social-democratic parties. We find this to be consistent with previous work on translation fairness, to the point that perplexity correlates with downstream translation metrics. Our method is applicable to both base LLMs as well as their instruction-tuned counterpart, and we find that both are highly correlated, suggesting that the political fairness of LLMs stems from their pretraining, and is hardly affected by instruction-tuning.

</details>


### [179] [Measuring the sensitivity of LLM-based structured extraction to prompt, model, and schema choices in clinical discharge summaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05970)
*Martin Murin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study analyzes the effects of prompt and model choices on clinical text extraction tasks, using large language models without relying on human annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the sensitivity of clinical text extraction outputs from large language models, influenced by upstream choices instead of fixed benchmark accuracy.

Method: The authors tested three prompt variations across two model sizes, evaluating agreement for 17 clinical flags and 47 admission tags using Cohen's kappa on MIMIC-IV discharge summaries.

Result: Prompt phrasing led to schema-related disagreements, whereas model choice caused noticeable differences in multi-class categorization, affecting dominant tag assignments.

Conclusion: Schema design influences disagreement patterns, and models affect multi-class categorizations more than prompt phrasing, providing insights into reproducibility in large-scale deployments.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used for structured extraction from clinical free-text notes, but the sensitivity of their output to upstream configuration choices is less understood than their accuracy on fixed benchmarks. This work measures that sensitivity without human-annotated ground truth, by holding the extraction task fixed and varying one choice at a time. The fixed schema comprises 17 clinical documentation flags on a three-way yes/no/not_documented value set and a 47-tag vocabulary for the primary admission reason. Three prompt variants expressing this schema were each run at two model sizes on MIMIC-IV v3.1 discharge summaries. Cross-prompt agreement was measured by Cohen's kappa on ICD-stratified subsets. A paired same-note comparison isolated the effect of model choice, and a post-hoc collapse of the three-way flags to binary tested the schema's contribution to disagreement. On the three-way flags, the two models reach the same pooled cross-prompt agreement (median kappa 0.69 and 0.68); the larger model raises agreement on some fields and lowers it on others, a redistribution rather than the absence of an effect. Collapsing the schema to binary dissolves most of the cross-prompt disagreement, locating it on the absence-versus-silence distinction rather than on whether the finding is present. On the multi-class admission categorization, changing the model reassigns the dominant tag on close to half of all notes while changing the prompt phrasing reassigns it on roughly one in eight, and the larger model places far less mass on residual catch-all categories (44% to 26%). These patterns indicate a schema-imposed source of disagreement concentrated on the absence-versus-silence axis and a dominance of model over prompt phrasing on multi-class categorization, identified by a reusable methodology for auditing extraction reproducibility on a population-scale deployment.

</details>


### [180] [Beyond Alignment: Value Diversity as a Collective Property in Multicultural Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05985)
*Shaoyang Xu,Jingshen Zhang,Long P. Hoang,Jinyuan Li,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces 'value diversity' as a metric to assess diversity in multicultural multi-agent systems based on the dissimilarity of culturally conditioned agents' responses, finding that current systems lack sufficient diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly used in diverse global contexts, but current evaluations focus only on value alignment, neglecting system-level diversity which is critical for accurately representing multiple cultures.

Method: The authors define and measure value diversity, using responses from the World Values Survey across 19 cultures and 18 backend models, and analyze the effects of social interaction and mixed-backbone configurations on cultural diversity.

Result: The study reveals that alignment and diversity are largely uncorrelated, existing systems exhibit lower diversity compared to human societies, and social interactions further erode diversity, limiting collective decision-making breadth.

Conclusion: Value diversity provides a new and important angle for evaluating multicultural agent systems, demonstrating that current systems face a persistent homogenization issue that needs addressing in future designs.

Abstract: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed in globally diverse settings, where different agents are grounded in different cultural backgrounds. Existing cultural evaluation focuses on value alignment: how closely a single agent matches a target culture. Yet alignment is a per-agent property and cannot reveal whether a system, taken as a whole, preserves the cultural plurality it is meant to represent. We propose value diversity as a system-level evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems, defined through the dissimilarity between culturally conditioned agents' responses on a shared value survey. Using the World Values Survey, we evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models across a wide range of system configurations. We find that diversity is largely uncorrelated with alignment, indicating that the two capture complementary system properties, and that current multicultural agent systems fall substantially below human societies in value diversity. Mixed-backbone systems narrow this gap but do not close it, and the gap persists across culture compositions and agent scales. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving agents toward consensus, and a participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization narrows the breadth of collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as a distinct evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems and reveal a persistent homogenization tendency in current LLM-based societies. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/iNLP-Lab/MultiAgent-Diversity.

</details>


### [181] [The Generator-Eraser Paradox: Community Guidelines for Responsible LLM-Assisted Dialect Resource Creation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06004)
*Wajdi Zaghouani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores the dual role of large language models (LLMs) in assisting the development of dialect resources while potentially leading to dialect erasure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how LLMs can support dialect resource development while addressing the risks of linguistic homogenization and community marginalization.

Method: The authors create a theoretical framework called the 'generator-eraser paradox,' derive 12 community guidelines for dialect resource creation, and use Arabic dialects as a case study to operationalize their approach.

Result: The paper provides actionable design guidelines and a detailed examination of Arabic dialect issues to balance the benefits and risks of using LLMs in dialect work.

Conclusion: LLMs can be utilized to develop dialect resources responsibly if guided by conscious efforts to preserve linguistic diversity and community sovereignty, following proposed guidelines.

Abstract: Dialect resources occupy a unique position at the intersection of scientific description, cultural preservation, and computational infrastructure. Large language models offer powerful capabilities for accelerating dialect resource development through retrieval-grounded drafting, corpus navigation, metadata enrichment, and annotation workflow support. However, the same systems pose substantial risks: they can contribute to dialect erasure by privileging prestige varieties, homogenizing orthography, and enabling synthetic feedback loops that reduce linguistic diversity over time. These risks are particularly acute for language varieties characterized by diglossia, limited written standardization, or marginalized speaker communities. This paper makes three contributions. First, we integrate insights from variationist sociolinguistics and corpus linguistics to formalize the generator-eraser paradox as a theoretical framework for understanding the dual nature of LLM-assisted dialect work. Second, we derive 12 community guidelines that operationalize this framework into implementable design requirements for dialect resource creation and documentation. Third, we provide an in-depth case study of Arabic dialects, including a structured comparison of widely used resources, to demonstrate how these guidelines address language-specific challenges including diglossia, orthographic variability, and community governance. The contribution is conceptual and operational rather than experimental, with the goal of enabling dialect communities and resource builders across languages to adopt LLMs without sacrificing authenticity, variation, or sovereignty.

</details>


### [182] [Contextualized Prompting For Stance Detection On Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06022)
*Tilman Beck,Shakib Yazdani,Simon Kruschinski,Marcus Maurer,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores how incorporating different types of contextual information into large language models (LLMs) affects zero-shot stance detection on social media, finding that context improves performance under limited conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stance detection on social media is difficult due to the brevity and noise in the language used, and existing approaches with LLMs are limited by the lack of contextual input during prompting.

Method: The study investigates the use of real-world, derived, and LLM-generated contextual features in prompts for zero-shot stance detection across four datasets, including a new high-quality German Twitter dataset. Effects of incorporating various types of contextual information are systematically evaluated.

Result: Results show that LLM-generated contextual information, such as target descriptions, consistently improves performance, while other types of user metadata have mixed or negative effects. Notably, including other tweets by the same user often degrades performance.

Conclusion: Incorporating contextual data into LLM prompting for stance detection offers potential but also introduces challenges, such as LLMs’ difficulty in filtering irrelevant context. When appropriately used, context improves zero-shot generalization for specific tasks.

Abstract: Stance detection on social media is challenging due to short, noisy, and context-dependent language. While large language models (LLMs) show zero-shot generalization, they are typically prompted without contextual information, which limits their ability to interpret ambiguous posts. In this work, we systematically investigate the impact of incorporating real-world (e.g., user biographies), derived (e.g., political party), and LLM-generated (e.g., target descriptions) contextual features into zero-shot prompting for stance detection on Twitter. Our evaluation spans four benchmark datasets, including a new high-quality German Twitter stance dataset. Across multiple LLMs, we find that integrating contextual information improves performance, but only under specific conditions. LLM-generated target descriptions consistently enhance accuracy, while other user metadata has mixed or even detrimental effects. Notably, we show that the inclusion of other tweets by the same user, often beneficial in supervised learning, can impair performance due to input noise. Our qualitative analysis reveals that LLMs struggle to distinguish task-specific useful information from irrelevant context. Our findings highlight both the promise and challenges of prompting with context information in noisy real-world settings. We publish code and data at this \href{https://github.com/tilmanbeck/stance-context-twitter}{page}.

</details>


### [183] [EGTR-Review: Efficient Evidence-Grounded Scientific Peer Review Generation via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06025)
*Xinpeng Qiu,Wang Yihu,Zhifeng Liu,Xiaochen Wang,Jimin Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EGTR-Review is a framework designed to generate evidence-based and traceable scientific paper reviews, leveraging a multi-agent teacher model and distilling knowledge into a lightweight student model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for automated peer review either generate generic comments or are computationally expensive. There is a need for models that can produce high-quality, evidence-supported reviews with better efficiency.

Method: The proposed framework uses a multi-agent teacher model that performs structured paper analysis, evidence-based reasoning, and review synthesis. A lightweight student model is derived using task-prefix multi-task learning and an evidence-weighted objective during training.

Result: Experiments show that EGTR-Review outperforms existing methods in terms of quality, factual accuracy, and source traceability, while being computationally efficient with lower token usage and inference time.

Conclusion: EGTR-Review improves peer review quality while reducing computational costs, effectively balancing evidence grounding, reasoning capability, and efficiency. It provides a practical and scalable solution for automated review generation.

Abstract: Scientific peer review generation has attracted increasing attention for reducing reviewing burdens and providing timely feedback. However, existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based methods often produce generic comments with insufficient evidence support and weak source traceability, while complex multi-agent systems incur high inference costs. To address these challenges, we propose EGTR-Review, an Evidence-Grounded and Traceable Review Generation framework via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation. EGTR-Review first constructs a multi-agent teacher that performs structure-aware paper decomposition, key-element extraction, external scholarly evidence retrieval, evidence-state labeling, verification reasoning, and review synthesis. It then distills both intermediate reasoning trajectories and final review comments into a lightweight student model through task-prefix-driven multi-task learning. An evidence-weighted objective further reduces the influence of weak, missing, or non-verifiable supervision. Experiments on public peer-review datasets show that EGTR-Review (Student) outperforms strong prompt-based, fine-tuned, and structured/agentic baselines across automatic metrics, LLM-as-Judge evaluation, and human evaluation, while maintaining strong factual grounding and source traceability with substantially lower token consumption and inference time. Our code, prompts, configurations, and sample data are available on GitHub.

</details>


### [184] [NAVIRA: Decoupled Stochastic Remasking for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06031)
*Andrey Fomenko,Maksim Kryzhanovskiy,Svetlana Glazyrina,Roman Ischenko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NAVIRA modifies the inference process for masked diffusion language models to improve text quality by separating the evaluation and correction of low-quality tokens, incorporating stochastic remasking for better fluency and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the issue of error propagation in masked diffusion language models where dependency errors from early steps contaminate the context in later steps, leading to reduced text quality.

Method: The proposed model, NAVIRA, decouples token-quality scoring and regeneration into separate forward passes while introducing stochastic remasking. This approach uses temperature-controlled remasking to manage fluency and diversity.

Result: In experiments, NAVIRA enhanced fluency, maintained entropy, and outperformed in LLM-judge scores under larger forward-pass budgets compared to coupled approaches like PRISM.

Conclusion: The remasking policy plays a crucial role in masked-diffusion text generation, and decoupling key processes along with stochastic remasking can significantly enhance the generated text quality.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models generate text by iteratively unmasking many tokens in parallel, but this speed comes with a correction problem: tokens generated in the same step are predicted from marginal distributions, and early local dependency errors can later contaminate the context. PRISM addresses this by learning token-level quality scores and remasking unreliable tokens, but its inference rule is coupled: the same forward pass both detects low-quality tokens and computes logits for their replacements, so the erroneous tokens still condition regeneration. We propose NAVIRA, an inference-time decoding policy that separates these two operations and samples remasking positions stochastically. A first forward pass scores tokens; selected tokens are masked; a second forward pass regenerates from the cleaned context. Temperature-controlled remasking reduces repeated correction of the same positions and balances fluency against diversity. In controlled experiments with a 170M masked diffusion language model, decoupling improves fluency, while scheduled stochastic remasking preserves entropy and achieves stronger LLM-judge scores under larger forward-pass budgets. These results show that remasking policy, not only the learned quality signal, is central to reliable masked-diffusion text generation.

</details>


### [185] [English-to-Prakrit Machine Translation via Multilingual Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06038)
*Om Choksi,Smit Kareliya,Shrikant Malviya,Pruthwik Mishra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper focuses on English-to-Prakrit machine translation using a transfer approach to adapt IndicTrans2 for an unsupported classical language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable machine translation for Prakrit, a low-resource and unsupported classical language, using minimal adaptation techniques.

Method: They map Prakrit to a Hindi language tag in a multilingual translation model without modifying the tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture, and utilize a small Prakrit parallel corpus.

Result: They achieve BLEU score improvements over an untuned baseline and demonstrate feasible transfer for Prakrit translation despite data scarcity and dialect mismatch.

Conclusion: The study shows the potential of script-compatible language routing for unsupported classical languages, while underscoring the challenges posed by limited data and dialect variations.

Abstract: We study English-to-Prakrit machine translation in a low-resource setting where the target language is unsupported by IndicTrans2. We adapt the multilingual model by mapping Prakrit to the Hindi language tag (hin_Deva) without modifying the tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture. Using a 1,474-pair Maharashtri Prakrit parallel corpus and evaluation on a 20-sample Ardhamagadhi test set, we report corpus BLEU improvements over an untuned baseline. The results indicate that script-compatible language routing can enable feasible transfer to unsupported classical languages, while highlighting limitations due to data scarcity and dialect mismatch. Our code and trained models are released to the public for further exploration https://github.com/D3v1s0m/indictrans2-prakrit-mt.

</details>


### [186] [IA-RAG: Interval-Algebra-Driven Temporal Reasoning for Dynamic Knowledge Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06044)
*Xiaoman Wang,Yaoze Zhang,Wenzhuo Fan,Hongwei Zhang,Ding Wang,Guohang Yan,Song Mao,Botian Shi,Yunshi Lan,Pinlong Cai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IA-RAG enhances Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a hierarchical temporal approach, improving temporal retrieval and reasoning for complex tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG frameworks inadequately address the rich temporal structures inherent in knowledge, such as duration and overlap, limiting their ability to utilize time-dependent information effectively.

Method: The proposed IA-RAG represents knowledge as Interval Event Units (IEUs) organized into a hierarchical Thematic Forest using Allen's Interval Algebra. It introduces a Sub-graph Time Tightening mechanism to refine temporal boundaries and supports implicit temporal retrieval via interval-algebra-guided traversal.

Result: IA-RAG outperforms previous RAG frameworks on multiple temporal question answering benchmarks, particularly excelling in complex compositional temporal reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: By modeling knowledge through interval-based temporal structures and formal constraints, IA-RAG provides a robust framework for temporal retrieval and reasoning, advancing RAG's capabilities for time-sensitive applications.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown strong effectiveness in grounding Large Language Models (LLMs) with external knowledge. However, existing RAG and Graph RAG frameworks largely treat knowledge as static or associate time with coarse-grained timestamps or metadata, failing to capture rich temporal structures such as duration, overlap, and containment. We propose IA-RAG, a hierarchical temporal RAG framework that models knowledge as time intervals and performs retrieval under formal temporal constraints. IA-RAG represents facts as Interval Event Units (IEUs) and organizes them into a hierarchical Thematic Forest, where temporal dependencies are governed by Allen's Interval Algebra. To handle incomplete or uncertain temporal boundaries, IA-RAG further introduces a Sub-graph Time Tightening mechanism that refines fuzzy intervals through logical constraints within connected event subgraphs. In addition, IA-RAG supports implicit temporal semantic retrieval through interval-algebra-guided traversal. Experiments on multiple temporal question answering benchmarks, including TimeQA, TempReason, and ComplexTR, demonstrate that IA-RAG achieves strong temporal retrieval and reasoning performance, particularly on complex compositional temporal reasoning tasks. Our code is released at https://github.com/xiaoAugenstern/LogicalRAG_TemporalQA.

</details>


### [187] [Automatic Labelling of Speech Translation Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06047)
*Dominik Macháček,Maike Züfle,Ondrej Klejch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper highlights the lack of standard methods for evaluating errors in Speech Translation (ST) systems and introduces Speech Translation Error Labelling (STEL) to address this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Speech Translation systems lack an established methodology to evaluate errors, particularly confidence and quality estimation, which affects their trustworthiness and practical usability.

Method: The authors propose an annotation protocol called Speech Translation Error Labelling (STEL), develop a small evaluation dataset, and analyze existing text-only and speech-processing systems on the STEL task.

Result: Results show that text-only systems like XCOMET and multimodal LLM systems like Qwen2.5-Omni achieve approximately half the precision of human performance, with direct speech processing being necessary and current systems complementing each other in handling specific error types.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that while existing systems show some capacity for error labeling in ST, improvements in direct speech analysis and a unified approach are needed to advance error detection frameworks.

Abstract: Errors in speech translations reduce trustworthiness of Speech Translation (ST) systems and can have serious consequences. Yet currently there is no established methodology for evaluating confidence and quality estimation of speech translations. To initiate progress in this direction, we propose Speech Translation Error Labelling (STEL). We create an annotation protocol, a small authentic end-to-end evaluation dataset, and we analyse how existing text-only and speech-processing systems perform the STEL task. Our results show that text-only XCOMET and multimodal LLM Qwen2.5-Omni are able to perform the STEL task in roughly half the precision of humans. We also find that direct speech processing is necessary for the STEL task, and that the current text-only and speech-processing systems are complementary in labelling translation-only vs. speech-processing errors in ST.

</details>


### [188] [Multi-task Learning is Not Enough: Representational Entanglement in Dual-output Second Language Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06065)
*Seung Hwan Cho,Young-Min Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates the application of multi-task learning (MTL) for L2 speech recognition, finding that shared representations across tasks do not uniformly benefit both transcription and meaning, especially for English.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the effectiveness and limitations of multi-task learning (MTL) in second-language (L2) speech recognition, specifically its impact on accurate transcription and intended meaning capture across diverse languages like Korean and English.

Method: The paper uses multi-task learning frameworks for surface transcription and intended meaning decoding in L2 English and Korean speech data. Encoder and decoder analyses are conducted to identify task-specific representation entanglements and adaptation behaviors.

Result: MTL improves intended meaning recognition but degrades transcription accuracy, particularly in English. The degradation is linked to surface-meaning divergence and encoder-level entanglement, with English exhibiting more task interaction at the encoder level than Korean.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the need for improved MTL frameworks for L2 speech recognition to mitigate encoder-level entanglement, thereby balancing transcription and meaning decoding, particularly for languages with high surface-meaning divergence.

Abstract: Second-language (L2) speech recognition often requires transcriptions of pronunciations and intended meanings. Multi-task learning (MTL) is a natural approach because it assumes that shared representations benefit both outputs. However, this paper shows that this assumption does not hold across Korean and English. MTL improves meaning but degrades surface transcription, especially in English, where the degradation scales with surface-meaning divergence measured by Levenshtein edit distance.Encoder analysis links these patterns to encoder-level entanglement, with Korean preserving distinct task representations while English produces nearly identical ones. Cross-task decoder analysis shows that the meaning dual-output decoder adapts with a unique representation, while the surface dual-output decoder remains constrained by the encoder. These findings motivate the design of MTL frameworks that mitigate encoder-level entanglement to reduce surface degradation in dual-output L2 automatic speech recognition.

</details>


### [189] [SkillComposer: Learning to Evolve Agent Skills for Specification and Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06079)
*Qi Zhang,Zhaopeng Feng,Xiaonan Shi,Xiaomeng Hu,Chu Liu,Pengjun Xie,Xiaobin Wang,Jieping Ye,Bryan Hooi,Haobo Wang,Junbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillComposer, a framework for dynamically constructing and enhancing agent skills, showing significant performance improvements over baselines in various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for constructing agent skills lack mechanisms for balancing task specificity and generalizability, hindering transferability and guidance during inference.

Method: SkillComposer employs three learnable operations—create, improve, and merge—trained through systematic rejection sampling, enabling dynamic skill evolution and supporting offline, online, and hybrid deployment modes.

Result: SkillComposer outperforms baselines in tasks on $τ^2$-Bench, LiveCodeBench v6, and AppWorld, achieving improvements of up to +4.5 on agent tasks and +3.4 on code tasks, while generalizing to unseen domains and tasks.

Conclusion: SkillComposer demonstrates that skill composition is a transferable meta-ability, addressing quality dimensions and providing a practical framework for enhancing model inference capabilities.

Abstract: Agent skills, which consist of reusable strategies that guide agent reasoning and action, have shown strong potential for improving model capability at inference time. However, current skill construction methods treat the problem as one-shot extraction, overlooking a fundamental tension: a skill tailored to the specific task fails to transfer, while the abstracted skill often provides insufficient guidance. We attribute this fragility to the absence of explicit mechanisms for skill specification and generalization. To address this gap, we introduce SkillComposer, a framework that decomposes skill construction into three learnable operations: create, improve, and merge. Trained via systematic rejection sampling recipe, SkillComposer enables language models to self-evolve skills at inference time and supports three deployment modes: offline for building generalized libraries, online for task-specific refinement, and hybrid for combining both. Comprehensive experiments on $τ^2$-Bench, LiveCodeBench v6, and AppWorld show that SkillComposer consistently outperforms baselines. Our SkillComposer-4B improves a 27B executor by up to +4.5 on agent tasks and +3.4 on code tasks, while generalizing across domains and task types unseen during training. Analysis reveals that merge and improve address orthogonal quality dimensions and that skill composition is a transferable meta-ability, providing a practical recipe for skill-augmented inference.

</details>


### [190] [LatentSkill: From In-Context Textual Skills to In-Weight Latent Skills for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06087)
*Aofan Yu,Chenyu Zhou,Tianyi Xu,Zihan Guo,Rong Shan,Zhihui Fu,Jun Wang,Weiwen Liu,Yong Yu,Weinan Zhang,Jianghao Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LatentSkill introduces a method to convert textual skills into LoRA adapters, reducing context overhead while maintaining modular functionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies of using textual skills for task procedures, like high context overhead and plaintext exposure in prompts.

Method: Utilizing pretrained hypernetworks to convert textual skills into plug-and-play LoRA adapters, allowing storage in weight space rather than context space.

Result: Improved performance compared to in-context baselines (e.g., 21.4-point success increase on ALFWorld with 64.1% fewer tokens, 3-point exact match improvement on Search-QA with 72.2% less token overhead).

Conclusion: Weight-space skill adaptations enhance efficiency, modularity, and security in extending LLM agent capabilities.

Abstract: Agent systems increasingly use textual skills to encode reusable task procedures, but injecting these skills into the prompt at every step incurs substantial context overhead and exposes skill content as plaintext. We present LatentSkill, a framework that converts textual skills into plug-and-play LoRA adapters through a pretrained hypernetwork. LatentSkill stores skill knowledge in weight space rather than context space, removing per-step skill tokens while preserving modular loading, scaling, and composition. On ALFWorld and Search-QA, LatentSkill outperforms the corresponding in-context skill baseline while using substantially fewer prefill tokens: it improves ALFWorld success by 21.4 and 13.4 points on the seen and unseen splits with 64.1% fewer prefill tokens, and improves Search-QA exact match by 3.0 points with 72.2% lower skill-token overhead. Further analysis shows that generated skill LoRAs form a structured semantic geometry, can be precisely controlled via the LoRA scaling coefficient, and can be composed through parameter-space arithmetic when skill components are aligned. These findings suggest that weight-space skills provide an efficient, modular, and less exposed substrate for extending LLM agents.

</details>


### [191] [CHALIS: A Challenge Dataset for Language Identification in Difficult Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06088)
*Michal Tichý,Jindřich Libovický*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CHALIS, a dataset for testing challenging cases in language identification, focusing on cousin languages and orthographic noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address limitations of existing language identification systems in handling similar languages and text distortions, such as transliteration and slang.

Method: Authors created a benchmark dataset with two components: sentences from mutually intelligible language pairs and text with orthographic distortions like diacritic removal and homoglyph attacks. They evaluated four language ID systems on this dataset.

Result: All language ID systems tested struggled notably with cousin languages and transliterated input, particularly for lower-resource languages.

Conclusion: CHALIS highlights significant gaps in current language identification models and provides a publicly available dataset for addressing these challenges.

Abstract: We present CHALIS (Challenging Language Identification Samples), a new benchmark dataset explicitly designed to address difficult cases in language identification: cousin languages and orthographic noise. Our dataset has two parts: First, we collected sentences shared across mutually intelligible language pairs (Czech/Slovak, Spanish/Catalan, Portuguese/Galician, Danish/Norwegian). The second part tests for orthography noise: we transliterate text across multiple scripts, remove diacritics, simulate homoglyph attacks, and use Internet slang. We evaluate four widely used language identification systems on CHALIS and demonstrate that all struggle substantially in these scenarios, especially on lower-resource languages within cousin pairs and on transliterated input. The resource is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/michal-tichy/CHALIS.

</details>


### [192] [IR3DE: A Linear Router for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06098)
*Eros Fanì,Oğuzhan Ersoy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IR3DE, a Ridge Regression-based router for efficiently selecting domain-expert large language models (LLMs) for specific tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The proliferation of LLMs and the need for efficient selection mechanisms for task-specific prompts led to this research. Existing methods are either cost-focused or require substantial retraining, limiting their effectiveness.

Method: The authors propose IR3DE, a linear router that uses Ridge Regression to assign prompts to the most appropriate domain-expert LLM efficiently and with minimal computational overhead.

Result: IR3DE demonstrated competitive performance in causal language modeling tasks and surpassed benchmarks in reasoning tasks with a performance of 98.4%. Additionally, it allows for dynamic addition or removal of LLMs without retraining the router.

Conclusion: IR3DE offers a cost-effective and adaptable approach to routing prompts among domain-expert LLMs, making it a practical solution for managing diverse and evolving LLM environments.

Abstract: Foundational Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate proficiency on a wide range of general tasks, and achieve remarkable results on various specialized tasks via domain-expert LLMs. With the ever-growing list of available LLMs, inference routers are being proposed to select the most appropriate LLM for each prompt. However, existing routing methods either optimize cost across weak-to-strong generalist LLMs or require substantial training to support domain-expertise routing. In this paper, we propose IR3DE, a Ridge Regression-based Router for Domain Experts that provides cheap and fast routing decisions for each prompt. We evaluate IR3DE in two Causal Language Modeling (CLM) settings where the tasks are next-token prediction for all domains, and one reasoning setting where each domain has its own distinct reasoning task. Despite being a linear router, IR3DE achieves performance comparable to the other baselines in both CLM settings, and surpassing them in the reasoning setting, with a normalized performance of 98.4%. Moreover, IR3DE enables the addition or removal of new domain experts without requiring the router to be retrained from scratch, allowing a dynamic set of LLMs to be served with minimal disruption to the router itself. Our code is available at: github.com/gensyn-ai/IR3DE.

</details>


### [193] [Harnessing Structural Context for Entity Alignment Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06109)
*Xingyu Chen,Yuanning Cui,Zequn Sun,Wei Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ContextEA, a transferable entity alignment (EA) framework enhancing structural context usage in heterogeneous knowledge graphs (KGs), showing superior performance on 29 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve entity alignment across heterogeneous KGs by addressing limitations in structural context usage within existing models.

Method: ContextEA employs a cross-KG interaction encoder for early relation-aware propagation and a structural calibration decoder to refine alignment scores using multiple structural evidences.

Result: Experiments on 29 datasets show consistent improvements over existing methods, with ContextEA outperforming finetuned baselines even when pretrained.

Conclusion: Explicitly leveraging structural context improves entity alignment models, and ContextEA offers a more robust and transferable approach to EA tasks.

Abstract: Entity alignment (EA) aims to identify equivalent entities across heterogeneous knowledge graphs (KGs) and is a key component of knowledge fusion and cross-KG reasoning. The recent EA foundation model demonstrates that alignment knowledge, once pretrained, can be directly applied to diverse previously unseen KG pairs. However, it still underuses structural context in two places: cross-KG interaction is weak during encoding, and final candidate ranking still relies too heavily on coarse similarity. We address these limitations with ContextEA, an enhanced encoder-decoder framework for transferable EA. On the encoder side, we introduce a cross-KG interaction encoder that unifies the two KGs with anchor bridges and performs earlier relation-aware cross-graph propagation. On the decoder side, we introduce a structural calibration decoder that calibrates alignment scores with entity-level, neighborhood-level, relation-level, and anchor-aware structural evidence. This design strengthens both structural context construction and structural context exploitation while remaining lightweight. Experiments on 29 EA datasets in OpenEA, SRPRS, and DBP show consistent gains over strong transferable baselines. Notably, the pretrained ContextEA already surpasses the finetuned baselines on all three benchmark groups, demonstrating substantially stronger transfer to unseen KGs. These results suggest that explicitly harnessing structural context is an effective direction for improving EA foundation models.

</details>


### [194] [Ouvia: A User-centered Framework for Measuring Usability of Speech Translation in Real-World Communication Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06177)
*Giuseppe Attanasio,Beatrice Savoldi,Daniel Chechelnitsky,Matteo Negri,Marine Carpuat,Maarten Sap,André F. T. Martins*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study proposes 'Ouvia,' a framework to evaluate speech translation (ST) systems based on usability in real-world, one-to-one communication scenarios. Findings indicate current systems only meet user needs around half the time, with usability disparities across demographic groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of speech translation systems focus on technical quality rather than understanding how well these systems meet real-world user communication needs.

Method: The 'Ouvia' framework evaluates ST usability through a web app and multi-phase study, involving 1,750 interactions in healthcare and daily contexts mediated by four ST systems, including diverse English dialects and genders.

Result: The study found that modern ST systems have limited usability, with usable interactions occurring in only about 50% of cases. Usability varies significantly among demographic groups, and QA-based metrics are better predictors of usability than traditional evaluation methods.

Conclusion: It emphasizes the need for user-centered evaluation frameworks that consider usability, real-world applicability, and demographic inclusivity instead of solely focusing on holistic quality scores.

Abstract: Speech translation (ST) is increasingly adopted in user applications, yet its evaluation largely focuses on decontextualized testbeds and holistic quality, rather than end users' communication needs. We introduce Ouvia, an evaluation framework for measuring user-perceived usability of speech translation outputs in real-world settings. Ouvia focuses on one-to-one communication: an English speaker needs to convey a request to a Portuguese speaker, and the message is automatically translated. Through a custom web app and multi-phase study design, we collect more than 1,750 such interactions in healthcare and everyday situations, mediated by four ST systems, involving speakers from three English dialects and two genders. We find that modern ST serves people only to a limited extent -- only around half of interactions are rated as usable -- with significant gaps in reported usability across demographic groups. Moreover, among quality metrics, we find that QA-based evaluation is a substantially stronger predictor of real-world usability than standard approaches. Together, these findings stress the importance of situated, user-centered evaluation frameworks that go beyond holistic quality scores and attend to who the technology serves -- and how well.

</details>


### [195] [The Tell-Tale Norm: $\ell_2$ Magnitude as a Signal for Reasoning Dynamics in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06188)
*Jinyang Zhang,Hongxin Ding,Yue Fang,Weibin Liao,Muyang Ye,Junfeng Zhao,Yasha Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores how the l2 norm of hidden states in large language models (LLMs) can serve as a signal of reasoning intensity, offering new methods to enhance reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify a model-intrinsic signal that provides insight into LLMs' layer-wise reasoning dynamics and use it for better understanding and control.

Method: This study links l2 norm of hidden states with reasoning intensity using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to analyze activation patterns. It also proposes test-time scaling techniques utilizing l2 norms to enhance reasoning without additional training.

Result: Empirical and theoretical validation shows that heightened l2 norms align with critical reasoning steps, with the proposed techniques significantly boosting performance across models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: The l2 norm is a reliable intrinsic signal for understanding and improving LLMs' reasoning dynamics, facilitating enhanced performance with minimal intervention.

Abstract: Recent work has sought to understand Large Language Models (LLMs) reasoning, yet a principled, model-intrinsic signal that captures its layer-wise reasoning dynamics remains underexplored. We bridge this gap by demonstrating that the l2 norm of hidden states serves as an endogenous signal of the model's reasoning intensity. Using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a diagnostic probe, we observe that LLMs' internal reasoning is marked by a sharp increase in reasoning feature activations concentrated in late layers. Motivated by this pattern, we establish a formal link between reasoning intensity and the model's latent geometry and theoretically prove that the l2 norm of hidden states bounds the activation strength of SAE reasoning features. Empirical correlation analysis and causal interventions further validate the l2 norm as a faithful indicator, where heightened norms consistently correspond to critical reasoning steps. We then introduce three test-time scaling techniques guided by l2 norms: (i) Adaptive Layer-wise Reasoning Recursion, (ii) Endogenous Reasoning State Steering, and (iii) l2-guided Response Selection, which requires no additional training or data and is compatible with advanced inference engines. Experiments across model architectures and benchmarks show that l2-norm-based techniques significantly improve reasoning performance, offering a principled yet simple lens to perceive and control LLM latent reasoning dynamics. Our code is available at https://github.com/zjy1298/The-Tell-Tale-Norm.

</details>


### [196] [Improving Answer Extraction in Context-based Question Answering Systems Using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06197)
*Hafez Abdelghaffar,Ahmed Alansary,Ali Hamdi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a fine-tuned Roberta-based QA system using SQuAD1.1, achieving improved answer precision and relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing QA systems, such as irrelevant or imprecise answers and poor generalization across domains.

Method: Fine-tuning a pre-trained Roberta-base LLM on the SQuAD1.1 dataset to improve contextual understanding and answer extraction.

Result: The fine-tuned model achieved strong performance metrics: ROUGE-L (86.84%), BLEU (28.24%), and BERTScore (95.38%).

Conclusion: The approach shows substantial improvements in QA system precision and reliability, validating the benefits of targeted fine-tuning.

Abstract: Question answering (QA) systems have achieved notable progress with the advent of large language models (LLMs). However, they still face challenges in accurately extracting and generating precise answers from given contexts, particularly when dealing with complex or ambiguous queries. Existing approaches often struggle with contextual understanding, answer consistency, and generalization across diverse domains. In this work, we propose a question answering system based on large language models, where the input consists of a textual context and a corresponding question, and the output is a concise and accurate answer. The motivation behind this research lies in addressing the limitations of current QA systems, particularly their tendency to produce irrelevant or imprecise responses despite having access to the correct context. Our methodology involves fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM on a benchmark QA dataset to improve its contextual comprehension and answer extraction capabilities. Specifically, we utilize the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD1.1), which provides high-quality context-question-answer triplets for supervised training and evaluation. Experimental results show that the fine-tuned Roberta-base model achieves the highest performance, attaining a ROUGE-L score of 86.84%, a BLEU score of 28.24%, and a BERTScore of 95.38%. These results indicate strong accuracy and answer relevance, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach for context-based question answering tasks. Furthermore, the findings confirm that targeted fine-tuning substantially improves the reliability and precision of QA systems.

</details>


### [197] [Dense Contexts Are Hard Contexts: Lexical Density Limits Effective Context in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06203)
*Giovanni Dettori,Matteo Boffa,Danilo Giordano,Idilio Drago,Marco Mellia*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Lexical density, often overlooked, notably impacts long-context performance in LLMs by reducing their effective processing capacity in dense contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the factors that degrade the long-context performance of LLMs, focusing on lexical density as a potentially overlooked contributor.

Method: Benchmarked various LLMs (9B-685B sizes) on three tasks with identical length (12k tokens) but varying lexical densities, systematically analyzing the impact of contextual density while controlling for other variables.

Result: Found a sharp performance decline in models when processing high-density contexts, with retrieval scores dropping below 60%, despite near-perfect performance in sparse contexts. Lowering density restored performance.

Conclusion: Lexical density directly reduces the effective context window of LLMs and has critical implications for their performance on complex, dense inputs in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Input length and the position of relevant information are widely cited as the primary causes of degraded LLM long-context performance. Here, we study lexical density -- the rate at which a context introduces distinct information -- as a third, largely overlooked factor that systematically reduces the effective context window of LLMs. We quantify the impact of lexical density on open-weight LLMs (9B-685B) using three "find-the-needle" style benchmarks with identical length (~12k tokens) and controlled needle position, but increasing density of information. We observe a sharp performance collapse in higher-density benchmarks: models that are near-perfect in sparse contexts drop below 60% retrieval score on denser ones. To rule out task-type confounds, we vary and control the density within each benchmark while keeping all other properties unchanged. Reducing density generally restores performance, especially in the high-density regimes where degradation appears. These results show that effective context capacity is a function of lexical density, with direct implications for real-world LLM systems operating on compact, information-rich inputs.

</details>


### [198] [FiLM-Based Speaker Conditioning of a SpeechLLM for Pathological Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06211)
*Fernando López,Santosh Kesiraju,Jordi Luque*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study examines speaker conditioning via FiLM in ASR systems to adapt to pathological speech, yielding competitive results without compromising performance on standard speech.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pathological speech from neurological conditions remains difficult for ASR systems, even though standard speech recognition has advanced significantly.

Method: The paper introduces speaker conditioning using Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) with x-vector-derived information applied to a frozen ASR encoder's transformer layers, benchmarking against fine-tuning baselines on pathological speech in two languages.

Result: Speaker-conditioned ASR demonstrated competitive performance compared to established adaptation strategies while maintaining performance for non-conditioned speech.

Conclusion: Injecting speaker-specific information through FiLM effectively adapts ASR systems to pathological speech with a parameter-efficient method, preserving the system's overall utility.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) has advanced remarkably for standard speech; however, pathological speech from neurological conditions remains a significant challenge. We investigate speaker conditioning via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM), injecting x-vector-derived information into each transformer layer of a frozen ASR encoder to adapt internal representations to individual pathological speakers without modifying base model weights. We benchmark this for the ASR task against standard and parameter-efficient fine-tuning baselines, complemented by post-processing, on Spanish and English pathological speech. Additionally, we evaluate if the adapted model preserves the ability to answer speech-related questions. Results show that speaker-conditioned ASR is competitive with established adaptation strategies while retaining performance on non-conditioned speech.

</details>


### [199] [Benchmarking Open-Source Layout Detection Models for Data Snapshot Extraction from Institutional Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06242)
*AJ Carl P. Dy,Aivin V. Solatorio*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a benchmark and evaluation framework for extracting analytical content from institutional documents, revealing performance challenges of current models and providing data and tools for future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current document layout analysis approaches that fail to properly identify and localize analytically meaningful content (figures and tables) in institutional documents.

Method: The authors created a benchmark dataset of institutional documents with annotated figures and tables, evaluated open-source layout detection models on both detection and spatial extraction tasks, and identified common failure modes.

Result: The results demonstrate that existing models perform well on academic benchmarks but struggle with operational institutional documents, often misclassifying analytical content, fragmenting composite artifacts, or failing to extract required contextual information.

Conclusion: The study underscores the need for improved approaches tailored to operationally useful content extraction and provides a released dataset and tools for advancing research in this area.

Abstract: Institutional documents contain substantial amounts of operational and analytical information embedded within figures and tables. Current approaches for extracting visual content from documents are largely built around generic document layout analysis, where figures and tables are treated as uniformly relevant document objects rather than semantically meaningful analytical artifacts. In this work, we introduce a benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for \textit{data snapshot extraction}, the task of identifying and localizing semantically meaningful visual artifacts within institutional documents. The benchmark spans humanitarian reports, World Bank policy research working papers, and project appraisal documents, and includes annotations for figures and tables that contain reusable analytical information. Using this dataset, we benchmarked multiple open-source layout detection models and evaluated both detection performance and spatial extraction quality. Our results show that current models struggle to generalize to operational institutional documents despite strong performance on conventional academic benchmarks. Common failure modes include confusion between analytical and non-analytical content, fragmentation of composite analytical artifacts, and incomplete extraction of contextual information required for interpretation. These findings highlight a persistent gap between generic document layout analysis and operationally useful data snapshot extraction. We release the source PDFs, annotation dataset, metadata, and source code to support future research in operational document intelligence. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ai4data/data-snapshot and the source code is available at https://github.com/worldbank/ai4data/tree/main/experimental/data-snapshot.

</details>


### [200] [From Self to Other: Evaluating Demographic Perspective-Taking in LLM Hate Speech Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06266)
*Paloma Piot,Javier Parapar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether persona-conditioned large language models can effectively simulate the subjective nature of hate speech detection across demographic groups. Results show inconsistency but highlight Llama 3.1 as the most reliable for approximating human disagreements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of accurately detecting hate speech, given its subjective interpretation across diverse demographic groups, in an efficient and scalable manner.

Method: The authors evaluate persona-conditioned language models on three human social judgment aspects: inter-group disagreement, in-group sensitivity, and vicarious prediction. They compare model performance, particularly focusing on the effectiveness of minimal identity prompts and vicarious prompting.

Result: The study finds that no model consistently succeeds in all three evaluation dimensions. However, Llama 3.1, using vicarious prompting, demonstrates the highest agreement with human disagreement patterns across demographic groups.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that persona-conditioned language models, despite their promise, remain inconsistent, but vicarious prompting with Llama 3.1 offers a promising direction for replicating human-aligned annotation of subjective judgments in hate speech detection.

Abstract: Hate speech detection is inherently subjective: people from different demographic groups perceive the same content very differently. Collecting enough annotations from multiple demographic groups is costly and difficult to scale. Persona-conditioned Large Language Models (models prompted to adopt a specific demographic identity) have been proposed as a way to simulate diverse perspectives at scale. But do they actually reflect how different groups disagree? We evaluate three aspects of human social judgement: (i) whether personas from different groups disagree in human-like ways (inter-group disagreement), (ii) whether they become more sensitive when content targets their own identity (in-group sensitivity), and (iii) whether they can accurately predict how another group would react (vicarious prediction). Our results show that no model consistently captures all three dimensions, and performance is highly model-dependent and does not emerge reliably from minimal identity prompts alone. However, vicarious prompting with Llama 3.1 yields the highest cross-group agreement in most demographic axes and provides the closest overall approximation to human disagreement patterns, indicating that this configuration may provide a more reliable setting for automatic annotation aligned with human judgements.

</details>


### [201] [Many Circuits, One Mechanism: Input Variation and Evaluation Granularity in Circuit Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06267)
*Alireza Bayat Makou,Jingcheng Niu,Subhabrata Dutta,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies that structural differences in discovered circuits do not necessarily indicate functional differences, a concept termed "phantom specialization."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the assumption that structural differences in circuit discovery correspond to distinct mechanisms and to investigate this phenomenon systematically.

Method: The authors conducted experiments using Literal Sequence Copying across token-frequency bands in five Pythia models, extracting 75 circuits and testing their interchangeability, core functionality sharing, and edge-level evaluation.

Result: Structurally different circuits were shown to implement the same computation, with structural specialization not leading to functional difference. Evaluation also showed standard methods obscure the many-to-one mapping from structure to function.

Conclusion: Structural differences in discovered circuits are insufficient to conclude distinct mechanisms without thorough edge-level evaluation and functional transfer tests.

Abstract: Circuit discovery methods identify subgraphs that explain specific model behaviors, and structural differences between discovered circuits are commonly interpreted as evidence of distinct mechanisms. We test this assumption by varying input statistics while holding the task fixed, and show that the resulting structural differences exhibit apparent specialization but do not correspond to functional differences, a pattern we term phantom specialization. Using Literal Sequence Copying across four token-frequency bands plus a control condition in five Pythia models (70M-1.4B), we extract 75 circuits and find that structurally distinct circuits implement the same computation: band-specific edges transfer broadly across bands, a core shared across most bands recovers at least 99% of circuit performance, and causal interchange interventions confirm that internal representations are interchangeable across frequency bands. Repeated extractions within the same frequency band further suggest that discovery algorithms sample from an equivalence class of valid subgraphs rather than recovering a unique mechanism. Standard evaluation practice obscures this pattern: source-level evaluation inflates apparent faithfulness, while edge-level evaluation reveals the many-to-one mapping from structure to function. Our results show that structural differences between circuits are not sufficient evidence for distinct mechanisms, and that exposing this requires edge-level evaluation and cross-condition transfer tests.

</details>


### [202] [FOXGLOVE: Understanding Goal-Oriented and Anchored Writing Feedback from Experts and LLMs on Argumentative Essays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06271)
*Yijun Liu,Yifan Song,John Gallagher,Sarah Sterman,Tal August*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces FOXGLOVE, a dataset comparing expert and LLM-sourced writing feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to fill the gap in systematic comparisons between human and LLM-generated feedback in writing contexts.

Method: Researchers used a dataset of 696 feedback comments from writing instructors and 1,644 feedback comments generated by four leading LLMs, comparing feedback quality, distribution, and complexity.

Result: LLMs and human instructors distribute feedback similarly but diverge in specific feedback targeting. LLMs write more complex, longer feedback and receive higher quality ratings, potentially due to the length.

Conclusion: FOXGLOVE enables a structured analysis of agreements, divergences, and differences in feedback between LLMs and human instructors.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate writing feedback, there remains no systematic comparison of LLM and expert feedback on the dimensions that writing research identifies as central to revision: goal-orientation, anchoring to specific sentences, and prioritization. We introduce FOXGLOVE, a dataset of 696 feedback comments written by trained writing instructors on 69 twelfth-grade argumentative essays, paired with 1,644 comments generated from four frontier LLMs under a shared protocol, totaling 2,340 comments. We provide expert quality ratings on a subset of both instructor and LLM comments. We find that instructors and LLMs distribute feedback similarly across goals and essay positions, yet instructors and models diverge on the specific sentences on which to provide feedback. Additionally, we find that models tend to write more complex feedback and use fewer questions than instructors. LLM feedback also receives higher ratings on most dimensions of quality, as rated by instructors, but much of this advantage appears to be attributable to lengthier comments. FOXGLOVE enables systematic comparison of where human and LLM feedback align, diverge, and differ.

</details>


### [203] [LLMs Can Leak Training Data But Do They Want To? A Propensity-Aware Evaluation of Memorization in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06286)
*Gianluca Barmina,Peter Schneider-Kamp,Lukas Galke Poech*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates how large language models memorize training data under ordinary usage and the conditions required for this memorization to occur.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations focus on whether models can be forced to reveal training data rather than whether they do so under normal circumstances. The authors aim to address this gap by developing a propensity-aware approach.

Method: The authors introduce PropMe, a framework contrasting adversarial prefix attacks with non-adversarial evaluations. They also propose propensity metrics via a metric transformation and present SimpleTrace, a tracing pipeline that attributes model outputs to training data and computes memorization metrics.

Result: The study finds a gap between adversarial and non-adversarial methods, with adversarial attacks eliciting stronger memorization signals. It also shows that the DFM Decoder model reduces memorization when later training emphasizes different data.

Conclusion: Memorization audits should measure both worst-case and ordinary leakage to provide a broader perspective on model memorization and its contexts.

Abstract: Large language models can reproduce training data, but existing memorization evaluations mostly measure whether models can be forced to do so, rather than whether they do so under ordinary use. We introduce PropMe, a propensity-aware framework for memorization evaluation that contrasts prefix-based capability attacks with non-adversarial evaluations. We propose a metric transformation that, applied to existing functions, allows to create propensity metrics. We further introduce SimpleTrace, a lightweight tracing pipeline built on infini-gram that deterministically attributes model generations to large-scale training corpora and computes verbatim, near-verbatim, and propensity-transformed memorization metrics. Evaluating two fully-open models: Comma and DFM Decoder on two datasets: Common Pile and Dynaword in two languages, we find a consistent gap between capability and propensity: prefix attacks elicit substantially stronger memorization signals than generic or dataset-specific prompts, while propensity scores remain low overall. Thus, the models can reveal training data when directly elicited, but rarely do so in more common non-adversarial settings. We also find that DFM Decoder, which is continually pre-trained from Comma, exhibits reduced memorization and memorization propensity for Common Pile, confirming that memorization capability can decrease when later training emphasizes partially different data. Our results suggest, and we encourage, that memorization audits should report both worst-case extractability and ordinary leakage propensity in order to have a more comprehensive view of this phenomenon.

</details>


### [204] [Decomposing Factual Sycophancy in Language Models: How Size and Instruction Tuning Shape Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06306)
*Victor De Marez,Luna De Bruyne,Walter Daelemans*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines factual sycophancy in language models, decomposing it into two factors: truth margin and manipulation sensitivity, studied across various model sizes and tuning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and address factual sycophancy, where a language model changes its correct answer under social pressure, which has implications for robust AI.

Method: The authors analyze 56 open-weight models (0.3B-32B parameters) using 13 manipulation types. They decompose factual sycophancy into truth margin and manipulation sensitivity.

Result: Model vulnerability primarily depends on size. Instruction tuning changes this dynamic: smaller tuned models may lose robustness, while larger tuned models become more robust. Instruction tuning affects truth margin but depends on manipulation type.

Conclusion: Factual sycophancy should not be evaluated as a single measure; instead, robustness should be reported by its specific channels (truth margin, manipulation sensitivity), manipulation types, and model size.

Abstract: Factual sycophancy occurs when a language model abandons a correct, verifiable answer under social pressure. Because a flip occurs only when pressure toward a false answer exceeds the model's neutral preference for the truth, flip rates conflate two mechanisms: the strength of that baseline preference (truth margin), and how far pressure shifts it (manipulation sensitivity). We decompose factual sycophancy into these channels and use them to separate the effects of size and instruction tuning across 56 open-weight models spanning 0.3B-32B parameters and 13 manipulation types. We find that vulnerability is governed mainly by size, but instruction tuning changes how size acts: small instruction-tuned models can become less robust, whereas large instruction-tuned models usually become more robust. Instruction tuning primarily increases truth margin, but its behavioral effect depends on manipulation type. Scaling also changes the two channels differently: base models gain margin but become mildly more manipulation-sensitive, whereas instruction-tuned models gain margin faster and become less sensitive. Factual sycophancy is therefore not a single scalar property. Evaluations should report channel-specific, manipulation-specific, and size-conditioned robustness rather than flip rates alone.

</details>


### [205] ["Chi nas dal soch el sent de legn" -- Auditing Text Corpora for Lombard](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06349)
*Edoardo Signoroni,Pavel Rychlý*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines the lack of quality NLP datasets for the Lombard language continuum, finding issues like misidentification, noise, and orthographic biases in existing datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many languages, including Lombard, lack sufficient high-quality datasets for tasks like Machine Translation, hindering technological advancements in NLP.

Method: Conducted a manual audit of available parallel and monolingual corpora, scrutinizing their quality and orthographic composition across multiple datasets.

Result: The analysis revealed significant flaws in web-scraped data, including language misidentification and representational bias favoring Western Lombard over Eastern variations.

Conclusion: The study advocates for community-driven and variety-aware data curation to address the flaws in current datasets for under-resourced languages like Lombard.

Abstract: Several of the world's languages are still under-resourced in terms of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools. This is mostly due to the lack of high-quality datasets to train, develop, and evaluate systems and models for several tasks, such as Machine Translation (MT). We conduct a manual audit of the parallel and monolingual corpora available for Lombard, an under-resourced language continuum from Italy. Our analysis reveals that the perceived abundance of web-scraped data is an illusion, with massive datasets plagued by severe language misidentification, boilerplate text, and non-linguistic noise. Furthermore, we analyze the orthographic composition of the valid Lombard portions across web-scraped datasets, curated corpora, and benchmarks. Our findings show conflicting orthographical systems and severe representational bias across all corpora: high-quality data is heavily skewed towards Western Lombard varieties, with Eastern ones left on the margins. This underscores the need for variety-aware, community-driven data curation rather than purely quantity-driven scraping.

</details>


### [206] [EDIT: Evidence-Diagnosed Intervention Training for Rule-Faithful LLM Grading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06350)
*Zhihao Wu,Linhai Zhang,Taiyi Wang,Runcong Zhao,Peter Andrews,Cesare Aloisi,Yulan He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EDIT is a novel framework designed to improve rubric-faithful grading by addressing problematic reasoning steps and calibrating grader beliefs with sophisticated signal-based interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing grading models fail to reliably adhere to rubrics and explain mark assignments due to their inability to diagnose incorrect reasoning or track belief changes during grading.

Method: EDIT employs a two-phase training process: (1) EDIT-SFT diagnoses and fixes problematic reasoning steps using internal model signals and rubric checklists, and (2) EDIT-RL calibrates the model with belief-guided reward shaping to maintain alignment with rubric-driven reasoning.

Result: EDIT demonstrates superior performance compared to baseline algorithms in rubric-faithful grading tasks, tested across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks.

Conclusion: The study proves that internal state diagnostics are effective in improving grading models, making EDIT a robust and reliable methodology for rubric-faithful automated graders.

Abstract: Reliable rubric grading requires more than accurate score prediction. Each judgement must be grounded in the mark scheme and evidence from the student answer. Existing credit-assignment and intervention methods, primarily designed for self-contained reasoning tasks such as mathematics reasoning, struggle in this setting because they do not identify where grading reasoning goes wrong or how the model's belief about the final mark changes during reasoning.
