Result: Recognition rate for minority provincial license plates increased from 78.2% to 91.5%, with real-time processing speeds maintained at 152 FPS over four benchmarks.

Conclusion: Integrating spatially-aware decoding with class-balanced data augmentation enhances the performance of real-time license plate recognition systems significantly.

Abstract: Real-Time License Plate Detection and Recognition (LPDR) forms the backbone of modern smart cities. Although the YOLOV5-PDLPR model substantially improved system efficiency through a parallel decoder approach, its performance is still affected by spatial character mismatches and data imbalance within the training set. This paper addresses these limitations by introducing Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention (CSHA) and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation (CBSA). An extensive study involving 75,000 synthetic samples is conducted and evaluated on four benchmarks: CCPD, CLPD, PKU, and an application-specific dataset. Experimental results demonstrate a substantial improvement in the recognition rate of minority provincial license plates from 78.2% to 91.5% while maintaining real-time processing performance of 152 FPS. The results indicate that spatially-aware parallel decoding combined with class-balanced augmentation provides an effective solution for high-speed license plate recognition systems.

</details>


### [265] [Emotion-Aware Image Generation from Korean Diary Text via LLM-based Prompt Translation and LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05816)
*Jihun Cho,Soo-Yeon Jeong,Sun-Young Ihm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an emotion-aware text-to-image pipeline for generating children's hand drawing style images from Korean diary entries, utilizing sentiment recognition and a fine-tuned Stable Diffusion model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-image (T2I) models struggle to capture sentiment and emotional context, especially in nuanced forms like diaries, focusing more on objects than emotions.

Method: The pipeline uses Qwen3-8B for sentiment recognition from text and a fine-tuned Stable Diffusion model (trained with LoRA on emotional trigger words and children's drawings) for image generation. It also evaluates how emotion trigger words impact generated images.

Result: The proposed model effectively generates emotionally-aware drawings in the style of children's artwork, while shedding light on the limitations of using CLIP Score as a metric for emotion-based image evaluation.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the potential of incorporating emotion-awareness in T2I pipelines, while also highlighting challenges in evaluating such models.

Abstract: T2I models cannot effectively capture sentiment from various types of text, including diaries, as they primarily focus on visual object-related patterns rather than contextual emotional understanding. This paper proposes an emotion-aware text-to-image pipeline that generates children's hand drawing style images from short Korean diary entries. The proposed pipeline employs Qwen3-8B for recognising implicit sentiment from short diaries, and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium fine-tuned with LoRA on children's drawing images with emotion-based trigger words for image generation. Additionally, this paper presents experiments examining the effect of emotion trigger words on generated images and discusses the limitations of CLIP Score as an evaluation metric for emotion-aware image generation.

</details>


### [266] [Gender Artifacts from Art History to Text-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05829)
*Piera Riccio,Miriam Doh,Benedikt Höltgen,Noa Garcia,Nanne van Noord*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper examines how gender representation interacts with artistic styles, focusing on both historical and AI-generated images. It introduces the StyleGender dataset and metrics to analyze these interactions and finds significant gender biases amplified by generative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address a gap in AI research where artistic style has been treated merely as a visual property, ignoring its socio-historical context, especially gendered representations.

Method: The authors created a dataset, StyleGender, containing 74k images with annotations for artistic style and gender. They developed two metrics called Set Gender Artifact (SGA), utilized to analyze gender signals at pixel and compositional levels in both historical and T2I-generated images.

Result: The findings reveal that gender representation significantly influences visual elements across various styles. Style keywords propagate gender biases into T2I-generated images, and generative models exhibit amplified gender artifacts compared to historical sources.

Conclusion: The research underscores the importance of recognizing and addressing the socio-historical contexts of artistic styles, particularly gender, in AI-generated content to mitigate bias amplification.

Abstract: Artistic styles are rooted in specific socio-historical contexts that encode social hierarchies, including distinct constructions of gender. Yet in AI research, style has long been treated as a surface-level visual property: a filter of color, brushstroke, and texture applied to otherwise content-neutral scenes. We introduce the first dataset to investigate the interplay between gender representation and style in both historical and generated images. StyleGender comprises 74k images spanning 19 artistic styles, comprising art historical images with style and gender annotations, T2I-generated images under controlled style and gender prompts, and a semantically aligned set enabling direct art history-to-generation comparison. By proposing two Set Gender Artifact (SGA) metrics (PixelSGA and MaskSGA), capturing gender signals at the pixel level and in compositional structure, we show that (1) gender representation shapes visual features across artistic styles, (2) style keywords carry these patterns into T2I generation, and (3) generative models tend to amplify gender artifacts beyond what is observed in historical sources.

</details>


### [267] [Learning Geometric Representations from Videos for Spatial Intelligent Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05833)
*Haibo Wang,Lifu Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoVR introduces a framework to bring spatial intelligence to Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using 2D video sequences by leveraging 3D geometric knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs perform well in 2D semantic tasks but lack 3D spatial awareness due to limited geometric consistency across video frames and the unavailability of large-scale 3D datasets.

Method: GeoVR distills geometry knowledge from pre-trained 3D foundational models using 2D video sequences, employing a multi-objective learning strategy with four geometric targets: inter-frame camera pose estimation, dense depth mapping, metric scale factor prediction, and multi-scale 3D feature alignment.

Result: GeoVR demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks, showing its efficiency in learning 3D spatial intelligence from 2D data.

Conclusion: GeoVR offers a new direction for enhancing MLLMs with intrinsic 3D awareness without requiring large-scale 3D datasets, setting a new paradigm for spatial intelligence.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at 2D semantic understanding but lack intrinsic 3D awareness, resulting in representations that fail to maintain geometric and spatial consistency across video frames. Given the scarcity of large-scale 3D data, we present GeoVR, a novel framework that learns geometric representations using purely 2D video sequences. This approach effectively restructures the semantic latent space within MLLMs to unlock spatial intelligence. Rather than employing superficial feature mixing, GeoVR reshapes the internal representations of the MLLM by distilling geometry knowledge from pre-trained 3D foundation models. This is accomplished through a multi-objective learning strategy driven by four complementary geometric targets: (1) estimating inter-frame camera poses to embed varying viewpoint dynamics, (2) regressing dense depth maps to anchor physical distances, (3) predicting a metric scale factor for real-world calibration, and (4) distilling multi-scale 3D features to align the intermediate feature space. Guided by these explicit physical and geometric constraints, the model's internal representations naturally develop strong 3D awareness. Extensive experiments on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoVR achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a new paradigm for endowing foundation models with spatial intelligence.

</details>


### [268] [Geometry-Aware Dataset Condensation for Diffusion Model Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05883)
*Xiao Cui,Yulei Qin,Mo Zhu,Wengang Zhou,Hongsheng Li,Houqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper reformulates dataset condensation for diffusion models using a geometry-aware distribution alignment approach, achieving superior performance in dataset compactness and fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dataset condensation methods struggle with diffusion model training due to low-fidelity synthetic data and real subset selection failing to maintain essential distribution geometry.

Method: The paper proposes a geometry-aware distribution alignment approach using one-sided partial optimal transport to align a subset with the full dataset. It also employs feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization for enhanced distribution fidelity and uses a two-stage discrete optimization strategy.

Result: The method consistently achieves superior dataset fidelity and distributional coverage across various diffusion model setups, dataset sizes, resolutions, and training cycles.

Conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware condensation approach effectively bridges the gap between compactness and performance, optimizing dataset selection for diffusion model training.

Abstract: Dataset condensation aims to construct compact datasets from real data via synthesis or selection. However, existing approaches are ill-suited for diffusion model training: synthetic data generation often yields low-fidelity samples unsuitable for authentic modeling, while real subset selection typically fails to preserve the distributional geometry required by diffusion likelihood objectives. To address this, we propose to reformulate real subset selection as a geometry-aware distribution alignment problem. By incorporating one-sided partial optimal transport, our method selectively aligns a compact subset with the full data distribution while allowing unmatched mass in low-density regions, ensuring the preserved geometric structure necessary for effective diffusion model training. To further ensure distributional fidelity, we complement geometric alignment with lightweight feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization. An efficient two-stage discrete optimization strategy is proposed to achieve this alignment objective. Extensive experiments across diffusion variants, subset sizes, image resolutions, and training rounds show that our method achieves superior fidelity and distributional coverage in diffusion model training. Codes are available at https://github.com/2018cx/GADC.

</details>


### [269] [Resonant Minds: Closed-Loop Social Avatars with Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05896)
*Jianxu Shangguan,Jing Xu,Hang Ye,Xiaoxuan Ma,Yizhou Wang,Wentao Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a dual-agent framework merging social reasoning and multimodal generation to create lifelike digital humans with both conversational and visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current models excel separately at dialogue or visual expression but fail to combine social cognition and embodied interaction into a unified system.

Method: The authors develop a closed-loop framework with modules for perception, social reasoning (inferring mental states via Theory of Mind), and expression, enabling emotion-controllable video synthesis of speaker and listener interactions.

Result: Using a custom hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset, the method outperformed existing approaches in dialogue quality and video generation, surpassing full-information baselines in thoughtful dialogue under real-world uncertainty.

Conclusion: Integrating social reasoning and dynamic multimodal generation enhances the realism and emotional intelligence of digital human interactions, demonstrating potential for more nuanced artificial agents.

Abstract: Creating lifelike digital humans with genuine social intelligence requires unifying cognitive reasoning and multimodal generation within a coherent framework. Current approaches treat these as separate tasks: Large Language Models excel at dialogue but lack embodied expression, while diffusion-based talking head models achieve visual fidelity but ignore social cognition. To bridge this gap, we propose a closed-loop dual-agent framework integrating perception, social reasoning, and expression into a continuous interaction cycle. The perception module analyzes partners' multimodal behaviors from video, while the social reasoning module infers hidden mental states through Theory of Mind and selects responses via an ensemble mechanism. The expression module then generates emotion-controllable dual-agent videos synthesizing both speaker speech and expression alongside listener reactive behaviors, capturing bidirectional dynamics absent in prior work. We construct a hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset with psychologically grounded personas and private social goals to support evaluation under information asymmetry. Experiments on this dataset demonstrate competitive or superior performance on both dialogue quality and video generation metrics. Notably, our method surpasses even the full-information Script mode on key dialogue quality dimensions, suggesting that explicit mental state inference under uncertainty can elicit more thoughtful dialogue than unrestricted information access.

</details>


### [270] [Self-Learning Expression Deformations for Data-Efficient Gaussian Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05912)
*Jiahao Yang,Xiaohang Yang,Qing Wang,Yilan Dong,Gregory Slabaugh,Shanxin Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents SAGE, a framework for creating high-quality 3D animatable avatars efficiently, using minimal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in modeling dynamic facial expressions with limited data, making avatar creation scalable and accessible.

Method: SAGE jointly optimizes 2D Gaussian surfels and an SDF with self-supervised learning to model facial expressions from minimal input data.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction and animation quality while significantly reducing the required data input.

Conclusion: SAGE simplifies and democratizes dynamic avatar creation and paves the way for accessible, data-efficient expression modeling.

Abstract: Modeling dynamic facial expressions using 3D Gaussian representations remains challenging due to their unstructured nature. Conventional Gaussian avatar pipelines require extensive multiview and sequential expression data, limiting scalability and accessibility. In this work, we introduce Self-Adaptive Gaussian Expression (SAGE), a framework for self-learning expression-induced Gaussian deformations that enables high-fidelity, animatable avatars from minimal input data. Our method jointly optimizes 2D Gaussian surfels and a Signed Distance Field (SDF) to enforce compact, surface-aligned Gaussian distributions, while a self-supervised expression learning phase replaces long training sequences with geometric and appearance consistency constraints. This design allows flexible deployment across multiple reconstruction regimes: in the multiview setting, only a single frame (timestep) is required instead of thousands; in the monocular setting, only head rotations are needed without expression sequences; and in the one-shot setting, no pretraining or priors are necessary. Experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves reconstruction and animation quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing data requirements by several orders of magnitude. Our results highlight the potential of self-supervised Gaussian deformation learning as a step toward accessible, data-efficient avatar creation.

</details>


### [271] [CamFlow+: Hybrid Motion Bases for 2D Camera Motion Estimation with Stabilization Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05915)
*Haipeng Li,Zhen Liu,Zhanglei Yang,Hai Jiang,Tianhao Zhou,Zhengzhe Liu,Ping Tan,Bing Zeng,Shuaicheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CamFlow+ is a new method for estimating 2D camera motion, overcoming limitations of previous approaches by integrating hybrid bases and depth-aware smoothness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of traditional and piecewise planar homography-based methods in handling camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax.

Method: The proposed framework integrates physical, stochastic, and depth-translational bases to model camera motion in dense-flow space, combined with a depth-aware smoothness regularization.

Result: CamFlow+ achieved superior performance in camera-motion estimation and digital video stabilization, as validated by a new benchmark and a blind user study.

Conclusion: CamFlow+ offers a more robust method for 2D camera-motion estimation, improving both precision and stability in practical applications like video stabilization.

Abstract: Estimating 2D camera motion is fundamental to computer vision and computational photography. Existing homography-based methods work well for planar scenes or pure rotation, but struggle with camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax; local homography and mesh-based models improve flexibility but still rely on piecewise planar assumptions. We introduce CamFlow+, a hybrid-basis framework that represents 2D camera motion directly in dense-flow space. CamFlow+ combines homography-derived physical bases, stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and depth-translational bases derived from depth and camera intrinsics, relaxing the single-plane constraint while preserving camera-motion regularity. A depth-aware smoothness term further regularizes translation-induced parallax in continuous-depth regions while preserving motion changes near depth boundaries. We evaluate CamFlow+ on GHOF-Cam, a camera-motion benchmark that masks out dynamic objects and ill-posed occlusion regions in an optical-flow benchmark to isolate camera-induced motion. Experiments show that CamFlow+ improves sparse and dense camera-motion estimation. In digital video stabilization, CamFlow+ also improves global and local stability, achieving the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study. Code and datasets will be available on the project page: https://lhaippp.github.io/CamFlow+.

</details>


### [272] [Unveiling the Unknown: Open Vocabulary Object Detection with Scene Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05916)
*Yi Chen,Yinghao Lu,Zhehao Li,Chenchen Yan,Jiafei Wu,Chong Wang,Jiangbo Qian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework for open-vocabulary object detection (OVOD) that uses scene graphs and relational modeling to improve detection of novel object categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of the paper is to address the limitation in current OVOD methods, which often fail to consider structured, image-specific relationships like interactions and spatial arrangements between objects, leading to restricted detection effectiveness.

Method: The method involves a Scene-guided Relational Modeling detection framework. This includes using scene graphs to capture semantic and spatial relationships, a Relation Attention Module to enhance relational cues, and a scene-based textual alignment branch to distill category knowledge from captions.

Result: The proposed model outperforms existing OVOD methods, achieving higher AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

Conclusion: Incorporating structured relationships and scene-based alignment improves the ability to detect novel object categories, offering superior performance in OVOD tasks.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary object detection seeks to identify novel object categories that were not part of the training data. Many knowledge distillation-based approaches have shown promising performance by transferring knowledge from pre-trained vision-language models to object detection. However, these methods often overlook structured, image-specific relationships between objects, such as interactions and spatial arrangements. This oversight can significantly restrict the effectiveness of detecting novel categories. To address this issue, we propose a Scene-guided Relational Modeling detection framework. This framework utilizes scene graphs to capture structured semantic and spatial relationships between candidate regions and their contextual objects. It explicitly models interactions among neighboring regions and incorporates a Relation Attention Module to implicitly amplify the key relational cues extracted from the scene graph. Furthermore, we present a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment. This approach facilitates a seamless integration of visual relations with semantic information for enhanced detection performance. Comprehensive experiments show that our model achieves superior performance compared to other OVOD methods, improving the AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

</details>


### [273] [T-FunS3D: Task-Driven Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Functionality Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05975)
*Jingkun Feng,Reza Sabzevari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: T-FunS3D is a task-driven, open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method for robots, balancing performance, speed, and resource efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in enabling robots to identify functional components in 3D scenes efficiently and effectively, avoiding the limitations of exhaustive or object-focused methods.

Method: The method employs a hierarchical, task-oriented open-vocabulary 3D segmentation approach using a scene graph constructed from 3D point clouds, RGB-D images, and a vision-language model to locate relevant functional components.

Result: T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art methods in segmentation accuracy while offering faster runtime and lower memory usage.

Conclusion: T-FunS3D provides actionable, efficient, and precise 3D perception capabilities tailored for robotic applications.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation enables robots to localize functional object components in 3D scenes. It is a challenging task that requires spatial understanding and task interpretation. Current open-vocabulary 3D segmentation methods primarily focus on object-level recognition, while scene-wide part segmentation methods attempt to segment the entire scene exhaustively, making them highly resource-intensive and time consuming. Balancing segmentation performance in terms of granularity, accuracy, and speed remains a challenge. As one step towards alleviating this, we introduce T-FunS3D, a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method that provides actionable perception for robotic applications. Our method takes as input the 3D point cloud and posed RGB-D images of an indoor scene. We construct an open-vocabulary scene graph by extracting instances and their visual embeddings in the environment. Given a task description, T-FunS3D identifies the most relevant instances in the scene graph and locates their functional components leveraging a vision-language model. Experiments on the SceneFun3D dataset demonstrate that T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, while achieving faster runtime and reduced memory usage.

</details>


### [274] [MemoryCard: Topic-Aware Multi-Modal Clue Compression for Long-Video Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05917)
*Qing Yang,Pengcheng Huang,Xinze Li,Zhenghao Liu,Yukun Yan,Yu Gu,Ge Yu,Gang Li,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MemoryCard, a video-memory-based framework to improve long-video question answering by organizing videos into semantically coherent units called Memory Cards, enhancing VLMs' ability to handle event-level semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of existing frame-centric methods in long-video QA, which fail to effectively capture coherent event-level semantics due to reliance on isolated and fragmented frames.

Method: MemoryCard organizes long videos into semantically coherent Memory Cards by segmenting videos into topic- or event-specific units, generating event-level video gists, and selecting representative moments for answering questions.

Result: MemoryCard achieves up to a 21.8% relative improvement in long-video QA accuracy compared to existing methods, showing its effectiveness under comparable visual-token budgets.

Conclusion: MemoryCard enhances long-video QA by structuring video content into event-level units, enabling better semantic understanding and retrieval, and leads to significant accuracy improvements.

Abstract: Long-video question answering remains challenging for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), as answer-relevant evidence is often sparse, transient, and temporally dispersed across lengthy video contexts. Existing frame-centric approaches improve efficiency through uniform sampling, query-aware frame selection, visual-token compression, and adaptive resolution strategies. However, they still rely on isolated and fragmented frames as the fundamental evidence units, limiting VLMs' ability to effectively capture coherent event-level semantics. To address this limitation, we propose MemoryCard, a video-memory-based augmentation framework that organizes long videos into self-contained Memory Cards. Specifically, MemoryCard first performs a self-reading process over videos and aligned utterances to segment the video into semantically coherent units, each corresponding to a distinct topic or event. For each unit, it generates an event-level video gist and selects representative visual moments, which are then rendered into unified Memory Cards for retrieval and question answering. Experimental results demonstrate that MemoryCard consistently improves long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, achieving up to a 21.8% relative improvement in accuracy. All code is available at https://github.com/NEUIR/MemoryCard.

</details>


### [275] [Faithful, Enriched, and Precise: Benchmarking Natural-Science Illustration Generation by T2I models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05949)
*Yifan Chang,Jiaxin Ai,Jianwen Sun,Yuandong Pu,Siqi Luo,Liangliang Zhao,Yuchen Ren,Minghao Liu,Yunfei Yu,Yu Qiao,Kaipeng Zhang,Yihao Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces FEPBench, a benchmark for evaluating Text-to-Image models in scientific illustration generation, focusing on instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of fine-grained evaluation metrics in current benchmarks for T2I models used in scientific illustration generation.

Method: The authors create FEPBench, a multi-disciplinary benchmark with fine-grained annotations, aided by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and human experts. It evaluates models along visual, textual, relational, and layout dimensions.

Result: State-of-the-art T2I models still face challenges like text-rendering issues, insufficient reasoning capabilities, and difficulties in balancing richness and precision.

Conclusion: The findings highlight areas for improvement in T2I models and provide insights into their effective deployment for scientific illustration generation. The authors will release benchmark data, annotations, and evaluation code.

Abstract: Scientific illustrations are essential tools for communicating research findings, especially in natural science, where they visualize complex concepts and processes. As Text-to-Image (T2I) models become increasingly capable, researchers have started to use them for scientific illustration generation. However, existing benchmarks often assess outputs at a holistic level, overlooking fine-grained elements, while scientific reasoning ability and output conciseness remain under-quantified. We introduce FEPBench, a benchmark built from carefully selected high-quality scientific illustrations across multiple disciplines and layout types. With the assistance of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and human experts, we provide fine-grained atom set annotations and systematically evaluate T2I models along three dimensions: instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision. Our evaluation further decomposes model performance across visual, textual, relation, and layout elements. Results show that even state-of-the-art (SOTA) closed-source models, such as GPT Image 2 and Nano Banana Pro, still suffer from text-rendering bottlenecks, limited reasoning enrichment, and difficulty balancing generation richness with precision. These findings provide practical guidance for improving and deploying T2I models in scientific illustration generation. Benchmark data, atom set annotations, and evaluation code will be released by us.

</details>


### [276] [Synthetic Data Generation and Vision-based Wrinkle and Keypoint Detection for Bimanual Cloth Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06292)
*Ariel Herrera,Xueyang Kang,Atal Anil Kumar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper develops a robotic perception framework combining synthetic data and real-world annotations to improve textile manipulation, particularly wrinkle detection and garment folding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloth manipulation is particularly difficult for robots due to continuous deformation and occlusions, necessitating more robust visual perception systems that can work without relying heavily on annotated real-world data.

Method: A Blender-based synthetic data pipeline was used to generate auto-annotated keypoints, which were combined with manually labeled images and real-world data. The perception framework included a CNN for keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline for detecting grasping points. These were used for a bimanual algorithm to stretch and iron garments.

Result: The keypoint detection model achieved a Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. Additionally, the system was able to transfer well to physical fabrics without further training, outperforming baseline methods in handling occlusions and preventing false positives.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines real and synthetic data to enhance robotic cloth manipulation capabilities, demonstrating strong generalization and robustness in handling occluded and folded states.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation of textiles remains challenging because continuous deformation and self-occlusions hinder the robust visual perception required to estimate the cloth's state. To address the lack of annotated real-world data, we developed a Blender-based synthetic pipeline exporting auto-annotated keypoints, and combined manually labeled renders with real-world data to train a wrinkle detector. We present a perception framework integrating a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline to extract grasping points from structural wrinkles. A proposed bimanual algorithm uses this system to stretch fully folded garments via wrinkles, transitioning to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge. The keypoint model achieves a Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. The perception system transfers to physical fabrics without fine-tuning, outperforming baselines that fail in high-occlusion states or yield false positives on severe folds.

</details>


### [277] [Video-Rate Streaming Stylization on a Vision-Aware MLLM-Conditioned Edit Diffusion: Asymmetric Batched Inference on a Distilled UNet + MLLM Text Encoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05981)
*Yoshiyuki Ootani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents an efficient text-to-image streaming pipeline targeting real-time use, optimizing a distilled diffusion U-Net and multimodal language models for higher FPS and describing associated engineering modifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in real-time text-to-image pipelines, especially focusing on the bottlenecks during vision-aware edit diffusion workflows where multimodal language models constrain throughput.

Method: The authors propose a streaming pipeline with three main mechanisms: (1) asymmetric CUDA pipelining with batched amortization and prompt caching, (2) ControlNet-LLLite to fold the U-Net and adapter stack into a fused graph for better compilation, and (3) a conditioning-refresh schedule to reduce computational overhead.

Result: The proposed pipeline achieved up to 74.1 FPS on RTX 5090, maintaining real-time throughput for 512x512 resolution video over extended sequences, with generalization to new video clips and moderate unseen style families.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates a practical enhancement to video-rate text-to-image processes while maintaining stability and generalization, suggesting viable pathways for deploying high-performance streaming pipelines in real-world applications.

Abstract: Aggressive distillation of the diffusion U-Net inverts the per-frame bottleneck of real-time text-to-image pipelines: once the denoiser is a 4-step or 1-step distilled student, the text encoder becomes the critical path. This inversion is most acute in vision-aware edit diffusion, where the encoder is a multimodal large language model (MLLM). We study the case of a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net paired with a 2.13B MLLM text encoder (Qwen3-VL) and present a streaming pipeline targeted at this regime built around three engineering mechanisms: asymmetric side-stream / main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation (and optional static-prompt caching), a compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation that folds the entire U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph, and a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset that amortises the per-frame conditioning cost. On a single consumer RTX 3090 Ti at 512x512 the pipeline sustains 27.4 fps over a 480-frame run at batch size B=8 and 29.6 fps at B=16, with end-to-end p50 latency of approximately 0.5 and 1.0 seconds respectively; the same operating point measures 54.9 fps on RTX 4090 and 74.1 fps on RTX 5090. We report video-rate streaming throughput rather than interactive low latency, and locate our numbers against same-stack StreamDiffusion re-runs as systems context, not as a benchmark superiority claim. For the trained oil-painting style, the released temporal adapter generalises within in-clip noise to 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips from seven sources; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.

</details>


### [278] [Multimodal Sexism Identification and Characterization using Large Language Models and Gradient Boosting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05997)
*Kyriakos Chaviaras,Maria Lymperaiou,Athanasios Voulodimos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper describes AILS-NTUA's system for identifying sexism in memes and short videos using a multimodal feature-engineered pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance the ability to detect and characterize sexism in different media formats (memes and short-form videos) effectively.

Method: A feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline employing gradient-boosted regression models, LLM-derived semantic indicators for memes, and various multimedia features for videos.

Result: LLM-derived semantic cues enhance meme analysis, while video analysis challenges include cross-modal noise and the sensitivity to feature dimensionality.

Conclusion: Semantic feature engineering works well for static memes, but video settings require robust temporal modeling to handle data noise and diversity.

Abstract: We present the AILS-NTUA submission to the EXIST 2026 Lab at CLEF, addressing multimodal sexism identification and characterization in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3). Our system follows a feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline built around gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes, we combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators designed to capture high-level cues such as stereotyping, objectification, irony, and misogyny. For videos, we investigate the effect of feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor-derived metadata. Development results show that focused LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification, while video performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. For videos, development results favor compact feature selection, but official test results show that this conclusion does not fully transfer to unseen data, where the unfiltered representation generalizes better. Overall, our findings highlight the usefulness of targeted semantic feature engineering for static memes and the need for more robust temporal modeling in noisy short-form video settings.

</details>


### [279] [Deep Learning-based 3D Oral Cavity Reconstruction Using 2D Intraoral Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05998)
*Jihun Cho,Soo-Yeon Jeong,Eun-Jeong Bae,Sun-Young Ihm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a cost-effective software approach for reconstructing 3D oral models using only 2D images, avoiding traditional hardware limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current oral 3D modeling methods like impression taking and intraoral scanning are either uncomfortable, error-prone, expensive, or require complex equipment.

Method: A reconstruction approach based on ten 2D intraoral images was developed using MobileNetV2 for encoding and Multi-head Attention for fusing multi-view features, trained on the Dental3DS dataset.

Result: The model achieved 77.49% accuracy with uneven point distribution caused by vertex clustering in high-density ground truth regions.

Conclusion: The proposed method significantly reduces costs and patient discomfort while eliminating hardware dependency, though further refinement is needed to address uneven point distribution.

Abstract: Oral 3D modelling is one of the most essential stages in dentistry, and many different approaches, such as impression taking and intraoral scanning, are commonly used for this phase, each with notable limitations. Impression taking, which involves placing alginate or silicone material in a tray and inserting it into the patient's oral cavity to form a negative mold, suffers from significant patient discomfort, material deformation errors, and difficulties in storage and transportation. Intraoral scanners, which directly scan oral structures in real time using structured light or laser technology, produce state-of-the-art results but are associated with substantially high equipment costs. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a software-based approach that reconstructs a 3D oral model using only ten 2D intraoral images captured from different angles, requiring no dedicated hardware devices. The proposed method reduces cost, eliminates the need for physical scanning equipment, minimises patient discomfort, and enables automated 3D reconstruction. The model is trained on the publicly available Dental3DS dataset, comprising 950 upper jaw samples, and employs MobileNetV2 as the image encoder combined with Multi-head Attention for multi-view feature fusion. The proposed model achieves an accuracy of 77.49%, measured by nearest-neighbor matching with a distance threshold of 0.035. However, predicted vertices tend to concentrate in high-density regions of the ground truth, resulting in uneven point distribution across the reconstructed model.

</details>


### [280] [ATT-CR: Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05999)
*Yang Wu,Ye Deng,Pengna Li,Wenli Huang,Kangyi Wu,Xiaomeng Xin,Jinjun Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal (ATT-CR) to address computational inefficiency and interference issues in existing Transformer-based methods for cloud removal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Transformer-based cloud removal models suffer from high computational complexity and interference from invalid cloudy pixels, leading to suboptimal performance.

Method: The proposed ATT-CR model introduces two components: Triangular Attention (TAN) for reduced computational costs using triangular matrices (O(N) complexity), and a Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM) to distinguish cloudy and clean pixels for improved accuracy.

Result: Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks show that ATT-CR achieves superior performance compared to existing cloud removal methods.

Conclusion: ATT-CR effectively addresses computational and interference issues in Transformer-based cloud removal, offering an efficient and accurate solution.

Abstract: Cloud removal aims to accurately reconstruct the ground objects obscured by clouds in remote sensing images. Existing Transformer-based methods utilizing self-attention have shown impressive results by effectively modeling long-range dependencies in cloudy images. However, they suffer from the following issues: 1) the high computational complexity of self-attention limits scalability; 2) treating both cloudy and clean pixels as valid within the attention computation brings disturbances in subsequent layers, leading to suboptimal performance. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal (ATT-CR), a model that effectively reduces computational costs and mitigates interference from cloudy pixels. Specifically, it consists of two core components: Triangular Attention (TAN) and Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM). TAN employs lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) computational complexity, significantly reducing the computational costs. The FSGM, on the other hand, integrates with TAN to adaptively distinguish between cloudy and clean features, which minimizes the introduction of invalid information into subsequent layers. Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks demonstrate that ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [281] [Global-Local Monte Carlo Tree Search in Vision-Language Models for Text-to-3D Indoor Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06002)
*Mengshi Qi,Wei Deng,Xianlin Zhang,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel method for text-to-3D indoor scene generation that utilizes a tree-search approach instead of sequential decision-making, addressing error propagation issues prevalent in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the process of text-to-3D indoor scene generation by overcoming the limitations of sequential decision mechanisms that suffer from error propagation, and to enrich benchmark diversity for better model evaluation.

Method: The authors propose a tree search method with global and local trees: global for object placement and local for detailed placement parameters. They use hierarchical scene representation (from room to object levels) and a PRM-guided MCTS technique for efficient exploration and optimization. They also employ diffusion models for generating consistent textures.

Result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art models in generating realistic and coherent 3D scenes. Additionally, a new large-scale dataset, 3DTindo-bench, was created to better evaluate models in diverse settings.

Conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates superior realistic scene generation and a significant advance in text-to-3D indoor scene modeling, aided by a unique dataset that fosters future innovation.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models have achieved significant reasoning performance in various tasks.However, there are few studies on text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs. The main challenge is that prevailing LVLM-based methods employ chain-of-thought sequential decision mechanisms that cannot revise earlier decisions, causing error propagation.In this paper, we consider the task as a planning problem constrained by spatial and layout commonsense.To solve this problem, we model it as a tree search problem with global and local trees, which differs from existing sequential decision-making approaches.In the global tree, we place each object iteratively and explore multiple attempts like humans furnishing a room, where the problem space is represented as a tree.To effectively search the tree, we propose a hierarchical scene representation and a PRM-guided MCTS method.The hierarchical representation abstracts a scene into room level, region level, floor object level, and supported object level.The PRM-guided MCTS method uses the PRM to prune unnecessary branches and the MCTS algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation to get an optimal solution with fewer attempts.In the local tree, it further decomposes the placement of each object into finer sub-steps, including the specific placement parameters.To make the whole appearance of the scene consistent, we leverage pre-trained diffusion image generative models to predict textures for all the objects in the scene.As existing benchmarks for text-to-3D indoor scene generation remain limited in scale and diversity, we collect a new large-scale diverse dataset that contains 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions with diverse sizes, layouts, and styles, named 3DTindo-bench, to better assess the capability of the state-of-the-art models. Our experiments show that our method generates more realistic 3D scenes than state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [282] [ReSAGE-PAR: Representational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06020)
*Pablo Ayuso-Albizu,Pablo Carballeira,Juan C. SanMiguel,Paula Moral*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces ReSAGE-PAR, a pipeline for generating high-quality, attribute-specific pedestrian images using diffusion models, addressing key challenges in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) data scarcity and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the need to overcome limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), specifically addressing controlled image generation and reliable attribute verification.

Method: The proposed method, ReSAGE-PAR, employs a three-step pipeline: adapting diffusion models for native PAR resolutions with a LoRA Image-to-Image approach, using vision-language alignment for validation with comprehensive prompts, and applying a Bayesian classifier for reliable binary pseudo-labeling.

Result: ReSAGE-PAR achieves high-fidelity dataset expansion, significant improvements in PAR performance with gains up to 8.7%, and pushes state-of-the-art frameworks to higher accuracy levels.

Conclusion: ReSAGE-PAR is demonstrated as an effective, architecture-agnostic solution for improving PAR through scalable and high-quality data augmentation, proving its utility in advancing the field.

Abstract: To address the limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), we explore image synthesis using diffusion models guided by attribute-based prompts. While this enables the controlled generation of pedestrian images, it faces two critical challenges: (i) the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution, non-standard surveillance crops, and (ii) the need for reliable attribute verification to prevent generative hallucinations. In this paper, we introduce a robust generate-score-autolabel pipeline called ReSAGE-PAR (REpresentational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in PAR) that bridges this domain gap and enables scalable, high-fidelity dataset expansion. First, we adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. Second, we extract vision-language alignment scores between the generated images and their conditioning prompts, utilizing a comprehensive prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and inconsistent complements. Finally, we formulate a Bayesian classifier that converts these continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of ReSAGE-PAR in preserving spatial priors and verifying attributes. When integrated into PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR consistently yields significant improvements-achieving gains of up to 8.7% on standard backbones and pushing state-of-the-art frameworks to new performance levels. This proves its value as an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement. The complete codebase for ReSAGE-PAR is publicly available at http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/publications/ReSAGE-PAR.

</details>


### [283] [Texture-preserving implicit neural representation for Cone beam CT truncated reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06039)
*Genyuan Zhang,Junyao Wang,Haoran Lan,Chuandong Tan,Songtao Zhu,Fenglin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a self-supervised 3D CBCT reconstruction framework that eliminates truncation artifacts and combines neural network extrapolation with iterative refinement for high-resolution results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) often faces data truncation issues, causing artifacts and limited FOV in reconstructions. Existing deep learning approaches rely heavily on supervised ground truth and fail to handle continuous 3D truncation variations.

Method: The paper proposes a self-supervised approach based on neural scene representations that maps spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, bypassing traditional reconstruction methods. It also integrates a physics-based iterative refinement module to recover high-frequency details otherwise missed by neural networks.

Result: The proposed method eliminates truncation artifacts, enables 3D data extrapolation, and effectively supplements high-frequency detail loss. It performs well across both simulated and real-world datasets, outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: The framework successfully combines the advantages of neural networks in artifact suppression and data extrapolation with the fine-detail preservation capabilities of iterative algorithms, thereby overcoming the limitations of existing methods.

Abstract: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) frequently suffers from data truncation, which introduces severe artifacts and limits the effective field of view (FOV). Existing deep learning methods for truncated cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) reconstruction suffer from serious limitations, including a strict reliance on supervised ground truth and a failure to account for continuous 3D spatial truncation variations. To address these challenges, we introduce a self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations. By directly mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, our approach inherently bypasses traditional filtering and backprojection operations, thereby fundamentally eliminating truncation-induced ring artifacts while enabling robust continuous 3D data extrapolation. However, coordinate networks are susceptible to an inherent spectral bias, which leads to a severe loss of clinically vital high-frequency textures. To resolve this bottleneck, we further incorporate a physics-based iterative refinement module into the neural scene representation architecture. Leveraging the artifact-free, extrapolated volume from the coordinate network as an optimal initialization, this module progressively re-extracts and injects high-frequency structural information from the original projections back into the volume. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method successfully unifies the exceptional artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with the high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms.

</details>


### [284] [LoomVideo: Unifying Multimodal Inputs into Video Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06042)
*Jianzong Wu,Hao Lian,Jiongfan Yang,Dachao Hao,Ye Tian,Yunhai Tong,Jingyuan Zhu,Biaolong Chen,Qiaosong Qi,Aixi Zhang,Wanggui He,Mushui Liu,Jinlong Liu,Hao Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces LoomVideo, a 5B-parameter architecture for video generation and editing, designed to be both efficient and computationally inexpensive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified video generation and editing models are limited by high computational costs and inefficiencies with existing large-scale architectures.

Method: LoomVideo replaces the traditional text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning mechanism as well as a Deepstack injection method for efficiency and robust editing.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in benchmarks, especially in e-commerce and fashion generation. LoomVideo achieves a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed.

Conclusion: LoomVideo represents a significant step towards practical, efficient, and highly capable unified video editing and generation models.

Abstract: Developing unified video generation and editing models capable of interpreting interleaved multimodal inputs is a promising yet challenging frontier field. Existing unified frameworks predominantly rely on massive models (typically 13B parameters or more) and incorporate source video conditions for editing by concatenating sequence tokens. This concatenation inevitably doubles the sequence length, quadrupling the computational complexity of the self-attention mechanism and introducing prohibitive overhead. To address these bottlenecks, we present LoomVideo, a highly efficient 5B-parameter unified architecture for both video generation and editing. LoomVideo replaces the standard text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs Deepstack injection mechanism to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Crucially, we introduce a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for video editing. By scaling and directly adding the clean source video latent to the noised target latent, this elegant design eliminates the need for token concatenation, drastically reducing computational cost while maintaining robust capabilities for complex, non-rigid edits. Furthermore, a Negative Temporal RoPE strategy is seamlessly integrated to handle multiple reference images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across comprehensive benchmarks, exhibiting exceptional superiority in e-commerce and fashion generation scenarios. Benefiting from the zero-overhead conditioning mechanism, LoomVideo achieves at least a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed compared to models of similar capabilities, paving the way for highly practical and efficient video foundation models.

</details>


### [285] [LLM-Conditioned Synthesis of Pathological Gaits via Structured Gait-Language Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06048)
*Mritula Chandrasekaran,Sanket Kachole,Jarik Francik,Dimitrios Makris*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework for synthesizing 3D pathological gait data from textual descriptions, improving classification tasks when combined with real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pathological gait datasets are limited due to issues like privacy concerns, recruitment, cost, and movement variability.

Method: The framework uses techniques like motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, semantic augmentation with LLMs, and language-to-gait generation to generate fixed-length 3D gait sequences.

Result: The synthetic sequences enhance pathological gait classification accuracy when used alongside real data. The best model is a GRU classifier achieving 92.77% accuracy with a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

Conclusion: The proposed approach successfully augments existing pathological gait classification datasets, demonstrating improved performance when real and synthetic data are combined.

Abstract: Pathological gait datasets remain scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability. Our work presents a multimodal LLM-guided framework for pathology-aware 3D gait data synthesis from structured textual descriptions. The proposed method generates fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences for pathological gait classification tasks. The framework combines motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. A key contribution is the proposed pathological tokeniser, which is designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning. Experiments suggest that the proposed synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. The best result is obtained using a GRU classifier trained with real and synthetic samples, achieving 92.77\% accuracy under a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

</details>


### [286] [ReCache: Learning Budget-Aware Caching Schedules for Diffusion Models via REINFORCE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06060)
*Mishan Aliev,Eva Neudachina,Ilya Bykov,Aleksandr Oganov,Kirill Struminsky,Aibek Alanov,Denis Rakitin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReCache, a method to optimize the recomputation steps in diffusion models for efficient sampling under computational constraints, significantly improving generation quality metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models produce high-quality results but are computationally expensive due to iterative denoising. This work focuses on optimizing computational schedules to better balance quality and efficiency.

Method: ReCache trains a policy to select recomputation steps that maximize generation quality within a given computational budget. It uses policy gradients for training and does not rely on labeled data.

Result: ReCache shows superior performance to baseline methods like DiCache and uniform HiCache, achieving higher quality metrics (e.g., LPIPS and VBench) and faster computational speeds.

Conclusion: ReCache provides a user-controlled mechanism for efficient sampling in diffusion models by optimizing recomputation schedules, offering significant improvements in quality and speed.

Abstract: Modern diffusion models generate high-quality images and videos, but their iterative denoising process makes inference expensive. Feature caching accelerates sampling by reusing or predicting intermediate activations across neighboring denoising steps, exploiting the redundancy of computations along the reverse trajectory. In this work, we focus on the caching schedule: selecting which denoising steps should be fully recomputed. Existing schedules are either fixed (e.g. uniform) or chosen adaptively from per-step error heuristics; in both cases, the actual compute cost is a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds rather than a quantity the user can specify. We propose ReCache, which inverts this: given a target budget k, it learns the recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality, turning compute into a directly controllable input. ReCache trains via policy gradients, sidestepping backpropagation through full diffusion inference, and uses no labelled data. Generations from uncached inference serve as matching targets, paired with a reward for generation quality. ReCache is compatible with any caching mechanism, including feature reuse and feature forecasting; for each mechanism, a single trained policy adapts across computational budgets at inference time. ReCache consistently outperforms scheduling baselines: under a $\times5.04$ FLOPs reduction on FLUX, it reduces LPIPS by 31% (from 0.456 to 0.316) compared to DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at a $\sim \times2.6$ speedup, it drops LPIPS by 65% (from 0.480 to 0.169) and boosts the VBench score by 7% (5.6 points, from 70.4 to 76.0) over uniform HiCache. Code is available at https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.

</details>


### [287] [FontFusion: Enhancing Generative Text in Diffusion Models with Typographic Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06066)
*Marian Lupascu,Nipun Jindal,Ionut Mironica,Zhaowen Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FontFusion is a framework for Diffusion Transformer architectures, resolving the trade-off between font control and text legibility through hierarchical tokens, position-aware embeddings, and a multi-level token dropping strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Typography generation in diffusion models struggles with balancing font precision and text readability.

Method: The paper introduces a hierarchical token representation, position-aware embeddings, and a multi-level token dropping strategy, combined with a dual encoder integrating DeepFont and DINOv2.

Result: FontFusion achieves 76% improvement on decorative fonts and improves font consistency by 68-76% compared to unconditioned models, integrating with existing architectures without retraining.

Conclusion: FontFusion effectively resolves typography trade-offs in diffusion models, offering enhanced precision and readability without modifying existing architectures.

Abstract: Typography generation in diffusion models faces a persistent trade-off: enabling precise font control typically degrades text legibility, while maintaining readability often sacrifices typographic fidelity. We present FontFusion, a plug-and-play conditioning framework for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures that resolves this dilemma through three core innovations: (1) a hierarchical token representation establishing explicit text-font relationships at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings creating spatial bindings between typography and image content, and (3) a multi-level token dropping strategy improving both computational efficiency and generalization to unseen fonts. Our systematic evaluation of font embedding spaces reveals that a dual encoder combining DeepFont and DINOv2 outperforms any single encoder for typography tasks. FontFusion demonstrates 76% relative improvement on challenging decorative fonts over single-encoder baselines and font consistency gains exceeding approximately 68-76% over unconditioned models, while integrating into existing DiT architectures without retraining.

</details>


### [288] [VZCrash: A Large-Scale IMU Dataset of Ego-Vehicle Crashes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06074)
*Tommaso Bianconcini,Henrique Piñeiro Monteagudo,Aurel Pjetri,Tomaso Trinci,Leonardo Taccari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduction of VZCrash, the largest open dataset of real-world vehicle collision data with IMU telemetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to provide a large-scale, publicly accessible dataset for developing and benchmarking vehicle crash detection models.

Method: Development of VZCrash dataset with over 31,000 crashes and 158,000 negative samples from diverse vehicles, followed by experimental analysis of crash detection approaches.

Result: Benchmarking of crash detection models and demonstration of the significance of scaling data for robust real-world model deployment.

Conclusion: The scale and quality of the VZCrash dataset advance research in crash detection and highlight the importance of large datasets for effective model training and deployment.

Abstract: We introduce VZCrash, the largest publicly available dataset of real-world vehicle collision data featuring Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) telemetry. The dataset contains more than 31,000 validated crashes and 158,000 negative samples, including hard cases and distractors. Each sample includes acceleration and angular velocity at 100 Hz, and GPS speed at 1 Hz. Events in VZCrash were captured by devices installed on a fleet of 73,010 commercial vehicles of different sizes driving in the United States over the span of several years. We also present an extensive experimental study enabled by the volume of the dataset. We first benchmark several different approaches, from a simple threshold-based heuristic to state-of-the-art deep learning models. Then, we present an experiment demonstrating the importance of scaling data to train high-quality crash detection models, and we show that scale is especially important when these models need to be deployed into a real-world environment.

</details>


### [289] [Knowledge Distillation for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06078)
*Elia Peruzzo,Aritra Bhowmik,Guillaume Sautiere,Yuki M Asano,Amirhossein Habibian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper explores using knowledge distillation (KD) for compressing autoregressive (AR) image generation models by proposing a novel framework, VarKD, that enhances performance and outperforms existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational challenges of AR image generation models and examine the underexplored area of knowledge distillation in visual AR model compression.

Method: The authors systematically analyze distillation strategies and propose VarKD, a framework that uses student samples for distillation, selectively applies teacher supervision, and mitigates token-level ambiguity in the visual domain.

Result: VarKD achieves superior performance over prior baselines in compressing multiple AR image generation backbones on ImageNet, effectively bridging the gap to large-scale models.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that VarKD is an effective distillation strategy for AR image models, overcoming limitations of traditional methods and language model adaptations while improving computational efficiency.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) image generation models are highly expressive but computationally intensive, motivating effective model compression. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a natural approach for model compression and has been widely studied in language modeling, yet its behavior in visual AR generation remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of distillation strategies for AR image models. Our analysis shows that while standard distillation can yield meaningful gains, recent methods developed for language do not directly transfer to images: long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity make teacher supervision unreliable especially under student-conditioned contexts. To address this, we propose VarKD, a distillation framework for visual autoregressive models that distills on student samples while selectively applying teacher supervision and reducing token-level ambiguity. Experiments on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones show that VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.

</details>


### [290] [HyperVis: Continuous Latent Visual Relational Graphs on the Lorentz Hyperboloid for Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06100)
*Moshiur Farazi,Sameera Ramasinghe,Mahbub Ahmed Turza,Shafin Rahman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies the limitations of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in compositional reasoning and introduces HyperVis, a novel hyperbolic space-based method for relational encoding, which achieves superior results in both training and inference tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face challenges in compositional reasoning due to their inability to effectively model inter-object relationships. This paper seeks to address this gap and avoid performance degradation caused by injecting explicit scene graph triplets.

Method: HyperVis is proposed, which avoids traditional Scene Graph Generation (SGG). It computes dense visual relations using cross-attention, projects these into a Lorentz hyperboloid, and applies spatial physics principles (like entailment cones and angle repulsion) to enforce hierarchy and relationships during model training and inference.

Result: HyperVis significantly improves generative and discriminative tasks for VLMs. It boosts GQA accuracy beyond baseline models during training and achieves +6.25 percentage points improvement on the SugarCrepe benchmark during inference. A hyperbolic space with high curvature is specifically effective for compositional tasks, surpassing the Euclidean counterpart.

Conclusion: The introduction of hyperbolic geometry in HyperVis provides a powerful framework for addressing compositional reasoning challenges in VLMs. The results highlight the necessity of strongly curved spaces for handling continuous visual features and inter-object relationships effectively.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with compositional reasoning that requires understanding inter-object relationships. A natural remedy is to inject explicit scene graph triplets $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ from an off-the-shelf scene graph generator (SGG), but we show this backfires: discrete text labels collide with the continuous visual modality, degrading GQA accuracy from 60.38\% to 58.86\%. We propose \textbf{HyperVis}, which bypasses the SGG semantic bottleneck entirely. From $N$ class-agnostic region proposals, we compute a dense $O(N^2)$ visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention, project it onto a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforce hierarchy through spatial physics, namely IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. We discover that HyperVis contributes in two complementary ways: (1) as a \emph{training-time regularizer}, the hyperbolic relational losses shape LoRA representations that improve generative VQA (GQA 61.03\% vs.\ 57.21\% for LoRA fine-tuning without relational losses, recovering and surpassing the baseline); and (2) as an \emph{inference-time relational encoder}, hyperbolic prefix tokens boost discriminative compositional scoring (SugarCrepe 79.94\%, $+$6.25pp over baseline). The learned curvature stabilises at $κ{=}4.0$, an order of magnitude above prior hyperbolic VLMs where $κ$ typically collapses toward zero, indicating that continuous visual features genuinely require the exponential volume of strongly curved space. A controlled Euclidean ablation confirms this decomposition: the relational pipeline regularises LoRA comparably in flat space (GQA 60.81\%), but the compositionality gain is specifically hyperbolic (SugarCrepe $+$4.58pp over Euclidean), with entailment loss ${\sim}6{\times}$ higher in Euclidean training. Codes are available at TBA.

</details>


### [291] [MS-DKC: A Dataset Knowledge Card Framework for Designing and Adapting Medical Image Segmentation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06103)
*Tariq M. Khan,Syed Saud Naqvi,Thantrira Porntaveetus,Hamid Alinejad-Rokny,Shahzaib Iqbal,Imran Razzak,Mohammad AU Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces MS-DKC, a framework for identifying dataset-specific requirements, and demonstrates its utility on three medical datasets, concluding that segmentation design should be guided by dataset-specific factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To shift focus from architecture-centric evaluations in medical image segmentation to a dataset-oriented approach, addressing factors such as morphology, annotation quality, and deployment risks.

Method: The paper introduces the Medical Segmentation Dataset Knowledge Card (MS-DKC), which uses descriptors tied to dataset-specific factors to guide segmentation design better.

Result: MS-DKC was tested on three datasets (DRIVE, ISIC2018, ACDC) with tailored designs producing improved and dataset-specific results, emphasizing the need for customized model evaluation.

Conclusion: Segmentation models should be evaluated and designed based on the specific requirements and attributes of the dataset, rather than solely on architectural strength.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is often framed as a search for stronger architectures, but this can obscure a more fundamental question: what does the dataset require from the model? In medical imaging, this requirement is shaped by foreground occupancy, morphology, boundary ambiguity, topology sensitivity, annotation quality, acquisition variation, and operating point.
  This paper introduces the Medical Segmentation Dataset Knowledge Card (MS-DKC), a framework for making these factors explicit. MS-DKC records dataset evidence through image/acquisition, morphology, supervision, context-dependence, and deployment-risk descriptors. These descriptors are mapped to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria, making segmentation design more traceable than architecture-first comparison.
  We evaluate MS-DKC on DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC, representing distinct regimes. DRIVE contains sparse, thin, branching vessels, favoring detail-preserving models, sensitivity-aware optimization, threshold analysis, and topology-aware metrics. DKC-TNet-v2 achieved Dice 0.8044 and IoU 0.6730 with 35103 parameters, while SA-UNetv2-DKC-AmbRef reached Dice 0.8141, IoU 0.6865, sensitivity 0.8265, specificity 0.9804, and AUC 0.9853. ISIC2018 involves compact but appearance-variable lesions; validation-constrained score-function selection on Att-Next-Topo/ATTNext produced MS-DKC-AttNextTopo-VCSF-NoAug with Dice 0.8872, IoU 0.8214, precision 0.9173, Boundary F1 0.4878, and ASSD 4.13, while plausible additions failed to improve the risk-aligned profile. ACDC provides a multi-class cardiac case, where MS-DKC recommends four-class softmax segmentation, class-balanced Dice/CE supervision, and class-wise surface evaluation. Overall, the results support dataset-conditioned design: different datasets require different priors, operating points, and evidence before a model can be judged appropriate.

</details>


### [292] [Where, What, Why, and Importance: Structured Defect Grounding for Text-to-Image Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06113)
*Huaisong Zhang,Hao Yu,Yuxuan Zhang,Jiahe Wang,Xinrui Chen,Haoxiang Cao,Feng Lu,Wendong Zhang,Changqian Yu,Chun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Structured Defect Grounding (SDG), a novel framework for diagnosing failures in text-to-image models, including a dataset (SDG-30K) and evaluation protocol (SDG-Eval), showing superior performance in identifying and improving localized defects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image models often exhibit subtle and localized failures, and current methods struggle to accurately diagnose these defects in a structured and semantic manner. A better diagnosis framework is needed to address these shortcomings and improve model performance.

Method: The authors propose SDG, which represents defects as structured tuples containing location, type, reason, and importance. They also design SDG-30K, a new dataset with annotations for defect grounding, and present a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as an SDG detector. They further introduce BoxFlow-GRPO to use defect information for image refinement.

Result: The proposed SDG detector outperforms existing vision-language models in defect diagnosis, while SDG-guided rewards improve text-to-image model alignment and enable localized image enhancement.

Conclusion: SDG provides a unified and effective framework for diagnosing and enhancing the performance of text-to-image models, making it a valuable tool for improving image generation quality.

Abstract: Despite generating increasingly photorealistic images, text-to-image (T2I) models still exhibit localized, subtle, and structurally complex failures. Diagnosing these failures requires instance-level feedback that answers where a defect occurs, what type it is, why it is defective, and its importance to overall image quality. While recent dense-feedback methods move beyond scalar supervision, their heatmap-centric representations still formulate diagnosis as pixel-field regression, making it difficult to localize variable-cardinality defects and bind semantic reasons to individual failures. To address this representation bottleneck, we propose Structured Defect Grounding (SDG), which casts T2I diagnosis as structured set prediction by modeling each defect as a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. To make this formulation trainable and measurable, we introduce SDG-30K, a 30K-image dataset with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators, together with a dedicated evaluation protocol, SDG-Eval. Building on this structured representation, we further present a diagnosis-to-alignment framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as the SDG detector, and BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment. Extensive experiments show that our SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding, while SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement. These results establish SDG as a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

</details>


### [293] [Diff-CA: Separating Common and Salient Factors with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06120)
*Michaël Soumm,Alexandre Fournier Montgieux,Yunlong He,Pietro Gori,Alasdair Newson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel framework using diffusion models for contrastive analysis to separate common and unique data factors without compromising on image generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing contrastive analysis methods suffer from limitations in image quality and effective latent factor separation when applied to high-fidelity image generation and editing.

Method: The authors propose a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model that decomposes conditioning into common and salient factors using weak supervision. They also demonstrate the identifiability of additive contrastive factorization under mild conditions.
