TLA-Prover reaches 9/30 (i.e. pass@1 = 30%) at both Gold and Diamond on a held-out 30-problem benchmark. This is roughly 3.5x the 8.6% untuned baseline. The DPO variant reaches 20% at Diamond. Gold and Diamond coincide at every checkpoint; this prevents the trivial-property failure mode.

</details>


### [525] [Towards the Readability of LLM-Generated Codes through Multitask Representation Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06214)
*Huifan Gao,Liuhua He,Yinghui Pan,Shenbao Yu,Yifeng Zeng,Shengchao Qin,Weidi Sun*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates the use of representation engineering (RepE) to enhance code readability while maintaining correctness, introducing a multitask RepE framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored issue of improving code readability in Large Language Model (LLM)-generated code, which is essential for ease of comprehension but challenging due to its subjective nature.

Method: Developed a multitask Representation Engineering (RepE) framework, theorizing its effectiveness in balancing readability and correctness, and supported the framework with extensive experiments.

Result: Demonstrates the feasibility of employing multitask RepE for controlling code readability across multiple tasks with low data and computational demands.

Conclusion: Multitask RepE effectively achieves a balance between improving code readability and maintaining correctness, with open-source implementations provided.

Abstract: Correctness and readability are key measures of code quality, respectively ensuring functional fidelity and ease of comprehension. While most existing research focuses on improving the correctness of large language models~(LLMs) generated codes, readability remains under-addressed. Enhancing readability through targeted control is challenging due to its subjective nature. In this article, we employ representation engineering~(RepE) as the targeted control method given its characteristics of low data dependency and low computational cost. Prior work on RepE has primarily focused on the targeted control for a single task, but improving the code readability requires the control across multiple tasks. Accordingly we proposes the multitask RepE framework and theoretically discuss the impact of the multitask steering method on the tradeoff between the code readability and correctness. We further provide comprehensive experiments in support. All the relevant implementations are open-source and available upon request.

</details>


### [526] [Domain Diversity, Motivation, Inclusion, and Feedback in Software Modelling Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06275)
*Isabella Graßl,Christopher Lazik,Shalini Chakraborty,Grischa Liebel,Miguel Goulão*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper examines how the selection of problem domains impacts student motivation and inclusivity in software modeling education, revealing that socially relevant and selectable domains engage students the most.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of fostering motivation and inclusivity in software modeling education by investigating the influence of problem domains and teaching methods.

Method: Conducted parallel surveys with 90 students and 22 educators to explore perspectives on problem domains, teaching methods, and feedback in modeling education.

Result: Found a gap between educator assumptions and student preferences, with students favoring socially relevant problems and perceiving valuable feedback when acted upon.

Conclusion: Inclusive, student-centred domain selection is crucial for enhancing motivation and inclusivity in modeling education.

Abstract: Student engagement is critical for effective learning in software modelling, yet fostering motivation and inclusivity remains a challenge. While existing research has focused on modelling tools, notations, and assessment, little attention has been given to how the choice of problem domains and the diversity, relatability, and cultural perspectives they bring shape students' learning experiences. This study explores how problem domains and teaching methods influence motivation, engagement, inclusiveness, and feedback in modelling education. To investigate these dimensions, we conducted parallel surveys with 90 students and 22 educators. Our findings reveal disconnects between educator assumptions and student preferences: Students show greatest motivation for socially relevant domains and prefer choice in selection, while educators overestimate interest in study-related domains. The study identifies how minor design choices can exclude students. Students perceive feedback as meaningful when visibly acted upon. These findings suggest inclusive domain selection is central to student motivation; thus, we recommend student-centred domain selection.

</details>


### [527] [More than a Judge: An Empirical Study of Agent-Human Interaction in Crowdsourced Testing Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06301)
*Yue Wang,Yuan Zhao,Shengcheng Yu,Zhenyu Chen,Qing Gu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper explores integrating agentic AI-based feedback into crowdsourced testing workflows to improve report quality and transfer better practices, demonstrating positive results in report revisions and subsequent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of the heavy review burden for developers caused by the large volume and uneven quality of crowdsourced testing reports and to investigate whether AI-generated feedback can enhance human performance within workflows.

Method: The authors conducted a controlled four-stage human-subject study involving 20 testers working on three real-world applications. Feedback derived from a multi-agent assessment backbone was provided to participants to examine its effect on report revision, task performance, and transferability across applications.

Result: The study found that AI-generated feedback led to immediate improvements in report revisions, enhanced performance on subsequent tasks, and partially transferable better practices to new applications. Participants generally found the feedback understandable and actionable but suggested improvements in specificity and execution.

Conclusion: Assessment agents can act as both judges and workflow-integrated feedback providers, supporting report-quality improvements and better practices in crowdsourced testing. However, there is room to refine feedback specificity and minimize friction in execution.

Abstract: Agentic AI is increasingly being integrated into software engineering workflows. In crowdsourced testing, however, the large volume and uneven quality of submitted reports still create a substantial review burden for developers. In prior work, we developed and validated a multi-agent assessment backbone based on the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm. That backbone assesses reports along three dimensions--textuality, adequacy, and competitiveness--and was shown to align well with human consensus while substantially reducing assessment effort. Yet reliable automated judging does not by itself show whether agent outputs can improve human work when embedded into workflow. This paper studies that missing question in the context of crowdsourced testing. We investigate whether assessment-derived, actionable feedback can improve how testers revise reports, perform on later tasks, and transfer reporting practices across applications. To do so, we conducted a controlled four-stage human-subject study with 20 testers across three real-world applications. The results show that agent-generated feedback supports immediate improvements in revised reports, better first submissions on a new task after prior feedback exposure, and evidence of partial but meaningful transfer to a later application. A post-task questionnaire completed by 17 participants complements these artifact-based findings by suggesting that the feedback was generally understandable, acted upon in revision, and carried into later tasks, while also revealing remaining friction in specificity and execution. Overall, the study provides empirical evidence that, in the studied crowdsourced testing setting, assessment agents can serve not only as post-hoc judges but also as workflow-integrated feedback providers that support upstream report-quality improvement.

</details>


### [528] [From Failed Trajectories to Reliable LLM Agents: Diagnosing and Repairing Harness Flaws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06324)
*Mengzhuo Chen,Junjie Wang,Zhe Liu,Yawen Wang,Qing Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: HarnessFix is a framework designed to diagnose and fix problems in the execution environments (harnesses) of LLM-based agents. It works by analyzing their execution traces and producing targeted repairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents face difficulties due to unreliable behavior stemming from issues in their execution environments (harnesses). Current methods for improving these systems struggle to identify the exact causes of failures, leading to inefficient or broad fixes.

Method: HarnessFix introduces a trace-guided framework that compiles execution traces and harness code into a normalized representation (HTIR). This representation identifies the errors and their origins in the harness layers, consolidates the errors into flaw records, and applies scoped repair operators to fix the identified problems.

Result: HarnessFix showed significant improvements—between 15.2% and 50.0%—in test performance across multiple benchmarks, outperforming both human-designed approaches and current self-evolution methods.

Conclusion: HarnessFix offers a precise and systematic solution to improving LLM-based agent harnesses, resolving recurring issues efficiently and establishing a framework that outperforms traditional methods.

Abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly rely on harnesses that provide execution environments, tool interfaces, context, lifecycle orchestration, observability, verification, and governance. Existing self-improving agents and automatic harness evolution methods mainly improve agents through runtime supervision, prompt optimization, workflow search, or harness modification based on final outcomes. However, they often fail to diagnose where the responsible evidence lies in failed trajectories and which harness layer causes the unreliable behavior, resulting in broad, indirect, or poorly scoped changes. This paper proposes HarnessFix, a trace-guided framework for diagnosing agent failures and repairing agent harnesses. HarnessFix compiles raw execution traces and harness code into a Harness-aware Trace Intermediate Representation (HTIR), which normalizes fragmented trajectory evidence and captures step-level provenance and control-flow relations. It then attributes failures to responsible trajectory steps and harness layers, consolidates recurring diagnoses into actionable flaw records, and maps them to scoped repair operators. Finally, HarnessFix generates and validates harness patches under flaw-specific repair specifications to reduce target flaws without introducing unacceptable regressions. We evaluate HarnessFix on SWE-Bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0 Verified, GAIA and AppWorld. Across these benchmarks, HarnessFix improves held-out test performance over the initial harnesses by 15.2%--50.0%, outperforms human-designed and self-evolution baselines, and reveals recurring harness-flaw patterns across ETCLOVG layers.

</details>


### [529] [Scaffold, Not Vocabulary? A Controlled, Two-Tier, Pre-Registered Study of a Popperian Code-Generation Skill](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06454)
*Mehmet Iscan*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The study evaluates the efficacy of a 'Popperian falsificationist' prompt structure in improving code generation in large language models (LLMs) and finds that performance gains are primarily due to scaffold structure rather than Popperian procedural content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to evaluate whether the reported gains from using a Popperian falsificationist prompt skill in LLMs are due to its specific scientific reasoning approach or merely the structural influence of the scaffold.

Method: The authors pre-registered a two-tier ablation experiment with three control conditions: a length-matched placebo, a labels-only scaffold (headers but no procedures), and an execution oracle (unit tests). These were tested on both a frontier model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) and a smaller model (Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B) with additional measures such as self-judge audits and vocabulary checks.

Result: In the frontier model, gains were limited by benchmark ceilings, showing no performance boost from the Popperian content. In the smaller model, structured scaffolds improved correctness significantly, but the improvement was attributed to the structure rather than the Popperian procedure itself. The self-judge mechanism failed to outperform random selection, suggesting systemic limitations.

Conclusion: The experimental data suggest that the procedural content of the Popperian falsificationist skill does not add a separable benefit to execution correctness. The observed gains are primarily attributable to the structural aspects of the scaffold, not its Popperian methodology.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly write, review, and judge code, and a fast-growing practice equips them with prompt 'skills' that ask the model to reason like a scientist. A prominent example tells the model to act as a Popperian falsificationist, and such skills are reported to improve generated code. But these gains are almost always read off an LLM-as-a-judge, an instrument with documented positional, self-preference, and stylistic biases. We ask: if it appears to help, is the gain from the skill's Popperian content, or from the structure any scaffold imposes? We pre-register a two-tier ablation with three controls: a length-matched placebo, a labels-only scaffold that keeps the Popperian headers but strips the procedure, and an execution oracle (HumanEval+ unit tests), plus a vocabulary-halo sentinel and a same-model self-judge audit. On a frontier model (Claude Sonnet 4.6, N=163) all conditions sit near the benchmark ceiling and do not separate, so the pre-registered +5-point improvement is not supported (a ceiling-limited non-detection). On a small model (Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B, N=164) structured arms lift best-of-eight correctness by 20-22 points, but the full skill shows no separable benefit over a labels-only scaffold (aggregate F@8=L@8 vs V@8=34.8%), and the placebo trails by only 2.4 points. A 0.5B self-judge applying the Popperian rubric does not beat random selection and concentrates 60% of its picks on one index. In the two settings tested, the skill's Popperian procedural content adds no separable execution-correctness benefit beyond a labels-only scaffold, so the gains track scaffold structure. We contribute a calibrated negative result and a reusable disambiguation protocol; the finding bounds an engineering claim about one prompt-skill family and is not an evaluation of Popperian methodology in general.

</details>


### [530] [Code2LoRA: Hypernetwork-Generated Adapters for Code Language Models under Software Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06492)
*Liliana Hotsko,Yinxi Li,Yuntian Deng,Pengyu Nie*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces Code2LoRA, a framework that generates repository-specific LoRA adapters for code language models, avoiding inference-time overhead and adapting to static or evolving codebases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency and brittleness of existing methods that inject repository context into code language models through either long inputs or per-repository fine-tuning, especially in evolving codebases.

Method: Code2LoRA uses a hypernetwork to create repository-specific LoRA adapters without adding token overhead. It offers two modes: Code2LoRA-Static for stable codebases and Code2LoRA-Evo for evolving ones, updating with each code diff.

Result: On a newly created benchmark (RepoPeftBench), Code2LoRA shows state-of-the-art performance, matching baselines for static codebases and outperforming them in evolving scenarios by +5.2 percentage points in cross-repo tasks.

Conclusion: Code2LoRA effectively captures repository-specific context in a parameter-efficient manner, addressing the needs of stable and dynamic codebases while performing competitively with or better than existing methods.

Abstract: Code language models need repository-level context to resolve imports, APIs, and project conventions. Existing methods inject this knowledge as long inputs (retrieved through RAG or dependency analysis) or through per-repository fine-tuning and LoRA -- costly at repository scale and brittle to evolving codebases. We introduce Code2LoRA, a hypernetwork framework that generates repository-specific LoRA adapters, effectively injecting repository knowledge with zero inference-time token overhead. Code2LoRA supports two usage scenarios: Code2LoRA-Static converts a single repository snapshot into an adapter, suitable for comprehension of stable codebases; while Code2LoRA-Evo maintains an adapter backed by a GRU hidden state updated per code diff, suitable for active development of evolving codebases. To evaluate Code2LoRA against parameter-efficient fine-tuning baselines, we build RepoPeftBench, a benchmark of 604 Python repositories with two tracks: a static track with 40K training and 12K test assertion-completion tasks, and an evolution track with 215K commit-derived training and 87K commit-derived test tasks. On the static track, Code2LoRA-Static achieves 63.8% cross-repo and 66.2% in-repo exact match, matching the per-repository LoRA upper bound; on the evolution track, Code2LoRA-Evo achieves 60.3% cross-repo exact match (+5.2 pp over a single shared LoRA). Code2LoRA's code can be found at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/code2lora-6857; the model checkpoints and RepoPeftBench datasets can be found at https://huggingface.co/code2lora.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [531] [Cross-scale spatially-aware generative modeling of transcriptomic programs underlying neurodegenerative brain organization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05870)
*Krishnakumar Vaithianathan*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a generative framework to model the biological processes underlying neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease, achieving strong predictive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the lack of comprehensive understanding of the spatial selectivity of brain regions' vulnerability to neurodegeneration in disorders like Alzheimer’s disease by moving beyond traditional correlation-based analyses.

Method: The authors developed a spatially-aware generative framework based on regional gene-expression profiles from the Allen Human Brain Atlas and neurodegenerative vulnerability maps from ADNI FreeSurfer cortical thickness measurements. A variational generative architecture with graph-based spatial smoothness regularization was employed.

Result: The framework achieved a strong prediction of regional neurodegenerative vulnerability, explaining 86.04% of the variance and showing significant spatial correlation (r = 0.9439) between predicted and observed degeneration.

Conclusion: The work highlights the utility of generative modeling in linking molecular and macroscopic frameworks of neurodegeneration, paving the way for advanced computational tools in neuroscience.

Abstract: Neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer's disease exhibit highly organized patterns of regional brain vulnerability, yet the biological mechanisms underlying this spatial selectivity remain incompletely understood. Existing imaging-transcriptomic studies have largely relied on correlation-based analyses between gene expression and neuroimaging phenotypes, limiting their ability to model how molecular organization gives rise to neurodegeneration. Here, we introduce a cross-scale spatially-aware generative framework for modeling transcriptomic programs underlying cortical neurodegeneration. Regional transcriptomic profiles were derived from the Allen Human Brain Atlas using 910 landmark genes across 68 cortical regions. Neurodegenerative vulnerability maps were constructed from ADNI FreeSurfer cortical thickness measurements by computing regional cortical thinning differences between cognitively normal controls (NC = 926) and Alzheimer's disease subjects (AD = 426). A variational generative architecture was used to learn latent biological programs linking regional gene-expression organization to cortical degeneration while incorporating graph-based spatial smoothness regularization to preserve cortical organization. The proposed framework achieved strong prediction of regional neurodegenerative vulnerability, yielding an explained variance of 0.8604 and a significant spatial correlation between predicted and observed cortical degeneration profiles (r = 0.9439, p < 0.001). The learned latent representations revealed structured transcriptomic organization associated with distributed disease susceptibility. These findings demonstrate that biologically constrained generative modeling can bridge microscale molecular organization with macroscale neurodegeneration, providing a foundation for spatially-aware generative neurobiology and computational neuroscience.

</details>


### [532] [Early psychosis shows deviations in scaling behaviour within a critical regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06290)
*Irem Topal,Paola Moreno Ancalmo,Guillermo Montana Valverde,Philipp Homan,Wolfram Hinzen*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study examines early psychosis and healthy controls using fMRI data to discover that while critical-like brain dynamics are preserved, early psychosis involves reorganized scaling properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether altered scaling measures in psychiatric disorders reflect a common alteration in large-scale brain dynamics under the framework of criticality.

Method: Resting-state fMRI data from early psychosis patients and healthy controls were analyzed using phenomenological renormalization group (PRG), power spectral density (PSD), and detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA).

Result: Healthy controls showed critical-like scale-invariant dynamics, whereas early psychosis participants maintained scale invariance but exhibited systematic shifts in scaling exponents.

Conclusion: Early psychosis involves not a loss but a reorganization of critical-like brain dynamics, offering insights into the broader implications for psychiatric disorder research.

Abstract: Accumulating evidence suggests that large-scale brain activity exhibits scale-invariant dynamics consistent with operation in a near-critical regime. Such dynamics have been associated with long-range correlations, efficient information processing, and the emergence of collective organization. While altered criticality-related measures have been reported in psychiatric disorders, previous findings remain fragmented across observables and modalities, making it unclear whether different scaling measures capture a common alteration of large-scale brain dynamics. Here, we investigated scaling properties in resting-state fMRI data from individuals with early psychosis and healthy controls. We combined a phenomenological renormalization group (PRG) framework with power spectral density (PSD) and detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA) to characterize collective dynamics across scales. In healthy controls, resting-state activity exhibited non-trivial scaling behavior consistent with critical-like organization. Early psychosis participants showed the same overall phenomenology of scale-invariant organization, but with systematic shifts in scaling exponents across multiple observables. These findings indicate that early psychosis is not characterized by a simple loss of critical-like dynamics, but rather by a reorganization of collective dynamics within a preserved scaling regime. More broadly, our results suggest that combining coarse-graining approaches with temporal scaling analyses provides a principled framework for studying large-scale brain dynamics in psychiatric disorders.

</details>


### [533] [Intrinsic Computational Functionalism: From Observer-Relative Maps to Observer-Independent Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06424)
*Shuqin Ma,Ryota Kanai*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper proposes intrinsic computational functionalism to explain consciousness through observer-independent computational structures within systems themselves, operationalized via system-intrinsic instantiation and causal-dynamical organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the observer-relativity issue in computational theories of consciousness, proposing a way to identify computational structures that are intrinsic and do not rely on external labeling.

Method: The authors establish two criteria: system-intrinsic instantiation, which requires observer-independent properties, and causal-dynamical organization, which focuses on internal mutual constraints and responses under intervention.

Result: The paper provides a three-tier framework for identifying consciousness-related computational properties, emphasizing dynamics-internal grain selection while rejecting external labeling approaches.

Conclusion: Intrinsic computational functionalism offers a robust observer-independent computational account of consciousness, reconciling it with rigorous empirical and theoretical constraints.

Abstract: Anti-computational arguments show that externally imposed computational interpretations cannot ground consciousness, but they do not establish that all computational organisations are observer-relative. We develop intrinsic computational functionalism: the view that, if consciousness is computationally constituted, it depends on physically realised computational structures the system has in virtue of itself rather than on labels imposed by an external interpreter. Two criteria operationalise this view. (C1) System-intrinsic instantiation: the relevant property must be specifiable without an observer's labelling, and invariant under structure-preserving relabellings of the system's variables. (C2) Causal-dynamical organisation under intervention: the property must be grounded in a state-space structure whose variables mutually constrain one another, and whose organisation is exhibited in counterfactual response under intervention. Together these criteria specify what any candidate computational account must satisfy to remain observer-independent, without selecting which intrinsic structures bear on experience. The argumentative core is a three-tier decomposition of identification work: interpreter-relative label selection (tier i), theoretically constrained partition selection (tier ii), and dynamics-internal grain selection (tier iii). We argue that any computational property capable of avoiding the observer-relativity objection must be identified, if at all, through tier (iii) dynamics-internal grain selection, conditional on empirically disciplined tier (ii) choices. Syntax-is-not-semantics arguments, mapmaker arguments, and the observer-relativity component of biological-naturalist objections succeed against views that locate the consciousness-relevant property at tier (i); once the tiers are distinguished, intrinsic computational functionalism survives.

</details>


### [534] [Ontology-constrained multi-LLM scoring of hypothesis support in the predictive processing literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05206)
*Hamed Nejat,Alexander Maier,Jesse Spencer-Smith,André M. Bastos*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study addresses fragmentation in interdisciplinary fields, particularly in predictive coding neuroscience, and proposes a method using multiple local language models (LLMs) to synthesize literature by scoring studies against an expert-defined glossary of concepts and hypotheses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the need to address the synthesis problem in interdisciplinary fields like predictive coding neuroscience, where traditional meta-analytic methods fall short in handling diverse methods, data, and theoretical commitments.

Method: A local multi-LLM pipeline was developed. It processes papers by extracting evidence, incorporating figure descriptions, assembling constrained prompts, and validating results against an expert-defined glossary of 36 predictive-coding concepts grouped into three hypotheses. A council of 10 LLMs scored 31 studies, enabling analytical visualizations like pairwise agreement and three-dimensional hypothesis mapping.

Result: The study found high agreement among hypotheses in some cases but structured disagreements in others, especially in local vs. global experimental contexts. A new metric, hypothesis-space temperature, revealed greater dispersion in global oddball contexts compared to local ones.

Conclusion: This framework demonstrates the utility of local multi-LLM councils for synthesizing heterogeneous literatures by producing auditable, measurable disagreement metrics. It can be extended to other fields where conventional meta-analysis fails to provide a unified comparison space.

Abstract: Fragmentation is common in interdisciplinary fields with diverse methods and theoretical commitments. Predictive coding neuroscience is a clear example: its literature spans computational theory, electrophysiology, imaging, behavior, and modeling, creating a synthesis problem that conventional meta-analysis cannot easily resolve. Here, we describe a local multi-LLM pipeline for ontology-constrained literature synthesis. The pipeline reads papers, extracts evidence, incorporates figure descriptions, assembles constrained prompts, and validates outputs against an expert glossary. We manually defined a predictive-coding glossary of thirty-six concepts grouped into three hypotheses: predictive suppression, feedforward error propagation, and ubiquity. A council of ten local language models scored 31 studies according to their agreement or disagreement with each glossary factor across local and global oddball contexts. This enabled pairwise study-agreement analysis, cross-model comparison, and three-dimensional hypothesis-space mapping. Agreement was high for some hypotheses but weaker for others, revealing structured disagreement, particularly across local versus global oddball paradigms. We further define hypothesis-space temperature, a geometric dispersion metric measuring how compactly studies occupy the hypothesis space. Temperature was lower for local oddball contexts and higher for global oddball contexts, indicating greater dispersion in the latter. The scoring geometry also allowed us to estimate vectors of change between experimental contexts. These results demonstrate that local multi-LLM councils can produce auditable disagreement measurements that map heterogeneous literatures into quantitative evidence spaces. This framework may generalize to cross-study hypothesis mapping where conventional meta-analysis lacks a common comparison space.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [535] [HyFAD: Hybrid Time-Frequency Diffusion with Frequency-Aware Embedding for Time Series Imputation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05239)
*Hongfan Gao,Wangmeng Shen,Bin Yang,Jilin Hu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces HyFAD, a hybrid time-frequency diffusion model designed for time series imputation by addressing the limitations of existing methods regarding frequency-sensitive denoising, reconstruction, and trend-dynamics balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models in time series struggle with frequency-sensitive denoising, high-frequency reconstruction, and balancing between global and local dynamics. The paper seeks to overcome these limitations.

Method: The authors propose HyFAD, a hybrid model using a coupled time-frequency diffusion framework under the DDPM paradigm. Time-domain diffusion handles global trends, and frequency-domain diffusion refines high-frequency details. A frequency-aware embedding helps guide spectral reconstruction based on diffusion steps.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that HyFAD achieves state-of-the-art results across various benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: HyFAD provides an effective and innovative approach to time series imputation with enhanced performance, thanks to its hybrid time-frequency framework and frequency-aware embedding.

Abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated strong performance in time series modeling due to their ability to progressively capture complex data distributions through iterative denoising. However, existing approaches struggle with frequency-sensitive denoising, high-frequency reconstruction and balancing global trends with local dynamics. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{HyFAD}, a \textbf{Hy}brid time-frequency \textbf{D}iffusion model with \textbf{F}requency-\textbf{A}ware embedding for time series imputation. Built upon the DDPM paradigm, HyFAD adopts a coupled time-frequency diffusion framework, in which the reverse denoising proceeds sequentially from the time domain to the frequency domain, enabling coarse-to-fine generation. Specifically, the time-domain diffusion process captures low-frequency global trends, while the frequency-domain diffusion process refines high-frequency spectral components. We further introduce a frequency-aware step embedding that exploits the relationship between diffusion steps and spectral components, providing step-dependent spectral guidance and facilitates more accurate band-wise reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that HyFAD achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our source code is available at https://github.com/hongfangao/HyFAD.

</details>


### [536] [Deterministic Envelopes for Tamed SGLD: Decoupling Stochastic-Gradient Noise and Localizing Taming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05242)
*Yiwei Zhou,Ziheng Chen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to address stationary bias in stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms caused by gradient-dependent taming denominators, introducing a structure-preserving alternative that avoids this bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Highlighting the problem of stationary bias introduced by gradient-dependent tamed denominators in stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms and seeking a more reliable stabilization approach.

Method: A new framework uses deterministic-envelope designs for taming denominators, fixing them independently of oracle noise and introducing hybrid methods with both soft and hard-tail control for effective stabilization.

Result: The theoretical framework breaks down the stationary error sources, develops a hybrid mechanism to address bias and stabilization, and is validated by experiments demonstrating its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The proposed deterministic-envelope and hybrid approaches reduce bias while preserving stabilization, offering a significant improvement over gradient-dependent taming denominators.

Abstract: Stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms often use tamed denominators to stabilize non-globally Lipschitz drifts. This paper shows that when the denominator depends on the same stochastic-gradient realization as the numerator, the taming step changes the stochastic oracle itself and can create a stationary bias even if the original stochastic gradient is unbiased. We propose a structure-preserving framework for designing tamed denominators. It fixes the denominator before the oracle noise is sampled and uses localized deterministic envelopes to avoid unnecessary taming in typical regions. These kernels keep the stabilizing effect of taming while avoiding the bias introduced by a gradient-dependent denominator. Our theory explains how the stationary error splits into the bias caused by oracle-dependent taming and the remaining error introduced by deterministic stabilization. Within this deterministic-envelope family, the analysis identifies a far-tail condition that explains the limitation of local soft envelopes and motivates a hybrid member: soft in the typical region, but protected by hard-tail control on rare excursions. Experiments confirm the predicted stationary distortions of random denominators, the bias reduction of deterministic-envelope designs, and the stabilizing effect of the hybrid construction.

</details>


### [537] [Harnessing Source Heterogeneity for Cluster-Structured Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05258)
*Xiaohui Yin,Jun Jin,Shane J. Sacco,Robert H. Aseltine,Kun Chen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Trans-GLMC, a method to address source heterogeneity in transfer learning by clustering related sources, tested on hospital data to predict suicide risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to handle source heterogeneity in transfer learning, particularly when auxiliary sources vary in their usefulness, as demonstrated in a suicide-risk study using hospital data.

Method: Trans-GLMC clusters sources based on coefficient similarity, applies global and within-cluster adjustments, and includes a target-specific debiasing step to enhance predictions.

Result: The method improves facility-specific prediction accuracy, identifies clusters of hospitals with mutual transferability, and highlights interpretable factors associated with suicide risk.

Conclusion: Trans-GLMC effectively addresses source heterogeneity, delivering improved prediction performance and interpretable clustering structure in domains with sparse and clustered data.

Abstract: Transfer learning is a natural strategy when a target population has limited data but multiple related auxiliary sources are available. A central difficulty is source heterogeneity: auxiliary sources may not be equally useful, and their usefulness may vary in a structured, cluster-like fashion. Existing transfer-learning methods often reduce source selection to a binary informative/non-informative decision, overlooking subgroups of sources with differential transferability. Motivated by a suicide-risk study using data from the Connecticut Hospital Information Management Exchange (CHIME), comprising 636,758 patients across 27 hospitals, we propose Trans-GLMC, a cluster-structured transfer-learning procedure for generalized linear models. The CHIME setting illustrates the core challenge: hospital-specific risk models are unstable because suicide attempts are rare at any single facility, whereas indiscriminate pooling across hospitals can obscure facility-level differences in patient mix and risk profiles. Trans-GLMC first constructs a coefficient-based distance among the target and candidate sources to recover latent source clusters. It then combines global fusion, within-cluster refinement, and target debiasing to produce an estimator that adapts to the detected structure. We establish a non-asymptotic error bound that improves over its unclustered counterpart whenever a meaningful target cluster exists and matches the unclustered rate up to constants otherwise. In simulations and in the CHIME study, Trans-GLMC improves facility-specific prediction, identifies interpretable communities of hospitals with mutual transferability, and recovers clinically coherent suicide-risk factors.

</details>


### [538] [TabSODA: Tabular Diffusion based Imputation with Skip Pattern Detection and Ordinal Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05361)
*Yuyu Chen,Taehyo Kim,Hai Shu,Yang Feng*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces TabSODA, a novel method for handling missing data in large-scale surveys, addressing challenges such as structural skips and ordinal data representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to improve missing data imputation methods in large-scale surveys to handle structural skips and ordinal data, which are inadequately managed by current tabular diffusion methods.

Method: The authors propose TabSODA, an EM-based diffusion imputer utilizing the EDM framework, designed to propagate structural skips and incorporate ordinal-aware variable representation. They also introduce a CART-based skip-pattern miner for cases without a predefined skip mask.

Result: TabSODA outperforms baselines by reducing ordinal MACE by up to 23.7% and improving categorical accuracy by up to 9% on PATH and NSDUH datasets. The skip-pattern miner achieves near-perfect precision.

Conclusion: The proposed TabSODA method significantly enhances missing data imputation for large-scale surveys, effectively addressing structural skips and ordinal data challenges, and demonstrates robustness in both codebook-mask and skip-pattern mining scenarios.

Abstract: Missing data imputation in large-scale surveys faces two challenges that are not well handled by current tabular diffusion methods. First, \emph{structural skips}, cells made inapplicable by questionnaire design, should not be imputed but are often conflated with item nonresponse. Second, \emph{ordinal} responses encode ordered categories, yet most pipelines treat them as nominal levels through one-hot or analog-bit encodings. We introduce \textbf{TabSODA} (\textbf{Tab}ular diffusion with \textbf{S}kip pattern detection and \textbf{O}r\textbf{d}inal \textbf{A}wareness), an Expectation-Maximization (EM)-based diffusion imputer built on the Elucidated Diffusion Model (EDM) framework. TabSODA propagates structural skips through the denoising loss and reverse-time sampler, and represents ordinal variables with cumulative-probit scalar latents while retaining analog-bit encodings for nominal variables. When a codebook skip mask is available, TabSODA uses it directly; otherwise, the TabSODA+SKIP variant estimates the mask from raw responses and questionnaire order using a CART-based skip-pattern miner. On Population Assessment of Tobacco and Health (PATH) study and the National Survey on Drug Use and Health (NSDUH), two nationally representative U.S.\ surveys, TabSODA reduces ordinal MACE by up to $23.7\%$ and improves categorical accuracy by up to $9\%$ over the strongest baseline across MCAR, MAR, and MNAR masking. The skip miner achieves near-perfect precision on both datasets, allowing TabSODA+SKIP to closely track the codebook-mask variant.

</details>


### [539] [Environment-Robust Representation Learning with Empirical Bayes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05365)
*Yuli Slavutsky,Matthew Shen,Bohan Wu,David M. Blei*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents a Bayesian approach for addressing multi-environment prediction problems with stable conditional mechanisms, providing better predictions in unseen environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to build a robust prediction model for scenarios where latent variable distributions differ across environments, but the relationships between variables remain stable.

Method: The authors propose a Bayesian model with a variational objective that includes a decomposed per-environment term and a cross-environment balancing term. An empirical Bayes approach is used for setting the prior, and an amortized variational algorithm is developed for posterior approximation.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates superior prediction performance in simulations and real-world tasks, such as astronomical source identification, microbiome-based disease detection, and ICU sepsis prediction.

Conclusion: The study concludes that leveraging stable conditional mechanisms across different environments allows for robust generalization and better performance in unseen scenarios.

Abstract: We consider multi-environment prediction problems. We assume the environments change the distribution of a latent variable, while the mechanisms generating observed covariates and targets remain stable conditional on that variable. For example, hospitals or clinical cohorts may differ in the prevalence of latent patient states, even though the relationships between those states, physiological measurements, and outcomes remain unchanged. Given a dataset from multiple environments, we formulate a Bayesian model for such problems and derive the corresponding variational objective. We show that this objective decomposes into per-environment terms and an additional cross-environment balancing term induced by the model's structure. We use an empirical Bayes method to set the prior and incorporate it into the objective. Based on this objective, we develop an amortized variational algorithm for posterior approximation, and use the resulting learned latent variables to form predictions in new environments.We study our approach through simulations and real-world studies of astronomical source identification, microbiome-based disease detection, and ICU sepsis prediction. Across these settings, our method outperforms previous approaches for prediction in new environments.

</details>


### [540] [Sparse Functional Singular Value Decomposition for Biclustering and Triclustering Longitudinal Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05488)
*Yue Zhao,Thierry Chekouo,Sandra Safo*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Tri-SfSVD, a sparse functional Singular Value Decomposition framework to identify biclusters and triclusters in complex longitudinal omics data and applies it successfully to Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) and EEG datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of analyzing high-dimensional, sparsely sampled, and irregularly observed longitudinal omics data in conditions such as IBD, where conventional clustering and functional data analysis methods fall short.

Method: The authors propose Tri-SfSVD, a unified framework that combines continuous trajectory estimation with sparse penalties across subjects, variables, and temporal subregions to identify biclusters and triclusters directly from observed data without ad hoc imputation.

Result: Extensive simulations show that Tri-SfSVD outperforms existing methods, and its application to IBD data identified biclusters linking clinical characteristics with microbial pathways, while its use on EEG data revealed triclusters connecting phenotypes with brain activity patterns.

Conclusion: Tri-SfSVD effectively uncovers localized structures in high-dimensional longitudinal data, providing biologically and clinically interpretable insights for disease heterogeneity and phenotypic characterization.

Abstract: Identifying subtypes of complex conditions, such as Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD), often requires capturing latent patterns in longitudinal omics data. However, these data are typically high-dimensional, sparsely sampled, and irregularly observed over time, posing substantial challenges for conventional (bi)clustering and functional data analysis methods. We propose Tri-SfSVD, a unified sparse functional Singular Value Decomposition framework for discovering biclusters and triclusters in longitudinal data. Unlike existing functional biclustering methods that rely on ad hoc imputation or enforce restrictive shape-homogeneity assumptions, Tri-SfSVD integrates continuous trajectory estimation with simultaneous subject, feature, and temporal selection within a single optimization framework. By imposing sparse penalties across subjects, variables, and temporal subregions, the proposed method works directly on observed data to uncover localized structures at the subject, subject-feature, and subject-feature-time levels. Extensive simulations demonstrate that Tri-SfSVD outperforms existing approaches in high-dimensional settings. Applied to IBD multi-omics data, the method identified three biclusters linking sample clusters with distinct IBD-related clinical characteristics to microbial pathway groups associated with specific bacterial taxa, providing interpretable subject-pathway associations for characterizing disease heterogeneity. Applied to multi-channel EEG data, the method identified three triclusters linking sample clusters with distinct alcohol-related phenotypes to localized brain activity patterns, including subgroup differences separated by temporal subregions within the same spatial region.

</details>


### [541] [Conformal Risk-Averse Decision Making with Action Conditional Guarantee](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05551)
*Zihan Zhu,Shayan Kiyani,George Pappas. Hamed Hassani*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces an advanced method called action-conditional conformal prediction for improved uncertainty quantification and safer decision-making with machine learning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to ensure safety guarantees in decision-making pipelines that rely on machine learning predictions, especially in risk-averse scenarios, as current methods provide only marginal safety assurances.

Method: The paper introduces action-conditional conformal prediction to provide safety guarantees specific to decisions made, and proposes a finite-sample algorithm leveraging pinball-loss minimization for optimizing action-conditional value-at-risk.

Result: Experiments demonstrated that the proposed method yields significant performance improvements in action-conditional contexts compared to existing conformal prediction baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances the ability to make risk-averse decisions with explicit action-specific safety guarantees, advancing the utility of ML models in critical applications.

Abstract: Reliable decision making pipelines powered by machine learning models require uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods that come with explicit safety guarantees. Conformal prediction provides such UQ by wrapping ML predictions into prediction sets, and recent work by Kiyani et al. (2025b) established that these sets can be translated into optimal risk-averse decision policies -- yet only inheriting marginal safety guarantees. We generalize and strengthen their results by (i) introducing action-conditional conformal prediction, which yields safety guarantees conditioned explicitly on each action taken by the decision maker, (ii) showing that action-conditional prediction sets serve as a proxy for the feasible decision space for risk-averse decision makers aiming to optimize action-conditional value-at-risk, and (iii) proposing a principled finite-sample algorithm based on pinball-loss minimization, connecting the framework of Gibbs et al. (2025) to action-conditional guarantees. Experiments on two real-world datasets confirm that our approach significantly improves action-conditional performance over conformal baselines.

</details>


### [542] [Finding Most Influential Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05919)
*Lucas D. Konrad,Nikolas Kuschnig*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces an efficient method to identify the most influential size-$k$ subsets (MIS) in data by transforming the problem into a simpler sequence of top-k problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge lies in the computational infeasibility of identifying the most influential subsets due to the combinatorial explosion of possibilities as the data size increases.

Method: The authors propose using Dinkelbach's method to iteratively address the problem, which ensures a computational cost of $\mathcal{O}(n)$ per iteration and guarantees finite termination.

Result: Simulations and applications demonstrate the practical ability of the method to recover MIS that were unreachable using prior computational techniques.

Conclusion: This novel method addresses the computational challenge of MIS selection effectively and is verified by simulations and applications, expanding the accessibility of MIS analysis in practice.

Abstract: Identifying most influential sets (MIS) - size-$k$ subsets whose removal maximally changes a target estimand - is typically infeasible because it requires searching over $\binom{n}{k}$ subsets. For estimands with linear-fractional leave-set-out effects, we show that MIS selection reduces to a one-parameter sequence of top-$k$ problems. Dinkelbach's method yields an algorithm with $\mathcal{O}(n)$ cost per iteration and finite termination. For fixed residualized inputs, the algorithm returns a globally optimal set for the univariate ratio objective, including the oracle-residualized partial linear model. With estimated nuisance functions, uniform denominator and generated-score stability imply approximation to the first-order oracle orthogonal-score objective; exact set recovery follows under a separation condition. Simulations and applications show that the method recovers exact MIS that were previously computationally inaccessible.

</details>


### [543] [EML-CD: Causal Mechanism Recovery via EML Symbolic Trees in Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05942)
*Sota Asanuma*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces EML-CD, a framework integrating the EML operator into causal discovery to recover interpretable causal mechanisms and closed-form equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery methods using neural networks yield black-box mechanisms, which are difficult to interpret. The authors aim to recover not only causal structures but also interpretable causal mechanisms, overcoming the challenges highlighted in prior work.

Method: Proposed EML-CD, which encodes causal mechanisms in a gated EML binary tree, allowing the representation of closed-form equations. The framework computes analytical Jacobians for understanding causal effects and evaluates the approach on real and synthetic data benchmarks.

Result: EML-CD achieves promising results in structure discovery and mechanism recovery, performing on par with leading methods (e.g., PC, GES) in structure metrics and producing more interpretable mechanisms. On synthetic benchmarks, it demonstrates reduced mechanism errors but structure recovery is comparable to existing methods.

Conclusion: The EML-CD framework effectively combines causal structure learning with interpretabable and closed-form mechanism recovery, enhancing interpretability and analytical insights in causal modeling.

Abstract: Neural network (NN)-based nonlinear causal discovery methods recover DAG structure but leave each causal mechanism as a black box. Waxman et al. argued that extracting causal mechanisms from NN weights is ill-posed. We propose EML-CD, a framework that integrates the EML operator (capable of composing elementary functions from a single binary operator) into causal structure learning, with interpretable mechanism recovery as the primary objective. EML-CD represents each edge mechanism as a gated EML binary tree and automatically discovers closed-form causal equations. Analytical Jacobians can be directly computed from the output equations, enabling quantitative understanding of causal effects. On real data (Sachs protein signaling, d=11), EML-CD achieves SHD=11.2 +/- 0.4 (5-seed mean; baselines are single deterministic runs), on par with PC/GES within seed variance and below CAM, while attaching closed-form equations to each detected edge (precision 0.756, recall 0.365). In a controlled bivariate test with known mechanisms, EML-CD recovers 10 of 11 elementary function families faithfully (held-out shape correlation >= 0.96; only high-frequency sine is partial). On a symbolic synthetic benchmark, EML-CD attains a substantially lower and more stable held-out mechanism f-MSE than a fixed SINDy dictionary (mean 3.67 vs. 7644, the latter inflated by catastrophic extrapolation on one seed), although its structure recovery (SHD 14.0) only matches the dictionary and stays below specialized optimizers; on the Causal Chambers light-tunnel subset, a depth-2 model improves F1 over linear OLS-BIC (0.444 vs. 0.273).

</details>


### [544] [Fast and Robust Convergence Rate for TD(0) with Linear Function Approximation, Universal Learning Steps and I.I.D. Samples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05967)
*Ziad Kobeissi,Éloïse Berthier*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper establishes new convergence properties for the TD(0) method with linear function approximation, achieving a fast and robust rate of 1/k for the mean-square error and introducing a variant, PCTD(0), for better performance under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the understanding and efficiency of the TD(0) method with linear function approximation, especially around its finite-time behavior and its robustness to ill-conditioning.

Method: The study examines TD(0) with constant step size, i.i.d samples, Polyak-Juditsky averaging, and derives a 1/k convergence rate for the mean-square error, independent of ill-conditioning. It also proposes PCTD(0) to enhance convergence under strong mixing assumptions.

Result: The analysis reveals a convergence rate of 1/k for TD(0) that is independent of the smallest eigenvalue of the uncentered covariance matrix. PCTD(0) improves convergence further under strong mixing conditions for the Markov Chain.

Conclusion: The findings improve both the theoretical understanding and practical performance of TD(0), addressing prior limitations related to ill-conditioning and introducing PCTD(0) as a more efficient variant.

Abstract: In this paper, we study the finite-time behavior of the TD(0) temporal-difference method with linear function approximation (LFA). We consider on-policy independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) samples, a constant learning step, and the Polyak-Juditsky averaging method. We establish a new convergence rate, for the Mean-Square Error (MSE) on the approximated function, that is (i) fast in the sense that it admits an optimal dependency in the number of iterations k (i.e., of order 1/k), (ii) robust to ill-conditioning: it only depends on an initial error and modelindependent constants and (iii) sharp up to a multiplicative constant lower than 11. In particular, it does not depend on the smallest eigenvalue of the uncentered covariance matrix of the linear parametrization, unlike all pre-existing O(1/k) rates in the TD(0) literature. We also introduce PCTD(0), a variant of TD(0), which benefits from better convergence properties under an additional assumption of strong mixing on the Markov Chain.

</details>


### [545] [Adaptive Learning Rates with Surrogate Probability for Follow-the-Perturbed-Leader](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06043)
*Jongyeong Lee,Junya Honda,Shinji Ito,Chansoo Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel adaptive learning rate for the follow-the-perturbed-leader (FTPL) framework, addressing challenges in achieving best-of-both-worlds (BOBW) guarantees while maintaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FTPL is computationally efficient but struggles with probability-dependent adaptive learning rates, limiting its BOBW guarantees. Improving this can enhance its applicability in bandit problems.

Method: The paper proposes surrogate probability functions to define adaptive learning rates for FTPL, allowing BOBW guarantees while maintaining computational simplicity.

Result: The approach generalizes BOBW guarantees for FTPL with Pareto perturbations ($α>1$) and works effectively in the bandit problem with expert advice, extending prior results.

Conclusion: The method makes FTPL adaptive and computationally simple, with further potential applications in other algorithmic frameworks besides learning rate designs.

Abstract: Follow-the-regularized-leader framework has shown effectiveness and flexibility in online learning problems, where the choice of learning rates are known to be crucial. Recently, adaptive learning rates defined in terms of the arm-selection probabilities, obtained by solving convex optimization, have achieved improved best-of-both-worlds (BOBW) guarantees in various bandit problems. In contrast, BOBW guarantees for its computationally efficient alternative, follow-the-perturbed-leader (FTPL), remain relatively limited since its optimization-free nature ironically makes the design of adaptive, probability-dependent learning rates non-trivial. To address this challenge, we propose an adaptive learning rate for FTPL by introducing surrogate probability functions that can be computed only from the available quantities, without requiring the exact probabilities. Based on these learning rates with surrogate functions, we provide the BOBW guarantee for FTPL with Pareto perturbations for any shape parameter $α>1$, generalizing prior results restricted to specific choices of $α=2$. We further show the BOBW guarantees for FTPL with adaptive learning rates in the bandit problem with expert advices. Our approach preserves the computational simplicity of FTPL while enabling probability-dependent adaptivity, and the surrogate-based methodology may be of independent interest in other algorithmic frameworks beyond FTPL and learning rate designs.

</details>


### [546] [Effective Dimensionality as an Operator Invariant for Physics-Preserving Constraint Adaptation in Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06171)
*Cornelius Otchere,Michael Shields*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper studies the intrinsic issue of task interference in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) using the Fisher Information Matrix and introduces a novel diagnostic metric ($d_{eff}$) along with subspace projection techniques for faster adaptation to boundary changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address task interference in PINNs, which arises from the requirement to satisfy both governing differential equations and boundary conditions using a shared parameter space. This has a fundamental impact on the model's performance.

Method: The method involves analyzing task interference using the concept of effective degrees of freedom ($d_{eff}$) based on the Fisher Information Matrix, and further introducing subspace projection strategies to adapt boundary conditions efficiently without disrupting pre-trained physics.

Result: It is shown that $d_{eff}$ provides insights into the structural constraints of differential operators, effectively measuring how free network parameters are constrained. This metric is validated on linear and nonlinear operators, showcasing its reliability. Additionally, subspace projection strategies achieve accurate boundary adaptation in seconds to minutes, outperforming or matching traditional gradient-based fine-tuning in terms of efficiency.

Conclusion: This work provides a new structural perspective on PINNs by introducing $d_{eff}$ as a diagnostic tool and demonstrates the efficacy of subspace projections for rapid boundary adaptation, thereby improving the practical usability of PINNs.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks inherently suffer from task interference because they rely on a shared parameter space to satisfy both governing differential equations and boundary conditions. We analyze this structural conflict using the Fisher Information Matrix to quantify the effective degrees of freedom ($d_{eff}$) in a physics-constrained model. Unlike the classical $d_{eff}$ which measures how many parameter directions are informed by data against a statistical prior, our $d_{eff}$ measures the dimension of the parameter directions unconstrained by the differential operator. For operators with finite-dimensional kernel, we show that $d_{eff}$ converges to the kernel dimension exactly, independent of network width, depth, or activation function, recasting it from a fit diagnostic into a structural invariant of the underlying continuous operator. For operators with infinite-dimensional kernel, $d_{eff}$ instead measures the network's finite-dimensional representational bandwidth for that kernel rather than recovering an integer invariant. Importantly, $d_{eff}$ also serves as an a priori structural diagnostic. Driving $d_{eff}$ of a well-posed problem to zero certifies that the physics and boundary constraints have absorbed the network's free directions. Building on this characterization, we introduce subspace projection strategies for boundary adaptation. Rather than retraining from scratch, we project parameter updates into the null space of the pre-trained physics operator so that new boundary conditions are satisfied without disturbing the learned physics. Gradient-based fine-tuning can match or exceed this but needs more wall-clock time and tuning, whereas subspace projection delivers near-equivalent quality in seconds to minutes. We validate on linear and nonlinear operators, demonstrating accurate adaptation to initial and boundary shifts and unencountered constraint types.

</details>


### [547] [Diffusion Models Observe Only Gradients: A Geometric Perspective on Score Matching Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06179)
*Naïl B. Khelifa,Richard E. Turner,Ramji Venkataramanan*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that the $L^2$ score matching error is not an intrinsic measure of the quality of marginal distributions in score-based diffusion models. It introduces a decomposition of score errors, leading to a deeper understanding of model performance metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fundamental issues in evaluating the quality of score-based diffusion models, the paper investigates whether the $L^2$ score matching error truly reflects marginal distributional quality.

Method: Using Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition, the authors analyze score errors into gradient and solenoidal components. They provide theoretical proofs and bounds, including an impossibility result, a refined KL divergence bound, and a new estimator for evaluating model performance.

Result: The authors show that the solenoidal component is irrelevant for marginal dynamics, and propose a method to compute a gradient-based metric that correlates better with distributional quality. This leads to tight theoretical bounds and better empirical alignment with sample quality.

Conclusion: Existing reliance on $L^2$ score error to evaluate diffusion models is flawed. Focusing on the gradient component offers a more accurate and empirically validated measure of model performance. This insight advances understanding and evaluation metrics for diffusion models.

Abstract: Score-based diffusion models are typically trained by minimizing the $L^2$ score matching error, and standard theoretical analyses rely on this quantity to bound the sampling discrepancy between the learned and target distributions. We show the $L^2$ score error is not the right intrinsic measure of marginal distributional quality: a learned diffusion model can incur arbitrarily large $L^2$ score error while perfectly matching the target distribution. By decomposing score errors into a gradient and a solenoidal component (a Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition), we identify the geometric reason behind this: only the gradient component enters the marginal Fokker-Planck dynamics, while the solenoidal component is structurally invisible. We make this precise in three results. First, building on the corrected geometry, we prove an impossibility result: no monotone function of the $L^2$ score error can uniformly lower bound any divergence between the learned and target distributions. Second, we derive an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler divergence that depends only on the observable gradient component of the error, tightening the standard Girsanov bound and identifying its looseness as the cost of operating on path-space rather than marginal-space dynamics. Third, we give a tractable estimator of the gradient component via a dual Sobolev identity, which is shown to empirically correlate substantially better with sample quality than the full $L^2$ error.

</details>


### [548] [Anchor PCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06233)
*Benedikt Seiter,Anya Fries,Julius von Kügelgen,Jonas Peters*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Anchor PCA, a new method for robust unsupervised dimension reduction for multi-domain data, yielding embeddings that are invariant and perform better in unseen domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the limitations of traditional PCA in multi-domain data, specifically its inability to focus on shared directions of variation rather than spurious ones.

Method: A modified PCA approach targeting a shared embedding by trading off explained variance and agreement between shared and domain-specific embeddings, solved efficiently using a modified target matrix.

Result: Anchor PCA demonstrates robust performance recovering invariant subspaces and explaining more variance on unseen domains compared to pooling and worst-case baseline methods.

Conclusion: Anchor PCA is a useful tool for robust unsupervised dimension reduction, especially for multi-domain data with complex variation patterns.

Abstract: Principal component analysis (PCA) is one of the most widely used unsupervised dimension reduction techniques. We study PCA for data from multiple related domains. Since principal components generally differ across domains, one way to obtain a shared low-rank embedding is to perform PCA on the pooled data. However, this approach can focus on spurious directions that exhibit high variation in only a few domains. To find a robust embedding that still explains most variance in unseen but similar domains, we propose instead to focus on shared directions of variation. To this end, we introduce Anchor PCA which trades off overall explained variance with agreement between the shared and domain-specific low-rank embeddings. Anchor PCA amounts to PCA on a modified target matrix and thus can be solved efficiently. Moreover, we show that Anchor PCA recovers a maximal invariant subspace and admits a minimax reconstruction interpretation under bounded domain-specific covariance inflations. On simulated and real-world gas sensor data with temporal drift, we demonstrate, respectively, that Anchor PCA recovers the maximally invariant subspace and yields embeddings that explain more variance on unseen domains than the pooling baseline and a worst-case alternative. Taken together, these findings establish Anchor PCA as a promising approach to robust unsupervised dimension reduction from multi-domain data.

</details>


### [549] [Discrete Causal Representations from Heterogeneous Domains: A Bayesian Approach with Social Survey Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06288)
*Ankur Garg,Michael Stettler,Aaron Schein,Julius von Kügelgen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Bayesian method for learning high-level causal representations from multi-environment data using sequential Monte Carlo sampling. It showcases applications in understanding cultural values and political opinions through social survey data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work addresses the need for practical and uncertainty-aware methods for causal representation learning, particularly in dealing with heterogeneous data from multiple environments or domains.

Method: The authors propose a Bayesian hierarchical model incorporating priors based on causal assumptions, and use sequential Monte Carlo sampling for inference. They focus on discrete causal concepts and unknown soft interventions.

Result: The method effectively identifies high-level latent causal concepts and causal relations in multi-environment data, with the approach validated through case studies on social survey data.

Conclusion: The proposed Bayesian approach successfully learns causal representations of complex real-world data, providing interpretable insights into latent causal concepts and their relations in heterogeneous data scenarios.

Abstract: Causal representation learning aims to infer the high-level latent causal concepts that give rise to observed low-level measurements. This is particularly relevant for heterogeneous data from different environments or domains since distribution shifts often arise through sparse, localized changes in some of the underlying causal mechanisms, while other parts of the generative process remain unchanged. Whereas identifiability of causal representations has been studied extensively, practical uncertainty-aware methods and real-world use cases remain less explored. In this work, we propose a Bayesian approach to learning causal representations from multi-environment data, focusing on the case of discrete causal concepts and unknown multi-node soft interventions. To this end, we translate causal assumptions and interpretability desiderata into suitable priors and parametric choices within a hierarchical model. We then devise an inference scheme based on sequential Monte Carlo sampling to approximate the resulting multimodal posterior. We showcase our approach through case studies on social survey data, where latent causal concepts correspond to cultural values or political opinions, measurements to survey responses, and environments to different countries or states. Our model infers meaningful high-level concepts and plausible causal relations among them, demonstrating its utility for learning causal representations of complex real-world data.

</details>


### [550] [Symmetric Divergence and Normalized Similarity: A Unified Topological Framework for Representation Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06342)
*Yan Wang,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified topological toolkit for comparing neural representations, addressing limitations in previous methods by providing symmetric, bounded, and standardized metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing paired topological divergences are asymmetric and yield sample-size dependent, unbounded scores, making reliable benchmarking difficult.

Method: The authors propose two main tools: Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) and Normalized Topological Similarity (NTS). SRTD resolves asymmetry and consolidates diagnostics into a single signature, while NTS provides a bounded, scale-invariant similarity metric.

Result: The proposed toolkit successfully captures functional shifts in CNNs and maps LLM genealogy, outperforming geometric measures and overcoming limitations like distance saturation.

Conclusion: This framework provides a rigorous, topology-focused alternative for robust neural representation analysis and cross-scenario benchmarking, complementing existing geometric methods.

Abstract: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) offers a principled, intrinsic lens for comparing neural representations. However, existing paired topological divergences (e.g., RTD) are limited by heuristic asymmetry and, more critically, unbounded scores that depend on sample size, hindering reliable cross-scenario benchmarking. To address these challenges, we develop a unified topological toolkit serving two complementary needs: fine-grained structural diagnosis and robust, standardized evaluation. First, we complete the RTD framework by introducing Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) and its efficient variant SRTD-lite. Beyond resolving the theoretical asymmetry of prior variants, SRTD consolidates diagnostic information into a single, comprehensive cross-barcode signature. This allows for precise localization of structural discrepancies and serves as an effective optimization objective without the overhead of dual directional computations. Second, to enable reliable benchmarking across heterogeneous settings, we propose Normalized Topological Similarity (NTS). By measuring the rank correlation of hierarchical merge orders, NTS yields a scale-invariant metric bounded between -1 and 1, effectively overcoming the scale and sample-dependence of unnormalized divergences. Experiments across synthetic and real-world deep learning settings demonstrate that our toolkit captures functional shifts in CNNs missed by geometric measures and robustly maps LLM genealogy even under distance saturation, offering a rigorous, topology-aware perspective that complements measures like CKA.

</details>


### [551] [Function-Space Priors for Bayesian Neural ODEs with Application to Vessel Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06351)
*Jaeyeong Lee,Wonmo Koo,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method that combines GP-kernel-based regularization and probabilistic multiple shooting to improve vessel trajectory prediction from AIS data while quantifying uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vessel trajectory prediction is challenging due to irregular data sampling, missing reports, and complex dynamics. There is a need for uncertainty quantification to enable reliable maritime decision-making.

Method: The authors propose augmenting Bayesian Neural ODEs by imposing GP-kernel-based priors on vector fields at finite measurement points. They also incorporate probabilistic multiple shooting to handle long, irregular AIS trajectories.

Result: The approach provides a principled framework for trajectory modeling, achieves uncertainty quantification, and is computationally feasible compared to direct GP priors on ODE solutions.

Conclusion: The method addresses key challenges in maritime trajectory prediction and offers a practical solution to encode structural properties while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Vessel trajectory prediction from Automatic Identification System (AIS) data is essential for maritime situational awareness, yet it remains challenging due to irregular sampling, missing reports, and complex dynamics. Beyond accurate point forecasts, maritime applications also demand well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for reliable decision-making. Bayesian Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) offer a principled framework for continuous-time trajectory modeling with uncertainty quantification by placing a prior over the neural vector field parameters. However, the commonly used isotropic Gaussian weight prior fails to encode informative structural properties of vessel dynamics, such as smoothness and locality. Existing function-space Bayesian neural network methods address this limitation for static mappings, but do not transfer directly to Neural ODEs, where the primary quantity of interest is the trajectory rather than the vector field itself. In principle, one could place a Gaussian process (GP) prior directly over ODE solutions, but this requires propagating distributions through a nonlinear ODE solver, which is analytically intractable. To address this challenge, we adopt a practical approach that imposes a GP-kernel-based prior directly on the vector field evaluated at a finite set of measurement points. Specifically, we augment the standard weight-space variational objective with a kernel-based regularizer that penalizes deviations of the vector field from the structure implied by a GP prior.
